> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types - Variable Reluctance, Permanent Magnet and Hybrid Motors - Constructional Features - Principle of Operation - Modes of Excitation. Torque Production in Variable Reluctance Stepping Motor: Static Torque Characteristics - Position Error due to Low Torque

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Stepper Motor (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology)

A **Stepper Motor** (also called a **Stepping Motor** or **Step Motor**) is a *brushless, synchronous electromagnetic actuator* that converts a train of digital electrical pulses (DC square-wave pulses applied in a specific sequence to its stator windings) into discrete, incremental mechanical angular displacements (steps) of its rotor shaft. Each input pulse causes the rotor to advance by a precisely fixed angular increment known as the **Step Angle (α)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):** A stepper motor is an electromechanical transducer that operates on the principle of converting electrical pulses into discrete mechanical movements, where the magnitude of the rotation is proportional to the number of input pulses, and the speed is proportional to the frequency of those pulses.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Digital Ratchet of Motion"

Imagine a **clock's second hand**. It does not sweep smoothly like a fan blade. Instead, it *jerks* forward in tiny, identical clicks — one click per second. A stepper motor behaves exactly like that clock hand, but in a controllable, programmable way.

- The **electrical pulses** are analogous to the "tick" commands.
- The **step angle** is the size of each "tick" jump.
- The **pulse frequency** is how fast the ticks arrive.
- A **microcontroller** (like the Arduino in your lab) acts as the "clock master" that decides when to tick.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU love this topic?** Because stepper motors are the *backbone of open-loop position control* in 3D printers, CNC machines, robotic arms, disk drives, and biomedical equipment — all industries Kerala engineers frequently work in. The 2024 Scheme tests both the *physical concept* and the *analytical torque equations*.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Variables)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|--------------|---------|
| Step Angle | $\alpha$ | degrees ($\degree$) or rad | Angular advance per pulse |
| Holding Torque | $T_h$ | N·m | Static torque at energized phase |
| Detent Torque | $T_d$ | N·m | Residual torque with phases off |
| Pull-out Torque | $T_{po}$ | N·m | Max running torque |
| Step Frequency | $f$ | pulses/sec (pps) | Pulse rate |
| Number of Phases | $m$ | — | Independent stator windings |
| Number of Rotor Teeth | $N_r$ | — | Pole count on rotor |
| Number of Stator Poles | $N_s$ | — | Pole count on stator |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Stepper Motors (KTU Module 1 Core)

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes stepper motors into three principal types. Each type has a fundamentally different rotor construction and operating principle.

#### 2.1.1 Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM)

**Constructional Features:**
- **Stator:** Made of laminated silicon steel. Carries concentrated field windings on salient poles. Typically 3 or 4 phases wound on a 6-pole or 8-pole stator.
- **Rotor:** A *plain salient-pole* (toothed) soft-iron rotor **without any permanent magnet or field winding**. The rotor is built from high-permeability steel laminations.
- **Bearings:** Precision ball bearings to minimize friction and hold tight air-gap tolerance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In a VRSM, *no rotor excitation exists*. Torque is produced purely by the *tendency of the magnetic circuit to minimize its reluctance* (i.e., to align the rotor teeth with the energized stator poles along the path of minimum magnetic reluctance).

**Principle of Operation:**
- When a stator phase is energized, a magnetic field is established.
- The rotor (being ferromagnetic) experiences a force that pulls the nearest toothed pole toward alignment with the energized stator pole to **minimize the air-gap reluctance**.
- When phase A is switched off and phase B is energized, the rotor rotates by one *step* to align with phase B's pole axis.
- Continuous sequential switching produces continuous rotation.

#### 2.1.2 Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor (PMSM)

**Constructional Features:**
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon steel with concentrated windings (typically 2 phases on 4/6/8 poles).
- **Rotor:** A **cylindrical permanent magnet** (axially or radially magnetized), often with 2 or 4 pole pairs. Modern PMSMs use rare-earth magnets (Nd-Fe-B or Sm-Co).

**Principle of Operation:**
- The rotor's permanent magnet creates a constant bias flux.
- When stator windings are energized, the armature flux either **aids** or **opposes** the permanent magnet flux.
- Torque is produced by the *interaction* between the stator field and the rotor's permanent magnet field, following the principle:
$$\vec{T} = \vec{M} \times \vec{B}$$
where $\vec{M}$ is the rotor's magnetic moment and $\vec{B}$ is the stator's field.

#### 2.1.3 Hybrid Stepper Motor (HSM) — Most Industrially Important

**Constructional Features:**
- Combines features of *both* VRSM (toothed rotor) and PMSM (axially magnetized rotor magnet).
- **Rotor:** Two identical toothed cups mounted on a common shaft, separated by an axially magnetized cylindrical permanent magnet. The teeth on one cup are *offset* by half a tooth-pitch from those on the other cup.
- **Stator:** Toothed/slotted structure (typically 8 poles, 2 phases) with concentrated windings.

**Principle of Operation:**
- The axially magnetized PM polarizes the two end cups with **opposite polarities** (one North-seeking, one South-seeking).
- The stator field interacts with both cups simultaneously.
- *Effective* pole pairs double, giving **small step angles (typically $0.9\degree$ to $1.8\degree$)** with high torque density.

### 2.2 Modes of Excitation (Sequential Switching Schemes)

The KTU syllabus specifically tests the *modes of excitation* — the *sequencing logic* of pulses applied to the stator phases.

| Mode | Phases ON Simultaneously | Step Magnitude | Torque | Power |
|------|--------------------------|----------------|--------|-------|
| **Wave (One-Phase-On)** | 1 | $1\alpha$ | Lowest | Lowest |
| **Full-Step (Two-Phase-On)** | 2 | $1\alpha$ | Highest | Highest |
| **Half-Step (Alternate)** | 1 or 2 alternating | $0.5\alpha$ | Medium | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Wave excitation gives $T_{wave} \approx 0.707 \cdot T_{full}$, while Full-step (two-phase-on) provides *higher holding torque* due to constructive flux addition from two phases.

#### 2.2.1 Wave (One-Phase-On) Excitation Sequence — 4-Phase Example

$$\text{Step 1: } A^+ \rightarrow \text{Step 2: } B^+ \rightarrow \text{Step 3: } C^+ \rightarrow \text{Step 4: } D^+ \rightarrow \text{repeat}$$

Each phase is energized in sequence; only one is ON at any instant.

#### 2.2.2 Full-Step (Two-Phase-On) Excitation Sequence

$$\text{Step 1: } A^+B^+ \rightarrow \text{Step 2: } B^+C^+ \rightarrow \text{Step 3: } C^+D^+ \rightarrow \text{Step 4: } D^+A^+ \rightarrow \text{repeat}$$

Two adjacent phases are simultaneously energized, and the rotor aligns to the *resultant flux axis* (midway between two stator poles).

#### 2.2.3 Half-Step Excitation Sequence

$$\text{Step 1: } A^+ \rightarrow \text{Step 2: } A^+B^+ \rightarrow \text{Step 3: } B^+ \rightarrow \text{Step 4: } B^+C^+ \rightarrow \ldots$$

This doubles the resolution by interleaving single-phase and two-phase steps, halving the effective step angle.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\alpha = \dfrac{360\degree}{N_r \cdot m}$ | Step angle (VR type, full-step) | degrees |
| 2 | $\alpha = \dfrac{360\degree}{2 \cdot m \cdot N_r}$ | Step angle (Hybrid type) | degrees |
| 3 | $f = \dfrac{v}{\alpha}$ | Pulse frequency for shaft speed $v$ | pps |
| 4 | $v_{mech} = \alpha \cdot f$ | Mechanical rotational speed | rad/s |
| 5 | $T = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Instantaneous electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 6 | $T = -\dfrac{\partial W_{co}}{\partial \theta}\big\vert_{i=const}$ | Co-energy-based torque equation | N·m |
| 7 | $T \approx \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Static torque in VRSM | N·m |
| 8 | $T_{static} = T_{max}\sin\left(\dfrac{N_r \cdot \theta}{2}\right)$ | Static torque vs. position (ideal) | N·m |
| 9 | $\theta_{error} = \dfrac{T_L}{k_\theta}$ | Position error under load | rad |
| 10 | $k_\theta = \dfrac{dT}{d\theta}\big\vert_{\theta = 0}$ | Torsional stiffness of detent curve | N·m/rad |

> [!WARNING]
> **Formula Pitfall:** In equation (1) above, $N_r$ refers to the *total number of rotor teeth*, NOT the number of poles. KTU examiners frequently substitute these terms to test your understanding. The same is true for $N_s$ (stator teeth count).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **3D Printers & CNC Machines:** Open-loop position control of extruder heads and tool bits.
- **Robotic Joints:** Precise angular positioning without feedback sensors.
- **Disk Drives:** Head positioning actuator in HDDs.
- **Medical Infusion Pumps:** Reliable, calibrated fluid delivery.
- **Textile & Packaging Industries:** Synchronized cutting/sealing operations.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Step Angle of a Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor

Consider a 3-phase, $N_r$-toothed VR stepper motor operating in full-step (two-phase-on) excitation.

Let:
- $N_r$ = total number of rotor teeth
- $m$ = number of stator phases
- $N_s$ = total number of stator teeth
- $T_s$ = stator tooth pitch (in degrees, mechanical)

**Step 1:** For one full electrical cycle (all phases energized in sequence), the rotor advances by one stator tooth pitch, $T_s$.

**Step 2:** The stator tooth pitch (in mechanical degrees) is given by:

$$T_s = \dfrac{360\degree}{N_s}$$

**Step 3:** The number of discrete steps required to complete one full mechanical revolution is:

$$\text{Total steps per revolution} = \dfrac{360\degree}{T_s} = N_s$$

**Step 4:** However, in a typical VR stepper with $m$ phases energized in sequence, each pulse advances the rotor by $\dfrac{T_s}{m}$ (because $m$ phase transitions cover one full stator tooth pitch).

Therefore, the **step angle per pulse** is:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{T_s}{m} = \dfrac{360\degree}{m \cdot N_s}$$

**Step 5:** For a salient-pole rotor with $N_r$ teeth and a stator with $N_s$ teeth arranged such that $N_s = N_r$ is *not* generally true (they are often designed with a *fractional relationship* for proper stepping). For the most common configuration, the step angle is:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \dfrac{360\degree}{m \cdot N_r}}$$

**Numerical Example:** A 3-phase VRSM has 12 rotor teeth. Then:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{360\degree}{3 \times 12} = \dfrac{360\degree}{36} = 10\degree \text{ per step}$$

If the pulse frequency is 200 pps, the rotor speed is:

$$v = \alpha \cdot f = 10\degree \times 200 = 2000\degree /s = \dfrac{2000}{360} \text{ rev/s} = 5.556 \text{ rps} = 333.33 \text{ rpm}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Static Torque Equation of a VRSM (Energy Method)

This is the **most heavily tested derivation** in KTU Module 1.

**Step 1 — Define System Energy:** When a single phase of inductance $L(\theta)$ carries current $i$, the magnetic co-energy stored in the air gap is:

$$W_{co} = \dfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) \cdot i^2$$

where $L(\theta)$ varies with rotor angular position $\theta$ due to changing air-gap reluctance.

**Step 2 — Apply the Co-energy Theorem:** The electromagnetic torque at constant current is:

$$T = \dfrac{\partial W_{co}}{\partial \theta}\bigg\vert_{i = \text{const}}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate:**

$$T = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left[\dfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) \cdot i^2\right]\bigg\vert_{i = \text{const}}$$

Since $i$ is held constant in the static case, it factors out:

$$T = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \cdot \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Express L(θ) for a Multi-tooth VR Rotor:**

The inductance of a single phase, neglecting saturation, is approximately:

$$L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \cdot \theta)$$

where:
- $L_0$ = average (DC) component of inductance
- $L_1$ = amplitude of variation
- $N_r \cdot \theta$ = electrical angle (in rotor tooth units)

**Step 5 — Differentiate L(θ) with respect to θ:**

$$\dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} = -L_1 \cdot N_r \cdot \sin(N_r \cdot \theta)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into Torque Equation:**

$$T = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \cdot \left[-L_1 \cdot N_r \cdot \sin(N_r \cdot \theta)\right]$$

$$T = -\dfrac{1}{2} i^2 L_1 N_r \sin(N_r \cdot \theta)$$

The negative sign indicates the torque acts to *decrease* the magnetic co-energy (i.e., the rotor moves to maximize $L$). Rewriting in terms of *magnitude* of the *restoring/static* torque:

$$\boxed{T_{static}(\theta) = -\dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \cdot \dfrac{dL}{d\theta} = -T_{max} \sin(N_r \cdot \theta)}$$

where the **maximum static torque** is:

$$T_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 L_1 N_r$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Result:** The static torque in a VRSM is *independent of the direction of current* (it depends on $i^2$). This is fundamentally different from a DC motor, where torque depends on $i$ linearly. This is why a VR stepper can be driven with *unipolar* pulses.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Position Error Due to Low Torque (Load-Induced Angular Lag)

When an external load torque $T_L$ is applied to the rotor, the rotor does not settle at the *exact* commanded position $\theta = 0$. Instead, it lags by an angle $\theta_e$ such that the developed electromagnetic torque *exactly balances* the load.

**Step 1 — Linearization around the equilibrium point:** For small displacements, the static torque curve can be linearized:

$$T_{static}(\theta) \approx -T_{max} \cdot N_r \cdot \theta \quad \text{(for small } \theta \text{ near } 0\text{)}$$

This is because $\sin(N_r \cdot \theta) \approx N_r \cdot \theta$ for small arguments.

**Step 2 — Define Torsional Stiffness:**

$$k_\theta = \dfrac{dT_{static}}{d\theta}\bigg\vert_{\theta = 0} = T_{max} \cdot N_r$$

**Step 3 — Force/Torque Balance at Steady State:**

At the new equilibrium, the developed torque equals the load torque:

$$T_{static}(\theta_e) = -T_L$$

$$-T_{max} \cdot N_r \cdot \theta_e = -T_L$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Position Error:**

$$\boxed{\theta_e = \dfrac{T_L}{T_{max} \cdot N_r} = \dfrac{T_L}{k_\theta}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A *higher* number of rotor teeth $N_r$ or a *higher* maximum torque $T_{max}$ reduces position error. This is why multi-tooth hybrid stepper motors ($N_r = 50$ or more) have *much smaller* position errors than coarse, 4-tooth VRSMs.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mode of Excitation vs. Performance

| Property | Wave (1-$\phi$ on) | Full-Step (2-$\phi$ on) | Half-Step |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| Step Size | $\alpha$ | $\alpha$ | $\alpha/2$ |
| Holding Torque | $T_{max}$ | $\sqrt{2} \cdot T_{max}$ | $T_{max}$ to $\sqrt{2} T_{max}$ |
| Power Consumption | Lowest | Highest | Medium |
| Resolution | Lowest | Lowest | Doubled |
| Resonance Susceptibility | Highest | Medium | Lowest |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Operating Principle of a Stepper Motor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Controller / Microcontroller Unit]
    nodeB[Pulse Generator and Sequencer]
    nodeC[Driver Circuit with Power Transistors]
    nodeD[Stepper Motor Stator Phases]
    nodeE[Toothed or PM Rotor]
    nodeF[Mechanical Load Output Shaft]
    nodeG[Position Feedback Optional Encoder]

    nodeA -->|Sends Step and Direction Commands| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Generates Phase Excitation Sequence| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Amplified Current Pulses| nodeD
    nodeD -->|Establishes Rotating Magnetic Field| nodeE
    nodeE -->|Mechanical Rotation in Discrete Steps| nodeF
    nodeF -.->|Optional Closed Loop Monitoring| nodeG
    nodeG -.->|Position Correction Signal| nodeA
```

### 4.2 Constructional Comparison — Block Schematic of Three Stepper Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VR["Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor VRSM"]
        VR1[Laminated Salient Stator with Phase Windings]
        VR2[Toothed Soft Iron Rotor No Magnet]
        VR1 --> VR2
    end

    subgraph PM["Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor PMSM"]
        PM1[Laminated Stator with Phase Windings]
        PM2[Axially Magnetized PM Rotor]
        PM1 --> PM2
    end

    subgraph HY["Hybrid Stepper Motor HSM"]
        HY1[Toothed Laminated Stator with Phase Windings]
        HY2[Axially Magnetized Permanent Magnet Disc]
        HY3[Front Toothed Cup Polarized North]
        HY4[Rear Toothed Cup Polarized South]
        HY1 --> HY2
        HY2 --> HY3
        HY2 --> HY4
    end
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow — Static Torque vs. Rotor Position Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Rotor at Commanded Position theta equals 0]
    nodeB[Phase Energized Inductance is Maximum]
    nodeC[Co energy is Maximum Torque equals 0 Stable Equilibrium]
    nodeD[Load Torque Applied TL]
    nodeE[Rotor Displaces by theta error]
    nodeF[New Equilibrium: T static theta error equals TL]
    nodeG[Step Settled with Position Error theta error]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Modes of Excitation State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> WaveExcitation
    WaveExcitation --> WaveExcitation: Single phase ON, rotor steps by alpha
    WaveExcitation --> FullStepExcitation: Two adjacent phases ON, rotor steps by alpha, higher torque
    FullStepExcitation --> FullStepExcitation: Continue sequence
    FullStepExcitation --> HalfStepExcitation: Alternate one and two phase ON, step reduces to alpha over 2
    HalfStepExcitation --> HalfStepExcitation: Higher resolution stepping
    HalfStepExcitation --> [*]: System Stop
```

### 4.5 Physical Cross-Section Schematic of a VRSM (4-Phase, 8/6 Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph StatorAssembly["Stator Outer Ring 8 Salient Poles"]
        P1[Pole A]
        P2[Pole A bar]
        P3[Pole B]
        P4[Pole B bar]
        P5[Pole C]
        P6[Pole C bar]
        P7[Pole D]
        P8[Pole D bar]
    end
    subgraph RotorAssembly["Salient Pole Toothed Rotor 4 or More Teeth"]
        T1[Tooth 1]
        T2[Tooth 2]
        T3[Tooth 3]
        T4[Tooth 4]
    end
    StatorAssembly --> RotorAssembly
```
