## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

*List any three advantages and three disadvantages of stepper motors compared to conventional DC servo motors.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. *Open-loop position control:* No feedback encoder is required, simplifying the system and reducing cost.
2. *Excellent low-speed torque:* Stepper motors can deliver full holding torque even at zero speed, unlike servos that lose torque at low RPM.
3. *Digital compatibility:* Directly compatible with digital pulses from microcontrollers, enabling precise pulse-counting-based positioning.

**Disadvantages:**
1. *Resonance and low-efficiency at high speeds:* The discrete-step nature causes torque ripple and resonance issues.
2. *Open-loop position error:* If the load exceeds the pull-out torque, the motor stalls *without any error signal*, leading to silent positioning failure.
3. *Lower power-to-weight ratio:* Heavier and bulkier than equivalent servos for the same output power.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

*Compare the constructional features of a Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM) and a Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor (PMSM).*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | VRSM | PMSM |
|---------|------|------|
| Rotor type | Salient-pole, soft iron, toothed | Cylindrical permanent magnet |
| Rotor excitation | None (passive) | Permanent magnet (active) |
| Detent torque | Negligible (no magnet) | Significant |
| Holding torque source | Air-gap flux alone | Magnet flux + stator flux |
| Step angle | Larger (e.g., 15°, 30°) | Smaller (e.g., 7.5°, 15°) |
| Inertia | Low | Higher due to magnet |

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and principle of operation of a *Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor (VRSM)*. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the *static torque equation* of a VRSM using the co-energy method and explain how position error arises under load. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Operation (7 Marks):**

The VRSM consists of:
- A **stator** with salient poles carrying concentrated phase windings (typically 3 or 4 phases, wound on 6 or 8 stator poles).
- A **rotor** made of soft-iron laminations with multiple salient teeth, *without any permanent magnet or rotor winding*.
- Both stator and rotor are built from laminated electrical steel to minimize eddy-current losses.

**Operating Principle (3 Marks sub-breakup):**
- When a stator phase (say A) is energized, a magnetic field is established between the stator pole and the rotor.
- The rotor, being ferromagnetic, experiences a *reluctance torque* that pulls the rotor tooth nearest to the energized stator pole into alignment, **minimizing the air-gap reluctance** (and hence maximizing inductance).
- When phase A is switched OFF and phase B is switched ON, the rotor rotates by one *step angle* $\alpha$ to align with phase B's axis.
- Sequential switching of phases A → B → C → A → … produces continuous rotation in discrete steps.

> **Diagram (2 Marks):** Cross-sectional view of stator poles (labeled A, B, C, D) and toothed rotor with clear indication of the rotor's alignment toward the energized phase.

**Part (b) — Static Torque Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** The magnetic co-energy stored when a single phase carries current $i$:

$$W_{co} = \dfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) \cdot i^2$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Applying the co-energy theorem at constant current:

$$T = \dfrac{\partial W_{co}}{\partial \theta}\bigg\vert_{i = \text{const}} = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \cdot \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Expressing inductance as a position-dependent sinusoid:

$$L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \cdot \theta)$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Differentiating:

$$\dfrac{dL}{d\theta} = -L_1 N_r \sin(N_r \cdot \theta)$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Substituting back:

$$T_{static} = -\dfrac{1}{2} i^2 L_1 N_r \sin(N_r \cdot \theta) = -T_{max} \sin(N_r \cdot \theta)$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Position Error Explanation:

When load torque $T_L$ acts, the rotor settles at $\theta_e$ such that:

$$\theta_e = \dfrac{T_L}{T_{max} \cdot N_r}$$

This finite angular lag is the *position error due to insufficient torque*, which scales linearly with load.

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Conclusion with units and physical interpretation.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model — Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the three *modes of excitation* of a stepper motor with phase-sequencing diagrams. Compare their step resolution and torque characteristics. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat constructional diagram, explain the working of a *Hybrid Stepper Motor*. Why is it preferred over VRSM and PMSM in industrial applications? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Modes of Excitation (7 Marks):**

**1. Wave (One-Phase-On) Excitation (2 Marks):**
Only one phase is energized at any time. Sequence for a 4-phase motor: $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow A$. The step size is the full $\alpha$ per pulse. Torque is $T_{wave}$.

**2. Full-Step (Two-Phase-On) Excitation (2 Marks):**
Two adjacent phases are simultaneously energized: $AB \rightarrow BC \rightarrow CD \rightarrow DA$. The rotor aligns to the *resultant flux axis* between two poles. Step size is still $\alpha$ but torque is $\sqrt{2} \cdot T_{wave}$ due to additive MMF.

**3. Half-Step Excitation (2 Marks):**
A combination of wave and full-step: $A \rightarrow AB \rightarrow B \rightarrow BC \rightarrow C \rightarrow \ldots$ The effective step size becomes $\alpha/2$, doubling the resolution. Torque alternates between $T_{wave}$ and $\sqrt{2} \cdot T_{wave}$.

**Comparison Table (1 Mark):**

| Mode | Step | Holding Torque | Power |
|------|------|----------------|-------|
| Wave | $\alpha$ | $T$ | $I^2 R$ |
| Full-step | $\alpha$ | $\sqrt{2} T$ | $2I^2 R$ |
| Half-step | $\alpha/2$ | $T$ to $\sqrt{2}T$ | $1.5 I^2 R$ |

**Part (b) — Hybrid Stepper Motor (7 Marks):**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
- Two *toothed rotor cups* mounted on a common shaft, separated by an axially magnetized permanent magnet disc.
- The teeth on the front cup are *offset by half a tooth-pitch* relative to the rear cup, so the magnetic flux is *axial* through the magnet and *radial* across the air gaps.
- The stator has toothed poles with concentrated windings (typically 2 phases, 8 poles).
- One rotor cup is polarized as North, the other as South, by the central PM.

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
- The PM produces a *constant bias flux* (like PMSM).
- The stator field modulates this bias flux (like VRSM).
- Each phase pulse causes the rotor to step by an angle determined by the tooth-pitch relationship, producing *very small step angles* (e.g., $0.9\degree$ or $1.8\degree$).

**Why Preferred (2 Marks):**
1. Combines *high holding torque* of PMSM with *small step angle* of VRSM.
2. Better damping and reduced resonance compared to VRSM.
3. Suitable for *high-resolution positioning* in CNC and 3D printers.
4. Lower position error due to high $N_r$ and strong $T_{max}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing $N_r$ with $N_s$:** Students often plug stator teeth count into the step-angle formula. Always double-check: $N_r$ = *rotor* teeth, $N_s$ = *stator* teeth.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in co-energy torque:** The static torque equation $T = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \, dL/d\theta$ yields a *negative* value when $dL/d\theta < 0$. The sign indicates *restoring* direction.
> 3. **Mixing up wave vs. full-step holding torque:** Wave gives $T$, full-step gives $\sqrt{2}T$. Many students write "$2T$" by mistake.
> 4. **Position error formula missing the $N_r$ factor:** Position error is $\theta_e = T_L / (T_{max} N_r)$, not $T_L / T_{max}$. This $N_r$ term is a *huge* factor (often 50+ in hybrid motors) and examiners *will* check it.
> 5. **Drawing the rotor magnetically aligned but with the *wrong* phase:** When the diagram shows phase B energized, the rotor must be drawn *closer* to pole B, not pole A.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 Stepper Motors**

- **Three Types:** VRSM (no magnet), PMSM (cylindrical PM rotor), Hybrid (toothed rotor + axially magnetized PM).
- **Step Angle Formula:** $\alpha = \dfrac{360\degree}{m \cdot N_r}$ (VR/PM); $\alpha = \dfrac{360\degree}{2 \cdot m \cdot N_r}$ (Hybrid).
- **Static Torque (VRSM):** $T = -\tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta} = -T_{max}\sin(N_r \theta)$. Depends on $i^2$, not on current direction.
- **Co-energy Method:** Always use $T = \partial W_{co} / \partial \theta$ at constant $i$.
- **Modes of Excitation:** Wave ($\alpha$, $T$), Full-step ($\alpha$, $\sqrt{2}T$), Half-step ($\alpha/2$, alternating).
- **Position Error:** $\theta_e = \dfrac{T_L}{T_{max} N_r} = \dfrac{T_L}{k_\theta}$ — directly proportional to load, inversely proportional to stiffness.
- **Speed Relation:** $v_{mech} = \alpha \cdot f$ (rad/s), with $f$ in pps.
- **Stator vs. Rotor Teeth:** $N_s$ ≠ $N_r$ in general; verify the diagram before applying formulas.
- **Hybrid is best** for industrial use due to *small step* + *high torque* + *good damping*.
- **VRSM advantage:** No detent torque → predictable, stable holding; uses unipolar drive.
- **PMSM advantage:** Higher detent torque gives inherent "locking" when power is removed.
- **Open-loop caveat:** Stall occurs silently; consider adding a feedback encoder for critical applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Parameters - Resolution - Single Step Response and Accuracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT638 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Performance Parameters - Resolution - Single Step Response and Accuracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Stepper Motor Performance Parameters

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Stepper Motor?
A **Stepper Motor** is a brushless, synchronous electromechanical device that converts electrical pulses into discrete mechanical angular steps. Each incoming pulse advances the rotor by a fixed angle called the **step angle (β)**. Unlike conventional motors, position is controlled **open-loop** — the rotor position is presumed to follow every pulse without feedback.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1, the focus is on the **static and dynamic performance parameters** that govern *how well* a stepper motor translates pulses into accurate shaft rotation. The three pillars are: **Resolution, Single Step Response, and Accuracy.**

### 1.2 Key Performance Parameters — Intuitive Overview

| Performance Parameter | Layman Analogy |
|------------------------|----------------|
| **Resolution (Step Angle β)** | Imagine a staircase. The height of each stair is the smallest distance you can move — that's the **step angle**. Smaller stairs = finer resolution. |
| **Single Step Response** | Push a swing once and watch it oscillate before settling — that's the **single step response** of a stepper motor. |
| **Accuracy** | The difference between where the swing *actually* stops and where you *intended* it to stop — that's the **positional error**. |
| **Stepping Error** | If the swing overshoots and lands one stair too high, that's a **stepping error** or **lost step**. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Step Angle):**
> The **step angle β** is the angular displacement of the rotor per input pulse, mathematically given as:
>
> $$\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r \times Ph}$$
>
> where **$N_r$** = number of rotor teeth and **$Ph$** = number of stator phases.

### 1.3 Resolution
**Resolution** is the smallest incremental angular movement a stepper motor can produce. It is identical to the step angle for a full-step drive but can be subdivided further using **microstepping** techniques.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Resolution is the **ability of a stepper system to distinguish between two closely spaced positions.** Higher resolution = finer angular increments per pulse.

### 1.4 Accuracy
**Accuracy** is the measure of how close the actual rotor position is to the commanded (theoretical) position. It is expressed in **degrees of mechanical error** or as a **percentage of step angle**.

- **Static Accuracy:** Measured at standstill or very low speeds.
- **Dynamic Accuracy:** Measured during stepping at rated pulse rates.

### 1.5 Single Step Response
When a **single pulse** is applied to a stepper motor winding, the rotor does not jump instantly to the new position. It undergoes a **second-order damped oscillation** before settling. This transient response is the **Single Step Response** and is the most important dynamic performance metric for evaluating motor damping and stability.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Resolution — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Step Angle Derivation
For a multi-stack variable reluctance (VR) stepper motor:
- The stator has **Ph** phases excited sequentially.
- Each phase produces a step of $\frac{360°}{Ph}$ if the rotor had no teeth.
- With **$N_r$** rotor teeth, the effective step angle is reduced by a factor of $N_r$.

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r \times Ph}}$$

### 2.1.2 Resolution Modes
| Drive Mode | Effective Step Angle | Use Case |
|------------|----------------------|----------|
| Full-Step | $\beta$ | Standard industrial |
| Half-Step | $\beta/2$ | Smoother motion |
| Microstepping (1/10) | $\beta/10$ | Precision CNC, robotics |
| Microstepping (1/256) | $\beta/256$ | High-precision optics |

## 2.2 Accuracy — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Sources of Error
1. **Mechanical Manufacturing Tolerances** — Tooth profile deviation.
2. **Magnetic Asymmetry** — Unequal air-gap flux.
3. **Friction and Load Disturbance** — Static friction holds rotor off target.
4. **Lost Steps** — At high pulse rates, rotor fails to follow the stator field.
5. **Damping Mismatch** — Inadequate damping causes overshoot and undershoot.

### 2.2.2 Stepping Accuracy Specification
The **positional accuracy** is typically specified as:

$$\text{Stepping Accuracy} = \pm \frac{\beta}{4} \text{ to } \pm 10\%\text{ of }\beta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A standard hybrid stepper motor has a non-accumulating stepping accuracy of $\pm 3\%$ (non-cumulative) but the position error can be cumulative over multiple steps in one direction.

## 2.3 Single Step Response — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 The Mechanical Model
A stepper motor in transient behaves like a **second-order rotational mass-spring-damper system**:
- **J** = Rotor + load moment of inertia (kg·m²)
- **D** = Viscous damping coefficient (N·m·s/rad)
- **K** = Torsional stiffness (N·m/rad), related to detent torque

### 2.3.2 Equation of Motion

$$J\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + D\frac{d\theta}{dt} + K(\theta - \theta_i) = 0$$

where $\theta_i$ is the initial rotor position and the new equilibrium is at $\theta_{eq} = \theta_i + \beta$.

### 2.3.3 Damping Conditions
| Damping Ratio $\zeta$ | Condition | Response Behavior |
|------------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| $\zeta < 1$ | **Underdamped** | Oscillatory with overshoot |
| $\zeta = 1$ | **Critically Damped** | Fastest no-overshoot |
| $\zeta > 1$ | **Overdamped** | Slow, sluggish response |
| $\zeta = 0$ | **Undamped** | Sustained oscillation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked about "optimum damping," the answer is **critical damping** or slight underdamping ($\zeta \approx 0.7$) for best trade-off between speed and stability.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all critical equations. **Memorize these for the 14-mark derivation questions.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Step Angle | $\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot Ph}$ | degrees | Smallest angular increment |
| 2 | Resolution | $R = \frac{1}{\beta}$ | steps/rev | Steps per revolution |
| 3 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$ | rad/s | Undamped natural frequency |
| 4 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{D}{2\sqrt{K \cdot J}}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of critical damping |
| 5 | Damped Natural Freq. | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ | rad/s | Actual oscillation frequency |
| 6 | Settling Time (2%) | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | seconds | Time to settle within 2% |
| 7 | Rise Time (10–90%) | $t_r \approx \dfrac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ | seconds | Time to reach 90% of $\beta$ |
| 8 | Peak Overshoot | $M_p = e^{\left(\dfrac{-\zeta \pi}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\right)}$ | dimensionless | Fractional overshoot |
| 9 | Phase Angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}{\zeta}\right)$ | radians | Initial phase |
| 10 | Stepping Error | $\Delta\theta = \vert \theta_{actual} - \theta_{cmd} \vert$ | degrees | Positional error |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Note:** All `|x|` notations in the table have been written as `\vert x \vert` style (e.g., `\vert \theta_{actual} - \theta_{cmd} \vert`) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application | Critical Performance Parameter |
|-------|-------------|-------------------------------|
| **CNC Machines** | X-Y-Z axis positioning | Resolution + Accuracy |
| **3D Printers** | Extruder head motion | Microstepping Resolution |
| **Robotics** | Joint articulation | Single Step Response (smoothness) |
| **Hard Disk Drives** | Head positioning | Sub-micron accuracy |
| **Medical Pumps** | Syringe drive | Step accuracy for dosage |
| **Watch / Clock Industry** | Quartz stepper motors | High accuracy, low power |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Step Angle of a Multi-Stack Stepper Motor

### Statement
Prove that the step angle of a multi-stack variable reluctance stepper motor with $N_r$ rotor teeth and $Ph$ stator phases is $\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot Ph}$.

### Proof

**Step 1 — Define the rotor tooth pitch.**
The angular pitch between two adjacent rotor teeth is:

$$\theta_{teeth} = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

**Step 2 — Effect of phase excitation.**
When the stator phases are excited in sequence (e.g., phase A → B → C → A), each phase shift advances the stator magnetic field by $\frac{360°}{Ph}$ around the air gap. The rotor aligns itself with the new field.

**Step 3 — Combined effect on rotor position.**
For one complete electrical cycle of the stator (all $Ph$ phases pulsed once), the rotor moves by **one tooth pitch**:

$$\text{Movement per electrical cycle} = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

**Step 4 — Movement per single pulse.**
The number of pulses per electrical cycle equals $Ph$ (one pulse per phase). Therefore, the movement per pulse (i.e., the step angle) is:

$$\beta = \frac{\text{Movement per cycle}}{\text{Pulses per cycle}} = \frac{360°/N_r}{Ph}$$

**Step 5 — Final Expression.**

$$\boxed{\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot Ph}}$$

**Verification Example (KTU Numerical):**
A hybrid stepper motor has 200 rotor teeth and 2 phases. Find:
(a) Step angle
(b) Resolution in steps/revolution
(c) Shaft rotation for 1500 input pulses

*Solution:*
- (a) $\beta = \frac{360°}{200 \times 2} = 0.9°$
- (b) $R = \frac{360°}{0.9°} = 400 \text{ steps/rev}$
- (c) $\theta = 1500 \times 0.9° = 1350° = 3.75 \text{ revolutions}$ ✓

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Single Step Response Equation

### Statement
Derive the time-domain equation $\theta(t)$ for the single step response of a stepper motor with inertia $J$, damping $D$, and stiffness $K$, when a step command of magnitude $\beta$ is applied at $t = 0$.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Formulate the governing differential equation.**
Let the rotor position be $\theta(t)$ and the new equilibrium be $\theta_{eq} = \theta_0 + \beta$. Define the displacement from the initial position:

$$x(t) = \theta(t) - \theta_0$$

Then $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + x(t)$, and $\frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{dx}{dt}$, etc.

Substituting into the rotational Newton's second law:

$$J\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + D\frac{dx}{dt} + K \cdot x = J\frac{d^2\theta_{eq}}{dt^2} + D\frac{d\theta_{eq}}{dt} + K \cdot \theta_{eq}$$

Since $\theta_{eq}$ is a constant for a single step, all time derivatives on the RHS are zero. The forcing function enters through the **initial conditions**:

$$J\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + D\frac{dx}{dt} + K \cdot x = 0$$

**Step 2 — Define standard second-order system parameters.**

Let:

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{K}{J}, \quad 2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{D}{J}$$

Then:

$$\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + 2\zeta\omega_n\frac{dx}{dt} + \omega_n^2 \cdot x = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve the homogeneous ODE.**
The characteristic equation is:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

Roots:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm j\omega_d$$

where:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

For an **underdamped system** ($\zeta < 1$):

$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left(A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply initial conditions.**
At $t = 0$:
- $x(0) = 0$ (rotor starts from initial position)
- $\dot{x}(0) = 0$ (rotor at rest)

From $x(0) = 0$:

$$A = 0$$

From $\dot{x}(0) = 0$:

$$B\omega_d - \zeta\omega_n \cdot A = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0 \text{ (if } A = 0\text{)}$$

This gives a trivial solution, so we need a **forcing function** approach instead. The step input of magnitude $\beta$ means we need a particular solution.

**Step 5 — Forced response with step input.**
The actual forcing is a step function of amplitude $\beta$ on the displacement. The standard step response of a second-order system is:

$$x(t) = \beta\left[1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\sin\left(\omega_d t + \phi\right)\right]$$

where:

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}{\zeta}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Verify boundary conditions.**
- At $t = 0$: $\sin(\phi) = \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$, so $x(0) = 0$ ✓
- As $t \to \infty$: $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} \to 0$, so $x(\infty) = \beta$ ✓

**Step 7 — Final boxed expression.**

$$\boxed{\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \beta\left[1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\sin\left(\omega_d t + \phi\right)\right]}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Performance Parameter Relationships

| Parameter | Increases When | Decreases When | Effect on Performance |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|-----------------------|
| **Stiffness $K$** | Detent torque ↑ | Tooth pitch ↑ | Faster response, more overshoot |
| **Inertia $J$** | Load mass ↑ | — | Slower response, less overshoot |
| **Damping $D$** | External damper added | — | Reduces overshoot, may slow response |
| **Tooth count $N_r$** | Manufacturing ↑ | — | Finer resolution, higher $\omega_n$ |
| **Phase count $Ph$** | — | — | Coarser step angle |

### 3.3.1 Trade-off Analysis (KTU Board Favorite)

| Optimization Goal | Adjust $J$ | Adjust $D$ | Adjust $K$ |
|-------------------|------------|------------|------------|
| Reduce overshoot | Increase | Increase | Decrease |
| Increase speed | Decrease | — | Increase |
| Improve accuracy | — | Critically damp | — |
| Increase resolution | — | — | Increase $N_r$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Open-Loop Stepper Motor Drive System

The following block diagram illustrates the **interactions** of a stepper motor control system and how performance parameters propagate through it.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Pulse Generator Controller] -->|Pulse Train| B[Ring Counter Decoder]
    B -->|Logic Signals| C[Power Amplifier]
    C -->|Phase Currents| D[Stator Winding]
    D -->|Magnetic Field| E[Rotor Alignment]
    E -->|Mechanical Step| F[Shaft Output]
    F -->|Load| G[Mechanical Load]
    F -->|Position| H[Open Loop No Feedback]
    I[Encoder] -.->|Optional Position Feedback| A
    F --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The system is **open-loop** by design. The pulse count directly determines the shaft position. This is why **single step response** and **accuracy** become critical — there is no automatic correction for missed steps.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Single Step Response Characteristics

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Input Single Step Pulse] --> nodeB[Stepper Motor Electromechanical System]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Second Order Response}
    nodeC -->|Underdamped Zeta less than 1| nodeD[Overshoot Oscillations Settling Time]
    nodeC -->|Critically Damped Zeta equals 1| nodeE[No Overshoot Fast Settling]
    nodeC -->|Overdamped Zeta greater than 1| nodeF[Slow Exponential Settling No Oscillation]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Maximum Overshoot Mp]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Settling Time Ts]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Rise Time Tr]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[Optimal Response]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Sluggish Response]
    nodeG --> nodeL[Accuracy Assessment]
    nodeH --> nodeL
    nodeI --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM[Final Stepping Accuracy]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Single Step Response Waveform Components

```mermaid
graph TB
    s1[Steady State Position Theta Eq] --> s2[Final Settled Position]
    s3[Commanded Step of Magnitude Beta] --> s4[At time t equals 0]
    s5[Peak Overshoot Mp Occurs at First Peak] --> s6[Subsequent Oscillations Decay]
    s7[Rise Time Tr] --> s8[From 10 percent to 90 percent of Beta]
    s9[Settling Time Ts] --> s10[Within 2 percent or 5 percent band]
    s11[Underdamped Response Curve] --> s12[Theta of t]
    s12 --> s13[Starts at Theta 0 Oscillates around Theta Eq]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** When drawing the single step response, always label:
> 1. **Steady-state value** ($\theta_{eq} = \theta_0 + \beta$)
> 2. **Overshoot** ($M_p$)
> 3. **Settling time** ($t_s$)
> 4. **Number of oscillations** (indicator of damping level)

## 4.4 Performance Parameter Inter-relationship Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    p1[Resolution Step Angle Beta] --> p2[Finer Step Better Positioning]
    p1 --> p3[More Steps per Revolution]
    p4[Single Step Response] --> p5[Damping Ratio Zeta]
    p4 --> p6[Natural Frequency Omega n]
    p5 --> p7[Overshoot Mp]
    p6 --> p8[Rise Time Tr]
    p7 --> p9[Stepping Accuracy]
    p8 --> p9
    p10[Stiffness K] --> p6
    p11[Inertia J] --> p6
    p12[Damping D] --> p5
    p9 --> p13[Overall Motor Performance Grade]
    p3 --> p13
    p7 --> p13
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "resolution" of a stepper motor. A hybrid stepper motor has 50 rotor teeth and operates in half-step mode with 4 phases. Calculate the effective step angle and resolution in steps/revolution.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
**Definition (1 mark):** Resolution is the smallest angular increment a stepper motor can produce per input pulse, equal to its step angle in full-step mode.

**Calculation (2 marks):**
- Full-step angle: $\beta_{full} = \frac{360°}{50 \times 4} = 1.8°$
- Half-step mode: $\beta_{half} = \frac{\beta_{full}}{2} = 0.9°$
- Resolution: $R = \frac{360°}{0.9°} = 400 \text{ steps/rev}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always state the formula before substituting. Mention half-step divides the basic step by 2.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is meant by "stepping accuracy" of a stepper motor? List any two factors affecting it.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
**Definition (1 mark):** Stepping accuracy is the degree to which the actual rotor position matches the commanded theoretical position, expressed as the maximum deviation in degrees or as a percentage of the step angle.

**Two factors (2 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Mechanical tolerance** of rotor and stator teeth (manufacturing imperfections).
2. **Frictional and load disturbances** causing static offset at rest.
3. **Lost steps** at high pulse rates (dynamic error).
4. **Damping characteristics** affecting overshoot and settling.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (i) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q. (a)** Derive the expression for the **step angle** of a multi-stack variable reluctance stepper motor. A VR stepper motor has 8 rotor teeth and 3 phases. Determine the step angle, resolution, and shaft rotation for 500 input pulses. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **single step response** of a stepper motor. Define overshoot, rise time, and settling time. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Derivation (4 marks):**
- Define rotor tooth pitch: $\theta_{teeth} = \frac{360°}{N_r}$ ... (1 mark)
- Identify phase contribution: $Ph$ phases per electrical cycle ... (1 mark)
- One electrical cycle moves rotor by one tooth pitch: $\frac{360°}{N_r}$ ... (1 mark)
- Step angle per pulse: $\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot Ph}$ ... (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Numerical (3 marks):**
Given $N_r = 8$, $Ph = 3$, $N_{pulses} = 500$:

$$\beta = \frac{360°}{8 \times 3} = \frac{360°}{24} = 15° \text{ per step} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

$$R = \frac{360°}{15°} = 24 \text{ steps/rev} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

$$\theta_{shaft} = 500 \times 15° = 7500° = \frac{7500}{360} = 20.833 \text{ rev} \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Sketch (3 marks):** Draw $\theta$ vs $t$ showing:
- Initial position $\theta_0$ at $t = 0$
- Steady-state value $\theta_{eq} = \theta_0 + \beta$
- Overshoot peak $M_p$
- Damped oscillations decaying to $\theta_{eq}$
- Label: rise time $t_r$, settling time $t_s$

**Definitions (3 marks — 1 each):**
- **Overshoot ($M_p$):** The maximum amount the response exceeds the steady-state value, expressed as a percentage:
$$M_p = \frac{\theta_{peak} - \theta_{eq}}{\theta_{eq} - \theta_0} \times 100\%$$
- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time taken for the response to rise from 10% to 90% of the final value.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time taken for the response to stay within a tolerance band (typically 2% or 5%) of the final value.

**Explanation (1 mark):** The response is a second-order underdamped oscillation, characterized by natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$. The motor must be properly damped (close to $\zeta = 0.7$) to balance speed and accuracy.

---

### Question B — Option (ii) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q. (a)** With the help of a **rotor mass-spring-damper mechanical model**, derive the differential equation of motion for a stepper motor and obtain the **single step response equation** $\theta(t)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** Discuss the **factors affecting stepping accuracy** and explain how damping ratio influences overshoot in a stepper motor. A motor has $K = 5 \text{ N·m/rad}$, $J = 0.005 \text{ kg·m}^2$, and $D = 0.1 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$. Find the **damping ratio** and comment on the response type. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Model diagram (2 marks):**
Represent the rotor as:
- Inertia $J$ (mass element)
- Damping $D$ (dashpot)
- Stiffness $K$ (spring representing magnetic detent torque)

**Step 2 — Equation of motion (2 marks):**
Sum of torques on rotor = Inertia × angular acceleration:

$$J\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + D\frac{d\theta}{dt} + K(\theta - \theta_{eq}) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Standard form (1 mark):**
Divide by $J$:

$$\frac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + 2\zeta\omega_n\frac{d\theta}{dt} + \omega_n^2 \theta = \omega_n^2 \theta_{eq}$$

**Step 4 — Solution (2 marks):**
For underdamped case, applying initial conditions $\theta(0) = \theta_0$, $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$:

$$\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \beta\left[1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\sin\left(\omega_d t + \phi\right)\right]$$

with $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ and $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}{\zeta}\right)$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Factors affecting accuracy (3 marks — 1 each for 3 factors):**
1. **Damping ratio** — Under-damped motors overshoot; over-damped motors lag.
2. **Load inertia mismatch** — Adds to $J$, slowing response and risking lost steps.
3. **Pulse rate** — At high rates, rotor cannot keep up, causing **lost steps** and **reduced accuracy**.
4. **Friction** — Static friction creates **dead zone** around the commanded position.

**Damping influence on overshoot (2 marks):**
- $M_p$ depends exponentially on $\zeta$:

$$M_p = e^{\left(\frac{-\zeta \pi}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\right)} \times 100\%$$

- Higher $\zeta$ → Lower overshoot.
- $\zeta = 0.7$ → $M_p \approx 4.3\%$ (industrial standard).
- $\zeta = 0$ → $M_p = 100\%$ (sustained oscillation).
- $\zeta \geq 1$ → No overshoot, but slow.

**Numerical (2 marks):**
Given $K = 5$ N·m/rad, $J = 0.005$ kg·m², $D = 0.1$ N·m·s/rad:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{J}} = \sqrt{\frac{5}{0.005}} = \sqrt{1000} = 31.62 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{D}{2\sqrt{KJ}} = \frac{0.1}{2\sqrt{5 \times 0.005}} = \frac{0.1}{2\sqrt{0.025}} = \frac{0.1}{2 \times 0.1581} = \frac{0.1}{0.3162} = 0.316$$

**Comment:** $\zeta = 0.316 < 1$ → **Underdamped** response. Significant overshoot (~35%) and visible oscillations. The motor is **poorly damped** and would benefit from external damping.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the phase angle $\phi$** in the single step response equation (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Confusing resolution and accuracy** — Resolution is *capability*, accuracy is *performance*. (lose 1 mark).
> 3. **Incorrect unit for step angle** — Must be in **degrees** (or radians, with conversion). (lose 0.5 mark).
> 4. **Not specifying the damping condition** when calculating response (lose 1 mark).
> 5. **Mixing up cumulative vs non-cumulative error** in stepping accuracy. (lose 1 mark).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] **Step Angle (β):** $\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot Ph}$ — smallest angular step per pulse.
- [ ] **Resolution:** Number of discrete steps per revolution = $\frac{360°}{\beta}$.
- [ ] **Stepping Accuracy:** Closeness of actual to commanded position; typically $\pm 3\%$ to $\pm 10\%$.
- [ ] **Single Step Response:** Transient second-order response to a single pulse command.

### Critical Equations
- [ ] **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{J}}$
- [ ] **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \frac{D}{2\sqrt{KJ}}$
- [ ] **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$
- [ ] **Step response:** $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \beta\!\left[1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)\right]$
- [ ] **Peak overshoot:** $M_p = e^{\left(\frac{-\zeta\pi}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}\right)} \times 100\%$

### Damping Behavior (Must Memorize)
- [ ] $\zeta < 1$ → **Underdamped** (oscillations, overshoot)
- [ ] $\zeta = 1$ → **Critically damped** (no overshoot, fastest)
- [ ] $\zeta > 1$ → **Overdamped** (sluggish, no overshoot)
- [ ] $\zeta \approx 0.7$ → **Industrial optimum** (~4.3% overshoot)

### Practical Notes
- [ ] Stepper motors are **open-loop** — accuracy depends entirely on **pulse fidelity**.
- [ ] **Lost steps** at high pulse rates = catastrophic accuracy loss.
- [ ] **Microstepping** improves resolution but not inherent accuracy.
- [ ] **Higher $N_r$** = finer resolution, higher $\omega_n$, faster response.
- [ ] **Damping** is often added externally via **eddy-current dampers** or **closed-loop control**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, always:
> 1. State the **assumption** (e.g., "assuming an underdamped second-order system").
> 2. Write the **governing equation** with units.
> 3. Solve **step-by-step** showing algebraic transitions.
> 4. **Substitute numerical values** with clear units.
> 5. **Conclude** with physical interpretation of the result.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pull-in and Pull-out Characteristics - Resonance Issues. Unipolar and Bipolar Drive Schemes - Bifilar Drives - Open Loop Position Control - Starting/Stopping Rate - Velocity Profiling - Closed Loop Control of Stepper Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT638 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pull-in and Pull-out Characteristics - Resonance Issues. Unipolar and Bipolar Drive Schemes - Bifilar Drives - Open Loop Position Control - Starting/Stopping Rate - Velocity Profiling - Closed Loop Control of Stepper Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Pull-in and Pull-out Characteristics

### 1.1.1 Formal Definition
**Pull-out torque** (also called *holding torque* or *slew torque*) of a stepper motor is defined as the maximum load torque that the motor can drive at a given stepping rate without losing synchronism. It represents the upper boundary of the safe operating region during slewing (continuous rotation) operation.

**Pull-in torque** (also called *start-stop torque* or *pull-in rate torque*) is defined as the maximum load torque against which the motor can start, stop, or reverse without losing synchronism at a given pulse rate. It establishes the upper boundary of the start-stop operating region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The two characteristic curves (pull-out vs. stepping rate and pull-in vs. stepping rate) bound the **start-stop region** (below pull-in curve) and the **slew region** (between pull-in and pull-out curves). The KTU examiner frequently asks students to *draw and label* these regions.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy
Think of a stepper motor like a person climbing a staircase with a heavy backpack:
- **Pull-out torque** = the maximum weight you can carry while *continuously climbing* at a steady pace. If the bag gets heavier, you lose balance mid-step.
- **Pull-in torque** = the maximum weight you can carry when you *start from rest on each step* (stop-and-go). This is always smaller because starting is harder than continuing.
- At very high speeds (very fast climbing), you can carry less weight — both limits drop because your muscles cannot react fast enough.
- At very slow speeds (one step per minute), pull-in and pull-out converge to the **holding torque** (your maximum strength at rest).

### 1.1.3 The Three Operating Regions

| Region | Definition | Torque Capability |
|---|---|---|
| **Start-Stop Region** | Below pull-in curve | Motor can start, stop, reverse at any point in this region |
| **Slew Region** | Between pull-in and pull-out curves | Motor can run continuously, but cannot start/stop/reverse at this speed |
| **Forbidden Region** | Above pull-out curve | Motor loses synchronism (stalls) |

## 1.2 Resonance Issues

### 1.2.1 Formal Definition
Resonance in a stepper motor occurs when the **pulse (step) frequency** coincides with the **natural mechanical frequency** of the rotor-load system, causing the rotor oscillations to build up in amplitude, leading to **loss of synchronism** or severe vibration.

The natural frequency of the single-step response is:
$$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K_T \cdot K \cdot N_s}{J}}$$

where $K_T$ is the torque constant, $K$ is a damping-related constant, $N_s$ is the number of steps per revolution, and $J$ is the rotor inertia.

### 1.2.2 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push at just the right rhythm (matching the swing's natural back-and-forth), the swing goes higher and higher — that's resonance. If you push randomly, the swing barely moves. A stepper motor behaves similarly: pulses arriving at the rotor's natural frequency cause wild oscillations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students confuse **mid-frequency resonance** (at single-step natural frequency, typically 100-300 Hz for PM stepper motors) with **low-frequency resonance** (at sub-harmonics of natural frequency). The KTU board expects both to be mentioned.

## 1.3 Drive Schemes for Stepper Motors

### 1.3.1 Unipolar Drive
A **unipolar drive** uses a center-tapped winding per phase. Only half the winding is energized at a time, driven by a single transistor per phase. Current flows in only one direction (hence "uni-polar"), and the direction is selected by energizing the appropriate half-winding.

### 1.3.2 Bipolar Drive
A **bipolar drive** uses the full phase winding (no center tap) with an **H-bridge** configuration. Current is *reversed* in the winding by changing the active transistor pair, allowing current to flow in both directions (hence "bi-polar"). This uses the entire winding, producing higher torque per unit volume.

### 1.3.3 Bifilar Drive
A **bifilar wound** stepper motor has each phase wound with **two identical parallel strands** that are physically wound together. One strand is energized (active) while the other acts as a return path, simulating a center-tap but with tighter magnetic coupling. This was historically used to allow bipolar drive control using unipolar-style circuits.

## 1.4 Open Loop and Closed Loop Position Control

### 1.4.1 Open Loop Control
In **open loop control**, the controller sends a fixed number of pulses to the motor without any feedback on the actual rotor position. The position is assumed to be proportional to the number of pulses sent. This is the *native* operating mode of stepper motors.

### 1.4.2 Closed Loop Control
In **closed loop control**, a position sensor (encoder or resolver) measures the actual rotor position and feeds it back to the controller. The controller compares actual vs. commanded position and adjusts the drive signals. This eliminates the possibility of **stall** (lost steps).

### 1.4.3 Velocity Profiling & Starting/Stopping Rate
**Starting rate** is the maximum pulse rate at which the motor can start from rest without losing steps (limited by pull-in torque).
**Stopping rate** is the maximum pulse rate from which the motor can stop without losing steps.
**Velocity profiling** is the technique of ramping the pulse frequency (acceleration → constant velocity → deceleration) to traverse the start-stop region safely and operate in the slew region with maximum throughput.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Velocity Profiling?** A stepper motor cannot instantaneously jump from rest to high speed because the required torque at high stepping rates falls below typical load torques. Velocity profiling uses a *trapezoidal* or *S-curve* profile to keep the motor in its safe operating envelope.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Static (Holding) Torque Equation

For a multi-stack or hybrid stepper motor, the static holding torque developed when one phase is excited is given by:

$$T_h = \dfrac{1}{2} \, i^2 \, \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$$

where:
- $T_h$ = holding torque (Nm)
- $i$ = phase current (A)
- $L$ = phase inductance (H)
- $\theta$ = rotor angular position (rad)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the inductance varies approximately sinusoidally with position, $L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$, the torque contains both a **detent torque** component (from permanent magnet) and a **reluctance torque** component (from variable reluctance).

## 2.2 Pull-out Torque vs. Speed Characteristic

The pull-out torque decreases with stepping rate due to the winding inductance limiting the rate of rise of current. The empirical relation is:

$$T_{po}(f) = \dfrac{T_{h}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$$

where:
- $T_{po}(f)$ = pull-out torque at pulse frequency $f$
- $T_h$ = holding torque
- $f_c$ = cutoff frequency $= R/(2\pi L)$

**Derivation of Cutoff Frequency:** The winding is a series RL circuit. The current response to a step voltage $V$ is:
$$i(t) = \dfrac{V}{R}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right), \quad \tau = L/R$$

The cutoff frequency (where reactance equals resistance) is:
$$2\pi f_c L = R \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_c = \dfrac{R}{2\pi L}$$

## 2.3 Pull-in Torque vs. Speed Characteristic

The pull-in characteristic is more complex than the pull-out because it involves the **single-step response** of the rotor. Empirically:

$$T_{pi}(f) = T_{po}(0) \left[ 1 - \left(\dfrac{f}{f_{pi,\max}}\right)^2 \right]$$

where $f_{pi,\max}$ is the maximum pull-in rate (typically much lower than the maximum slew rate).

## 2.4 Resonance Frequency

The single-step natural frequency of the unloaded rotor is:

$$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{T_h \, N_s}{J \, \theta_s^2}} \approx \dfrac{N_s}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{T_h}{J}}$$

where $\theta_s = 2\pi/N_s$ is the step angle.

**Low-frequency resonance** occurs at sub-multiples:
$$f_{res,k} = f_n / k, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

## 2.5 Acceleration Rate (Starting/Stopping)

The maximum acceleration rate is limited by the available torque:

$$\alpha_{\max} = \dfrac{T_{po}(f) - T_L}{J_{eq}}$$

where $J_{eq} = J_{rotor} + J_{load}$ is the equivalent inertia referred to the motor shaft.

**Converting to stepping rate acceleration:**

$$\dfrac{df}{dt} = \dfrac{N_s}{2\pi} \cdot \alpha = \dfrac{N_s}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{T_{po}(f) - T_L}{J_{eq}}$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Holding Torque | $T_h = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Nm | At $\theta = 0$ (aligned position) |
| 2 | Cutoff Frequency | $f_c = R / (2\pi L)$ | Hz | RL winding limit |
| 3 | Pull-out Torque | $T_{po} = T_h / \sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}$ | Nm | Slewing region |
| 4 | Pull-in Torque | $T_{pi} = T_{po}(0)[1 - (f/f_{pi,\max})^2]$ | Nm | Start-stop region |
| 5 | Natural Frequency | $f_n = (N_s/2\pi)\sqrt{T_h/J}$ | Hz | Single-step resonance |
| 6 | Sub-harmonic Res. | $f_{res,k} = f_n/k$ | Hz | $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ |
| 7 | Max Acceleration | $\alpha_{\max} = (T_{po}-T_L)/J_{eq}$ | rad/s² | During velocity ramp |
| 8 | Step Rate Accel. | $df/dt = (N_s/2\pi)\,\alpha$ | Hz/s | Pulses per second per second |
| 9 | Step Angle | $\theta_s = 360°/N_s$ | degrees | $N_s$ = steps per revolution |
| 10 | Electrical Time Const. | $\tau_e = L/R$ | s | Determines current rise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These equations are used in designing **CNC machines, 3D printers, robotics, and telescope tracking mounts** where precise position control is mandatory. Velocity profiling formulas are coded into motion controllers (e.g., GRBL, Mach3) to drive stepper motors reliably.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Pull-out Torque Frequency Dependence

### Step 1: Model the Phase Winding as a Series RL Circuit
When a voltage $V$ is applied across a phase winding of resistance $R$ and inductance $L(\theta)$ (function of rotor position), the circuit equation is:

$$V = R \, i(t) + \dfrac{d}{dt}[L(\theta) \, i(t)]$$

### Step 2: Linearize at the Worst-Case Position
The maximum inductance occurs at the unaligned position. Treating $L$ as approximately constant at its average value $\bar{L}$:

$$V = R \, i(t) + \bar{L} \, \dfrac{di(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 3: Solve for the Steady-State Sinusoidal Current
For a square-wave voltage excitation at frequency $f$, the fundamental component has amplitude $V_1 = 4V/\pi$. Solving the phasor equation:

$$I_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{R + j 2\pi f \bar{L}}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Magnitude of Current
The magnitude of the fundamental current is:

$$\vert I_1 \vert = \dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{R^2 + (2\pi f \bar{L})^2}} = \dfrac{V_1/R}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$$

where $f_c = R/(2\pi \bar{L})$ is the cutoff frequency.

### Step 5: Relate Torque to Current
Since torque is proportional to the square of current (or to the product of current and a position-dependent inductance gradient), the average pull-out torque is proportional to $\vert I_1 \vert^2$:

$$T_{po}(f) = T_h \cdot \dfrac{\vert I_1 \vert^2}{(V_1/R)^2} = \dfrac{T_h}{1 + (f/f_c)^2}$$

**Refinement (using magnitude, not square):** In practice, the torque is more accurately modeled as proportional to current (when detent torque is dominant), giving:

$$T_{po}(f) = \dfrac{T_h}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$$

This is the form most commonly cited in KTU textbooks.

## 3.2 Derivation of Natural Frequency

### Step 1: Rotor Equation of Motion
The rotor obeys Newton's second law for rotation:

$$J \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} + K_{spring}\,\theta = T_{motor} - T_{load}$$

For small oscillations about an equilibrium, the magnetic restoring torque acts as a torsional spring with stiffness $K_{spring}$.

### Step 2: Determine the Torsional Spring Constant
The torque per unit angular displacement (around equilibrium) is:

$$K_{spring} = \left. \dfrac{dT}{d\theta} \right\vert_{\theta = 0} = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{d^2 L}{d\theta^2}\bigg\vert_{\theta = 0}$$

For $L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$:
$$\dfrac{d^2 L}{d\theta^2}\bigg\vert_0 = -L_1 N_r^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_{spring} = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 L_1 N_r^2$$

### Step 3: Identify with Holding Torque
At the equilibrium position, the maximum holding torque corresponds to the peak of the sinusoidal torque-position curve. Equating the maximum torque to the area under the spring curve gives:

$$K_{spring} \approx T_h \cdot N_s / \pi$$

where $N_s = 4N_r$ for a hybrid stepper (electrical cycles per mechanical revolution).

### Step 4: Compute Natural Frequency
For the undamped case ($B = 0$):

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{spring}}{J}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K_{spring}}{J}}$$

Substituting:

$$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{T_h \, N_s}{\pi \, J}}$$

This shows why **low-inertia rotors** and **high holding torque** motors have higher resonance frequencies (more stable operation).

## 3.3 Derivation of Velocity Profile (Trapezoidal)

### Step 1: Define the Motion Task
Move from position $P_1$ to $P_2$ in minimum time $T_{total}$ such that the motor never leaves the safe region.

### Step 2: Set the Constraints
- **Maximum start-stop pulse rate:** $f_{ss,\max}$ (from pull-in curve)
- **Maximum slew pulse rate:** $f_{sl,\max}$ (from pull-out curve)
- **Maximum acceleration:** $\alpha_{\max} = (T_{po} - T_L)/J_{eq}$

### Step 3: Construct the Trapezoidal Profile
The pulse frequency $f(t)$ follows:
- **Acceleration phase:** $0 \le t \le t_a$, $f(t) = a \cdot t$
- **Constant phase:** $t_a \le t \le t_a + t_c$, $f(t) = f_{sl,\max}$
- **Deceleration phase:** $t_a + t_c \le t \le T_{total}$, $f(t) = f_{sl,\max} - a(t - t_a - t_c)$

where $a = df/dt$ is the constant pulse rate acceleration.

### Step 4: Compute Time to Reach Slew Speed

$$t_a = \dfrac{f_{sl,\max}}{a} = \dfrac{f_{sl,\max} \cdot 2\pi \, J_{eq}}{N_s (T_{po} - T_L)}$$

### Step 5: Compute Total Steps Required
Total steps executed (for $N$ total steps needed):

$$N = \int_0^{T_{total}} f(t)\, dt$$

For a symmetric trapezoidal profile:

$$N = a \, t_a^2 + f_{sl,\max} \, t_c$$

Solving for $t_c$:

$$t_c = \dfrac{N - a \, t_a^2}{f_{sl,\max}}$$

> [!TIP]
> If $N \le a \, t_a^2$, the profile is **triangular** (motor never reaches slew speed). The total time is then $T_{total} = 2\sqrt{N/a}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Static (f = 0) | Mid-Frequency | High Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Phase Current | $V/R$ (DC) | $V/(\sqrt{R^2 + \omega^2 L^2})$ | $\approx V/(\omega L)$ |
| Torque Capability | $T_h$ (maximum) | $T_h/\sqrt{1+(f/f_c)^2}$ | $\to 0$ |
| Operating Region | Start-stop = Slew | Slew only | Slew only |
| Resonance Risk | None | **MAXIMUM** (at $f_n$) | None |
| Control Mode | Open loop OK | Open loop risky | Closed loop preferred |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Torque-Speed Operating Envelope

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Origin: Zero Speed, Holding Torque] --> B[Rising Pull-in Curve]
    B --> C[Start-Stop Region Boundary]
    C --> D[Slew Region: Between Pull-in and Pull-out]
    D --> E[Pull-out Curve: Falls with Speed]
    E --> F[High Speed: Torque Tends to Zero]
    G[Cutoff Frequency Marker] --> E
    H[Resonance Forbidden Zone] --> D
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#ffe1e1
    style F fill:#f0f0f0
    style G fill:#ffe1ff
    style H fill:#ffcccc
```

## 4.2 Bipolar H-Bridge Drive Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[DC Bus Vplus] --> Q1[Transistor Q1]
    P --> Q2[Transistor Q2]
    Q1 --> nodeA[Phase A Terminal Top]
    Q2 --> nodeC[Phase A Terminal Bottom]
    nodeA --> LA[Phase Winding L_A]
    LA --> nodeC
    nodeC --> Q3[Transistor Q3]
    nodeA --> Q4[Transistor Q4]
    Q3 --> N[DC Bus Ground]
    Q4 --> N
    CTRL[Controller Logic] --> Q1
    CTRL --> Q2
    CTRL --> Q3
    CTRL --> Q4
    CTRL -.Direction.-> D1[Current Path A: Q1, Q3]
    CTRL -.Direction.-> D2[Current Path B: Q2, Q4]
    style P fill:#ffe1e1
    style N fill:#e1e1ff
    style CTRL fill:#fff4e1
    style D1 fill:#90EE90
    style D2 fill:#FFB6C1
```

**Operating Principle:**
- **Q1 + Q3 ON, Q2 + Q4 OFF:** Current flows from V+ through Q1 → winding → Q3 → Ground. (Forward direction)
- **Q2 + Q4 ON, Q1 + Q3 OFF:** Current flows from V+ through Q2 → winding (reversed) → Q4 → Ground. (Reverse direction)
- **Q1 + Q2 ON (both high-side):** Fast decay recirculation
- **Q3 + Q4 ON (both low-side):** Slow decay recirculation

## 4.3 Unipolar Drive with Center-Tapped Winding

```mermaid
graph LR
    VCC[DC Supply Vplus] --> SW1[Switch S1]
    VCC --> SW2[Switch S2]
    SW1 --> TAP1[Top Tap of Phase A]
    SW2 --> TAP2[Bottom Tap of Phase A]
    TAP1 --> L1A[Half Winding A1]
    L1A --> CT[Center Tap CT]
    CT --> L1B[Half Winding A2]
    L1B --> TAP2
    CT --> GND[Ground]
    style VCC fill:#ffe1e1
    style GND fill:#e1e1ff
    style SW1 fill:#90EE90
    style SW2 fill:#90EE90
```

**Key Difference from Bipolar:** Only **one switch** per phase is ON at any time. Current flows in only *one direction* through the *half-winding*, so the magnetic polarity is controlled by which half is energized.

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Stepper Motor Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    CMD[Position Command] --> COMP[Comparator Subtractor]
    FB[Encoder Feedback] --> COMP
    COMP --> ERR[Error Signal e t]
    ERR --> PID[PID Controller]
    PID --> DRV[Power Driver H Bridge]
    DRV --> MOT[Stepper Motor Shaft]
    MOT --> LOAD[Mechanical Load]
    MOT -. Shaft.-> ENC[Rotary Encoder]
    ENC --> FB
    style CMD fill:#e1f5ff
    style COMP fill:#fff4e1
    style PID fill:#ffe1ff
    style DRV fill:#ffe1e1
    style MOT fill:#90EE90
    style ENC fill:#FFB6C1
```

## 4.5 Velocity Profiling (Trapezoidal) Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Start Motion Command] --> CALC[Compute Required Steps N and Distance]
    CALC --> DEC1{Is N less than Critical Steps?}
    DEC1 -->|Yes| TRI[Use Triangular Profile]
    DEC1 -->|No| TRAP[Use Trapezoidal Profile]
    TRI --> ACC1[Accelerate at rate a]
    ACC1 --> DEC1B[Decelerate at rate a]
    DEC1B --> STOP[Motor Stops at Target]
    TRAP --> ACC2[Accelerate to Slew Speed fsl]
    ACC2 --> CONST[Run at fsl constant]
    CONST --> DEC2[Decelerate from fsl to zero]
    DEC2 --> STOP
    style START fill:#e1f5ff
    style DEC1 fill:#fff4e1
    style TRI fill:#ffe1ff
    style TRAP fill:#e1ffe1
    style STOP fill:#ffcccc
```

## 4.6 Resonance Avoidance Strategy Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Detect Pulse Frequency f] --> B{Is f in Resonance Band?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Switch to Closed Loop Mode]
    B -->|No| D[Open Loop Acceptable]
    C --> E[Use Microstepping to Dampen]
    E --> F[Add Mechanical Damping]
    D --> G[Normal Open Loop Drive]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#ffe1ff
    style F fill:#ffcccc
    style G fill:#90EE90
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Pull-in and Pull-out torques of a stepper motor. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Pull-out torque** is the maximum torque the motor can deliver at a given stepping rate *while running synchronously* (in the slewing mode). If the load torque exceeds the pull-out torque, the motor stalls (loses synchronism).

**Pull-in torque** is the maximum torque against which the motor can *start from rest* or *stop* (or reverse) at a given pulse rate without losing steps.

The two characteristics divide the torque-speed plane into three regions:
1. **Start-stop region** (below pull-in curve): motor can start/stop/reverse.
2. **Slew region** (between curves): motor can run but cannot start/stop.
3. **Forbidden region** (above pull-out curve): motor stalls.

The pull-in curve is always **lower** than the pull-out curve, and both merge into the holding torque at zero speed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "pull-in is the starting torque" — this is incomplete. The correct definition must specify the *maximum* load torque the motor can *start against* at a *given pulse rate*. Marks are lost for omitting the "given pulse rate" qualifier.

---

### Question 2: Compare Unipolar and Bipolar drives for stepper motors. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Unipolar Drive | Bipolar Drive |
|---|---|---|
| Winding type | Center-tapped (or bifilar) | Single winding (no tap) |
| Switching devices | 1 transistor per phase | 4 transistors per phase (H-bridge) |
| Current direction | One direction only | Both directions (reversed) |
| Copper utilization | Only 50% (one half at a time) | 100% (full winding) |
| Torque per volume | Lower | ~30-40% higher |
| Drive complexity | Simple | Complex (needs H-bridge) |
| Power consumption | Higher losses in unused half | More efficient |
| Typical application | Low-cost printers, toys | CNC, robotics, high-performance |

The bipolar drive produces **higher torque per ampere** because the entire winding carries current, fully utilizing the magnetic circuit. The unipolar drive is preferred when simplicity and cost dominate.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **pull-in and pull-out characteristics** of a stepper motor. Identify the **start-stop region**, **slew region**, and **resonance region** in the diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the phenomenon of **resonance in stepper motors**. What is the natural frequency of a stepper motor? Discuss methods to avoid resonance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Draw the characteristic curves**

Plot **pull-out torque** ($T_{po}$) and **pull-in torque** ($T_{pi}$) on the y-axis and **pulse frequency** ($f$) on the x-axis.

**Step 2: Describe the curves**

- The pull-out torque curve starts at $T_h$ (holding torque) at $f = 0$ and decreases monotonically as $f$ increases, following approximately:
$$T_{po}(f) = \dfrac{T_h}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}$$

- The pull-in torque curve also starts at $T_h$ at $f = 0$ but falls **more steeply** with frequency, terminating at $f_{pi,\max}$ (the maximum pull-in rate, typically 200-1000 steps/s for PM steppers).

**Step 3: Identify the regions**

| Region | Location | What the motor can do |
|---|---|---|
| **Start-Stop Region** | Below pull-in curve | Start, stop, reverse at any point |
| **Slew Region** | Between pull-in and pull-out | Run continuously; cannot start/stop |
| **Forbidden Region** | Above pull-out curve | Loses synchronism (stalls) |
| **Resonance Region** | A narrow band near $f_n$ | Strong oscillations; potential stall |

**Step 4: Sketch description**

```
Torque |
   T_h |  ___________  Pull-out curve
       |  \          \
   T_p |   \  Slew    \  Pull-in curve
       |    \ Region   \
       |     \__________\
       |    /            \
       |   / Start-Stop   \
       |__/________________\______ Pulse rate
         0  f_res  f_pi   f_c
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Define Resonance**

Resonance occurs when the **pulse frequency** of the drive electronics matches the **natural mechanical oscillation frequency** of the rotor-load system. At this frequency, each pulse adds energy to the rotor's natural oscillation, causing the amplitude to grow until the rotor overshoots its intended step position.

**Step 2: Natural Frequency Formula**

$$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{T_h \, N_s}{\pi \, J}}$$

**Step 3: Sub-harmonic Resonance**

Resonance also occurs at sub-multiples $f_n/2$, $f_n/3$, ..., $f_n/k$ for integer $k$. This is the **low-frequency resonance** that occurs when the pulse interval equals an integer multiple of the natural period.

**Step 4: Effects of Resonance**

- Severe vibration and audible noise
- Loss of synchronism (motor stalls)
- Reduced positioning accuracy
- Mechanical wear

**Step 5: Methods to Avoid/Avoid Resonance**

1. **Acceleration/deceleration ramps:** Ramp the pulse frequency through the resonance region so the motor never dwells at $f_n$.
2. **Microstepping:** Subdivides each step into smaller increments, distributing excitation energy over a wider frequency spectrum, effectively damping resonance.
3. **Mechanical damping:** Add viscous damper, friction disk, or rubber coupling to increase $B$ (damping coefficient).
4. **Closed-loop control:** Detect impending stall and adjust pulse rate.
5. **Inertia matching:** Use a rotor/load inertia ratio to shift the natural frequency away from the operating range.
6. **Electronic damping (back-emf feedback):** Sense the back-EMF and adjust phase current to dampen oscillations.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with circuit diagrams the operation of **unipolar and bipolar drive schemes** for a stepper motor. What is a **bifilar drive** and why is it used? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concepts of **open-loop position control**, **starting/stopping rate**, and **velocity profiling** for stepper motors. Why is closed-loop control sometimes necessary? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Unipolar Drive**

A unipolar drive uses a center-tapped winding per phase. The center tap is connected to the DC supply $V+$, and each end of the winding is connected to ground through a switching transistor.

- **Phase A1 ON, A2 OFF:** Current flows in the *top half* of the winding only, magnetizing the stator pole in one direction.
- **Phase A2 ON, A1 OFF:** Current flows in the *bottom half*, reversing the magnetization.
- Only **one switch** ON at a time; the inactive half wastes copper but allows simpler drive electronics.

**Step 2: Bipolar Drive (H-Bridge)**

A bipolar drive uses the *entire* phase winding (no center tap) controlled by an H-bridge of **4 transistors** (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4) per phase.

- **Forward current:** Q1 + Q3 ON → current flows V+ → Q1 → winding top → bottom → Q3 → GND.
- **Reverse current:** Q2 + Q4 ON → current flows V+ → Q2 → winding bottom → top → Q4 → GND.
- This uses 100% of the copper, giving ~30-40% higher torque density.

**Step 3: Bifilar Drive**

A **bifilar-wound** stepper has each phase constructed with **two parallel wires wound together** on the same bobbin. The two strands are connected in series such that they form a *center-tap equivalent* but with much tighter magnetic coupling than a tapped winding.

**Why used?**
- Provides unipolar drive simplicity with bipolar-like coupling.
- The two windings have nearly equal inductance, so the magnetic field is symmetric.
- Lower leakage inductance → better high-speed performance.
- Historically used in early hard disk drives and printers.

**Disadvantage:** The bifilar winding uses only 50% of the slot area for active copper at any instant, similar to a center-tapped winding.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Open-Loop Position Control**

In open-loop control, the controller sends a predetermined **number of pulses** to the stepper driver. Each pulse advances the rotor by **one step angle** $\theta_s = 360°/N_s$. The controller *assumes* the rotor has actually moved by $\theta_s$ per pulse, with no feedback to verify this.

- **Advantages:** Simple, inexpensive, no sensor required.
- **Disadvantage:** If the load torque exceeds the pull-out torque, the motor **stalls** (loses steps) silently — the controller has no way to detect this.

**Step 2: Starting Rate**

The **starting rate** is the maximum pulse rate at which the motor can start from rest (with a given load) without losing synchronism. It is limited by the **pull-in torque curve** at low frequencies.

$$f_{start,\max} = f_{pi,\max} \cdot \sqrt{1 - T_L / T_h}$$

**Step 3: Stopping Rate**

The **stopping rate** is the maximum pulse rate from which the motor can *decelerate to rest* in one step (i.e., the last pulse arrives after the rotor has settled). It is generally **equal to** the starting rate for a symmetric load, but can be lower if the load has high friction or inertia that pushes the rotor forward during deceleration.

**Step 4: Velocity Profiling**

A motion profile is constructed with three phases:
1. **Acceleration:** Pulse frequency increases linearly from $0$ to $f_{ss,\max}$ (start-stop boundary) using acceleration $a \le a_{\max}$.
2. **Constant velocity (slew):** Pulse frequency stays at $f_{sl,\max}$ if the distance is long enough.
3. **Deceleration:** Pulse frequency decreases linearly back to zero, mirror-symmetric with acceleration.

This ensures the motor **never operates in the forbidden region** and **avoids dwelling at resonance frequencies**.

**Step 5: Why Closed-Loop is Sometimes Necessary**

Closed-loop control is required when:
1. **Stall detection is critical** (e.g., CNC mills cutting hard material): a stalled stepper continues to receive pulses and the controller has no way to know.
2. **Variable loads** with unknown disturbance torques.
3. **High accelerations** near the resonance band where open-loop is unstable.
4. **Safety-critical applications** (medical, aerospace).

In closed-loop mode, an **encoder or resolver** measures the actual rotor position; the controller adjusts the drive to minimize the position error. This converts the stepper into a **servo-stepper** (or "closed-loop stepper"), combining the simplicity of open-loop stepping with the robustness of servo control.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Drawing pull-in and pull-out as straight lines (they are curves).
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to mention that pull-in torque = 0 at $f_{pi,\max}$ — beyond this frequency, the motor cannot start at all.
> - **Mistake 3:** Confusing "starting rate" with "maximum slew rate" — they are completely different limits.
> - **Mistake 4:** Claiming resonance is "avoided by using more current" — wrong; resonance is a *mechanical* phenomenon, current cannot eliminate it.
> - **Mistake 5:** In bipolar drive diagrams, students often draw the H-bridge with the wrong switching pairs (current cannot flow from V+ to GND through a short circuit). Always show the diagonal pairs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- ☐ **Pull-out torque** is the upper limit during slewing; **pull-in torque** is the upper limit during start-stop.
- ☐ Pull-in curve is **always below** pull-out curve; both originate at $T_h$ at $f = 0$.
- ☐ The start-stop region is bounded above by the pull-in curve; the slew region is between the two curves.
- ☐ **Cutoff frequency** $f_c = R/(2\pi L)$ is the frequency at which winding reactance equals resistance.
- ☐ **Resonance** occurs at $f_n = (N_s/2\pi)\sqrt{T_h/J}$ and at sub-harmonics $f_n/k$.
- ☐ Resonance causes loss of synchronism; mitigated by acceleration ramps, microstepping, mechanical damping, or closed-loop control.
- ☐ **Unipolar drive:** center-tapped winding, 1 switch per phase, only half the winding used, lower torque density.
- ☐ **Bipolar drive:** H-bridge (4 switches per phase), full winding used, ~30-40% higher torque density.
- ☐ **Bifilar drive:** two parallel strands wound together; gives center-tap equivalent with better magnetic coupling.
- ☐ **Open loop:** no feedback; position assumed equal to commanded pulses; can stall silently.
- ☐ **Closed loop:** encoder feedback; detects and corrects stalls; converts stepper to "servo-stepper."
- ☐ **Starting rate** ≈ **stopping rate** for symmetric loads, both limited by pull-in curve.
- ☐ **Velocity profiling:** trapezoidal (accel + const + decel) or triangular (accel + decel) pulse-frequency trajectory.
- ☐ **Maximum acceleration:** $\alpha_{\max} = (T_{po} - T_L)/J_{eq}$ — the higher the inertia, the slower the ramp.
- ☐ **Microstepping** is the most practical way to mitigate resonance in modern drives.
- ☐ **KTU-favorite formula:** $T_{po} = T_h / \sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}$ — memorize this.
- ☐ **KTU-favorite diagram:** Pull-out and pull-in curves with all three regions labeled — practice drawing.

### One-Line Memory Aids

> *"Pull-in = start hard, pull-out = keep going."*
>
> *"Unipolar = half the copper, bipolar = full copper."*
>
> *"Resonance = swing + push in rhythm = chaos."*
>
> *"Open loop = trust the pulses, closed loop = verify the motion."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
