topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT638 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Speed-Torque Characteristics - Torque Pulsation, Machine Modeling. Switching Schemes for BLDC Operation, Motor Control - Power Controllers - Full Wave and Half Wave Converters - Regeneration - Hall Sensor Based Control, Phase Advance Angle Control, Sensorless Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 BLDC Motor — Speed-Torque Characteristics

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A Brushless DC (BLDC) motor is a synchronous machine with a permanent-magnet rotor and a trapezoidal back-EMF waveform, electronically commutated using a three-phase voltage source inverter. The steady-state **speed-torque characteristic** of a BLDC motor is, in the ideal continuous-conduction region, a straight line described by:

$$\omega_m = \frac{V_s - I_a R_s}{K_e}$$

where $\omega_m$ is the mechanical angular speed, $V_s$ is the DC-link voltage, $I_a$ is the armature current, $R_s$ is the phase resistance, and $K_e$ is the back-EMF constant. Torque is directly proportional to current ($T = K_t I_a$), and the characteristic therefore drops linearly as load torque increases.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Key Point:** The speed-torque curve of a BLDC motor is structurally identical to a separately-excited DC motor, *except* that the field flux $\Phi$ is now produced by permanent magnets (constant). The drop in speed is therefore purely resistive — there is **no field-weakening drop** in the natural characteristic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The constant $K_e$ (V·s/rad) and $K_t$ (N·m/A) are numerically equal in SI units: $K_e = K_t$. This is a frequently tested relation.

### 1.2 Torque Pulsation (Torque Ripple)

**Formal Definition:**
Torque pulsation (or *torque ripple*) is the cyclic, non-uniform variation of instantaneous electromagnetic torque about its mean value, arising from the interaction between non-sinusoidal phase currents and non-ideal back-EMF waveforms, even under balanced DC supply.

$$\Delta T_{\%} = \frac{T_{max} - T_{min}}{T_{avg}} \times 100\%$$

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Bumpy Carousel":**
Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push in long, smooth strokes at exactly the right moment, the swing glides evenly. But if you push in short, choppy bursts at slightly wrong moments, the swing jerks — the speed is roughly the same on average, but the ride feels bumpy. A BLDC motor with ideal sinusoidal current and sinusoidal back-EMF would run "smoothly"; but because BLDCs use **trapezoidal** back-EMF and **rectangular** currents, the push comes in six discrete steps per electrical cycle, producing small torque pulsations at every commutation. This is the "bumpy carousel" of the BLDC drive.

### 1.3 Machine Modeling (Phase Variable Model)

**Formal Definition:**
The **phase-variable model** of a BLDC motor expresses the three-phase stator voltages as coupled differential equations linking resistance, self-inductance, mutual inductance, back-EMF, and rotor speed.

$$\begin{bmatrix} v_a \\ v_b \\ v_c \end{bmatrix} = R \begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} L & M & M \\ M & L & M \\ M & M & L \end{bmatrix} \frac{d}{dt}\begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} e_a \\ e_b \\ e_c \end{bmatrix}$$

### 1.4 Switching Schemes, Power Converters & Control — One-Line Intuition

| Sub-topic | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **120° / 180° switching** | Like tap-dancing vs. ballroom dancing — six crisp steps vs. continuous glide |
| **Full-wave converter** | A two-way valve: current flows in both motoring and braking |
| **Half-wave converter** | A one-way valve: braking energy is dumped as heat, not returned to source |
| **Hall-sensor control** | The bouncer checking your ticket at six different gates |
| **Phase advance** | Knocking earlier on a door so the person inside is ready when you open it |
| **Sensorless control** | Listening to the echo in a room to know where the wall is, instead of touching it |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The commutation frequency in a p-pole-pair, three-phase BLDC is $f_c = p \cdot f_{electrical}$. For a typical 4-pole ($p=2$) BLDC running at 3000 rpm, $f_{electrical} = 100$ Hz and $f_c = 200$ Hz.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Speed-Torque Character — Why is it Linear?

The DC-link of the inverter presents a stiff voltage $V_s$ to two phases in series during each 60° electrical interval. Applying KVL to the conducting pair (say, phases A+ and B−):

$$V_s = 2R_s I_a + (L-M)\frac{dI_a}{dt} + (e_a - e_b)$$

In steady state, $\frac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$ and the back-EMF of a flat-topped trapezoidal wave gives $e_a - e_b = 2K_e \omega_m$ during the constant region. Therefore:

$$V_s = 2R_s I_a + 2K_e \omega_m$$

Solving for $\omega_m$ yields the **linear no-field-weakening** characteristic. There is **no slope change** at the base speed — BLDC drives, unlike DC shunt motors, do not exhibit an armature-reaction-induced field distortion (because the field is a permanent magnet).

### 2.2 Sources of Torque Pulsation

1. **Commutation Torque Ripple (dominant):** Occurs during the 60° overlap when current in the outgoing phase has not decayed to zero before the incoming phase current has risen. Caused by phase inductance $L_s = L - M$.
2. **Cogging Torque:** Interaction of stator slotting with rotor magnets — slotless BLDCs eliminate this.
3. **Non-ideal Trapezoidal Back-EMF:** Manufacturing imperfections create deviations from the ideal flat-top.
4. **Current Ripple from PWM:** High-frequency switching harmonics modulate the average current.

The **ripple frequency** of commutation torque is six times the electrical frequency:

$$f_{ripple} = 6 f_e = 6 p f_m \quad [\text{Hz}]$$

### 2.3 Switching Schemes

**Scheme A — 120° Conduction (Trapezoidal / Six-Step):**
- Each phase conducts for **120°** electrical per cycle.
- Two switches (one high-side, one low-side from *different* legs) are ON at any instant.
- Six distinct switching states per electrical revolution → commutation at every 60°.
- Best suited for trapezoidal back-EMF BLDCs (the most common configuration).

**Scheme B — 180° Conduction (Sinusoidal / Sine-Drive):**
- Each phase is fed a **sinusoidal** current.
- All three legs are continuously switching; PWM duty cycles follow $\sin(\theta)$, $\sin(\theta - 120°)$, $\sin(\theta + 120°)$.
- Used with **PMSM** (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor, sinusoidal back-EMF) rather than BLDC, but listed here for completeness.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | $T_e = K_t I_a$ | Electromagnetic torque, [N·m] |
| 2 | $e = K_e \omega_m$ | Back-EMF, [V] |
| 3 | $K_e = K_t$ | Equality in SI units |
| 4 | $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_s - 2R_s I_a}{2K_e}$ | Ideal speed-torque, [rad/s] |
| 5 | $T_{ripple,\%} = \dfrac{T_{max}-T_{min}}{T_{avg}} \times 100$ | Torque pulsation index |
| 6 | $f_{ripple} = 6 p f_m$ | Commutation ripple frequency [Hz] |
| 7 | $L_s = L - M$ | Synchronous (leakage) inductance, [H] |
| 8 | $V_s = 2R_s I_a + 2L_s \dfrac{dI_a}{dt} + 2K_e\omega_m$ | Phase-pair KVL |
| 9 | $t_{com} \approx \dfrac{3 L_s I_a}{V_s - 2K_e\omega_m}$ | Commutation overlap time [s] |
| 10 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2}J\omega_m^{2}$ | Stored kinetic energy [J] |
| 11 | $P_{regen} = T_{brake}\cdot\omega_m$ | Regenerative power returned [W] |
| 12 | $V_{com,adv} = K_e \omega_m \cos(\alpha_{adv})$ | Back-EMF after phase advance |
| 13 | $e_{thd} = K_e\omega_m \sin(3\theta)$ | 3rd-harmonic back-EMF component |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Conventions:** $K_e$ is in V/(rad/s); $K_t$ in N·m/A. Speed $\omega_m$ is in mechanical rad/s. Electrical angle $\theta_e = p \cdot \theta_m$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Electric vehicles (EVs):** BLDC speed-torque linearity enables simple torque-control loops; phase-advance allows high-speed cruising on highways.
- **Drones / Quadcopters:** Sensorless control with back-EMF sensing removes fragile Hall sensors from high-vibration environments.
- **Industrial servo pumps and HVAC blowers:** Half-wave converters trade efficiency for cost in low-power applications.
- **Robotics:** Phase advance at high speeds prevents commutation-time-induced torque collapse, keeping the manipulator responsive.
- **Disk drives and medical infusion pumps:** Sensorless control enables miniaturization (no bulky position sensor).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the BLDC Speed-Torque Equation

**Starting point — Kirchhoff's Voltage Law for the conducting phase pair (A-high, B-low):**

$$V_s = R_s i_a + L_s \frac{di_a}{dt} + e_a - (R_s i_b + L_s \frac{di_b}{dt} + e_b)$$

**Step 1 — Use the constraint that exactly two phases conduct at any instant, in series, with opposite current polarity.**

For 120° conduction, $i_b = -i_a$ and $i_c = 0$ during this 60° step. Therefore:

$$V_s = 2 R_s i_a + 2 L_s \frac{di_a}{dt} + (e_a - e_b)$$

**Step 2 — Assume steady-state (current has settled, ripple-free):**

$$\frac{di_a}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 3 — In the flat-top region of the trapezoidal back-EMF, the difference is constant and equal to $2K_e\omega_m$:**

$$e_a - e_b = K_e \omega_m - (-K_e \omega_m) = 2 K_e \omega_m$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and rearrange:**

$$V_s = 2 R_s I_a + 2 K_e \omega_m$$

$$\omega_m = \frac{V_s - 2 R_s I_a}{2 K_e}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the torque relation $T_e = K_t I_a = K_e I_a$ (in SI units):**

$$\omega_m = \frac{V_s}{2 K_e} - \frac{R_s}{K_e^{2}} \, T_e$$

This is the canonical **straight-line speed-torque equation**. The y-intercept (no-load speed) is $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V_s}{2K_e}$ and the slope is $-\dfrac{R_s}{K_e^2}$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Commutation Overlap Time $t_{com}$

During a phase commutation, three phases carry current (two incoming, one outgoing). Summing the three phase equations and using $i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$ gives the classical overlap result:

$$t_{com} = \frac{L_s \, I_a}{V_s/2 - K_e \omega_m}$$

When the term $V_s/2 - K_e\omega_m$ approaches zero (i.e., back-EMF nearly equals half the DC-link), commutation fails — this is the **"demagnetization speed limit"** of trapezoidal control and is the fundamental reason **phase advance** is required at high speed.

### 3.3 Derivation of Torque Ripple Percentage

For an ideal 120° conduction BLDC with ideal rectangular current and trapezoidal back-EMF, the theoretical minimum ripple is:

$$\Delta T_{\%} = \frac{T_{max} - T_{min}}{T_{avg}} \times 100\% \approx \frac{1 - \cos(30°)}{1 + \cos(30°)} \times 100\% \approx 7.2\%$$

This residual 7.2% ripple is unavoidable in pure 120° trapezoidal drive and is a direct consequence of the 30° non-conducting zone in the trapezoidal back-EMF.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Six Commutation States

| Step | Conducting Switches | Active Phases | Hall Pattern (a,b,c) | $\theta_e$ range |
|------|---------------------|---------------|----------------------|------------------|
| 1 | T1, T6 | A+, B− | 1 0 1 | 0° – 60° |
| 2 | T1, T2 | A+, C− | 1 0 0 | 60° – 120° |
| 3 | T3, T2 | B+, C− | 0 1 0 | 120° – 180° |
| 4 | T3, T4 | B+, A− | 0 1 1 | 180° – 240° |
| 5 | T5, T4 | C+, A− | 0 0 1 | 240° – 300° |
| 6 | T5, T6 | C+, B− | 1 0 0 *(revisit)* | 300° – 360° |

> [!NOTE]
> The Hall pattern sequence repeats every 360° electrical, but is **unique** to each 60° sector. This six-bit Gray-code-like sequence is the cornerstone of both **Hall-sensor control** and **sensorless back-EMF zero-crossing** schemes.

### 3.5 Half-Wave vs. Full-Wave Converter — Power-Flow Analysis

**Full-Wave (Bidirectional / Four-Quadrant):**
- All six switches (T1–T6) of a standard 3-phase IGBT bridge are available.
- Motoring: T1,T2,T3,T4,T5,T6 switch as per the 120° pattern above.
- Regeneration: same bridge, switches in complementary pairs; current flows from the motor back into the DC-link capacitor and the source (with a step-up chopper if the source is a battery at lower voltage than the link).
- **Quadrants:** All four (forward motoring, forward braking, reverse motoring, reverse braking).

**Half-Wave (Unidirectional / Low-Cost):**
- Only the three high-side switches (T1, T3, T5) are used. The low-side return path is through the **neutral point** of the motor (Y-connected, neutral brought out).
- The other three phases are diode-clamped.
- Current flows in only **one direction** through the DC-link → regeneration is impossible; braking torque is dissipative (rheostatic / dynamic braking on a resistor across the DC link).
- **Quadrants:** Two (forward motoring, forward braking) only. Reverse rotation requires swapping two phase leads.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *Half-wave = "Half the switches, half the quadrants, half the cost, half the efficiency at low speed."* It is used in fans, pumps, and cost-sensitive appliances where 4-quadrant operation is not required.

### 3.6 Phase Advance Angle Control

The **phase advance angle** $\alpha_{adv}$ is the angle by which the commutation instant is **advanced** (i.e., fired earlier) relative to the back-EMF zero-crossing, to compensate for the finite commutation time $t_{com}$.

$$\alpha_{adv} = \omega_e \, t_{com} = \omega_e \cdot \frac{L_s I_a}{V_s/2 - K_e\omega_m \cos(\alpha_{adv})}$$

As speed increases, $\alpha_{adv}$ increases (typically 0° at start, up to 30° at high speed). This:
1. Shifts the current waveform *earlier* so the current has time to build up in the incoming phase.
2. Partially counters the back-EMF opposing the commutation, raising the high-speed torque ceiling.
3. Operates as a **poor man's field-weakening** — at extreme speeds the advance can exceed 30°, putting current in opposition to back-EMF and effectively weakening the equivalent "field".

### 3.7 Sensorless Control — Back-EMF Zero-Crossing Method

When no Hall sensor is present, the rotor position is inferred from the **back-EMF** induced in the floating (un-energized) phase:

$$e_{floating} = v_{phase} - R_s i_{phase} - L_s \frac{di_{phase}}{dt}$$

The zero-crossing of $e_{floating}$ occurs exactly 30° after the previous commutation and 30° before the next. The controller therefore schedules the next commutation 30° (electrical) after the zero-cross. At very low speed ($e_{floating} \to 0$), this method fails — a typical sensorless drive needs a minimum speed of about **5–10%** of rated to start reliably.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BLDC Drive — Top-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[DC Supply V_s] --> node2[DC Link Capacitor]
    node2 --> node3[Three-Phase VSI Bridge T1-T6]
    node3 --> node4[BLDC Motor 3-Phase Stator]
    node4 --> node5[Hall Sensors 120 deg apart]
    node4 --> node6[Rotor PM Flux]
    node5 --> node7[Hall Decoder Logic]
    node7 --> node8[Commutation Sequencer]
    node9[Phase Advance Calculator] --> node8
    node8 --> node10[PWM Generator]
    node10 --> node3
    node11[Speed / Torque Reference] --> node12[PI Controller]
    node12 --> node10
    node4 -.Back-EMF Feedback.-> node13[Sensorless Estimator]
    node13 -.Alt Position.-> node8
```

### 4.2 Six-Step Commutation State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State1
    State1 --> State2 : Hall 101
    State2 --> State3 : Hall 100
    State3 --> State4 : Hall 110
    State4 --> State5 : Hall 010
    State5 --> State6 : Hall 011
    State6 --> State1 : Hall 001
    State1 : Step 1 - T1 T6 ON
    State2 : Step 2 - T1 T2 ON
    State3 : Step 3 - T3 T2 ON
    State4 : Step 4 - T3 T4 ON
    State5 : Step 5 - T5 T4 ON
    State6 : Step 6 - T5 T6 ON
```

### 4.3 Power-Flow Comparison — Full-Wave vs. Half-Wave

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[DC Source] --> nodeB{Full Wave 4Q Bridge}
    nodeB -->|Motoring| nodeC[Motor - Forward]
    nodeB -->|Regen| nodeD[DC Source - Charging]
    nodeE[DC Source] --> nodeF[Half Wave Switch T1 T3 T5]
    nodeF -->|Motoring| nodeG[Motor via Neutral]
    nodeF -->|Braking| nodeH[Brake Resistor Heat Loss]
```

### 4.4 Sensorless Control — Signal-Flow Architecture

```mermaid

    nodeA[Phase Voltages v_a v_b v_c] --> nodeB[Subtract iR and L di/dt]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Estimated Back EMF]

    nodeD --> nodeE[30 deg Delay Timer]

    nodeF --> nodeG[Inverter Gate Signals]
    nodeG --> nodeA
```

```mermaid

    node1[Hall A] --> node4[Decoder LUT 3 to 6]
    node2[Hall B] --> node4
    node3[Hall C] --> node4
    node4 --> node5[T1 T2 T3 T4 T5 T6]
    node5 --> node6[Inverter]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define torque pulsation in a BLDC motor. List any two causes.*

**Model Answer:**
Torque pulsation is the cyclic variation of instantaneous electromagnetic torque about its average value, expressed as a percentage. Two main causes are:
1. **Commutation torque ripple** — produced during the 60° phase-current transfer between the outgoing and incoming switches, when the rising and falling currents do not perfectly cancel.
2. **Cogging torque** — produced by the interaction of stator slot openings with the permanent-magnet rotor flux.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the relation between the back-EMF constant $K_e$ and torque constant $K_t$ in SI units and justify.*

**Model Answer:**
In SI units, $K_e = K_t$ (numerically). This follows from power conservation: electrical input power $e \cdot i$ must equal mechanical output power $T \cdot \omega$ for a lossless machine, so $K_e \omega \cdot i = K_t i \cdot \omega$, hence $K_e = K_t$.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state speed-torque equation of a three-phase BLDC motor under 120° trapezoidal conduction. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole, 3-phase BLDC motor has $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad, $R_s = 0.5$ $\Omega$, and is supplied from a 48 V DC link. Compute (i) the no-load speed, (ii) the speed at a load torque of 2 N·m, and (iii) the torque pulsation frequency at 2000 rpm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Apply KVL to the active phase pair (A+, B−) during any 60° step:
$$V_s = 2R_s I_a + 2L_s \frac{dI_a}{dt} + (e_a - e_b)$$

Step 2: Assumptions:
- Steady-state, so $\frac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$.
- Flat-topped trapezoidal back-EMF, so $e_a - e_b = 2K_e\omega_m$.
- Two phases in series carry equal and opposite currents; the third is floating.
- Magnetic linearity, no saturation.

Step 3: Substitute to obtain:
$$V_s = 2R_s I_a + 2K_e\omega_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_m = \frac{V_s - 2R_s I_a}{2K_e}$$

Step 4: Using $T_e = K_t I_a = K_e I_a$:
$$\boxed{\omega_m = \frac{V_s}{2K_e} - \frac{R_s}{K_e^2}\,T_e}$$

This is a straight line with slope $-R_s/K_e^2$ and no-load speed $\omega_0 = V_s/(2K_e)$.

**(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad, $R_s = 0.5$ $\Omega$, $V_s = 48$ V, poles $P = 4$ → $p = 2$.

(i) **No-load speed:**
$$\omega_0 = \frac{48}{2 \times 0.05} = 480 \text{ rad/s} = \frac{480 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 4584 \text{ rpm}$$

(ii) **Speed at $T_L = 2$ N·m:**
$$\omega = \frac{48 - 2 \times 0.5 \times \frac{2}{0.05}}{2 \times 0.05}$$

$$I_a = \frac{T_L}{K_t} = \frac{2}{0.05} = 40 \text{ A}$$

$$\omega = \frac{48 - 2(0.5)(40)}{2(0.05)} = \frac{48 - 40}{0.1} = 80 \text{ rad/s} \approx 764 \text{ rpm}$$

(iii) **Torque ripple frequency at 2000 rpm:**
$$f_m = 2000/60 = 33.33 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_e = p \cdot f_m = 66.67 \text{ Hz}$$
$$f_{ripple} = 6 f_e = 6 \times 66.67 \approx 400 \text{ Hz}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain Hall-sensor-based commutation of a BLDC motor with a suitable logical truth table. Why is the Hall pattern a Gray-code-like sequence? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a half-wave converter for BLDC speed control. Why is regeneration not possible in this scheme? Suggest one modification to enable regeneration. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Hall-sensor commutation (7 marks):**

Three Hall sensors are placed 120° electrical apart on the stator. Each produces a binary output (1 if south pole faces, 0 if north pole faces). Together, they generate a 3-bit code that uniquely identifies each 60° sector.

| Hall A | Hall B | Hall C | Active Switches | Energized Phase Pair |
|:------:|:------:|:------:|:---------------:|:--------------------:|
| 1 | 0 | 1 | T1, T6 | A+ B− |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | T1, T2 | A+ C− |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | T3, T2 | B+ C− |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | T3, T4 | B+ A− |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | T5, T4 | C+ A− |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | T5, T6 | C+ B− |

**Why Gray-code-like:** The pattern changes by **only one bit at a time** between adjacent sectors (e.g., 101 → 100 → 110 → 010 → 011 → 001 → 101). This guarantees that no two-bit error can ever cause a *short-circuit firing* (shoot-through of a leg), which would otherwise destroy the inverter. The Hamming distance between any two consecutive codes is exactly 1.

**(b) — Half-wave converter (7 marks):**

In a half-wave scheme, only the three high-side switches T1, T3, T5 (one per phase) are IGBTs; the three low-side devices are replaced by **diodes** (D4, D6, D2). The motor's neutral point is brought out and tied to the negative rail of the DC link.

- **Motoring:** T1 fires during sector 1, current flows from V+ → T1 → Phase A → motor windings → neutral → D6 (or D2) → V−. T3 fires during sector 3, etc. The current always returns through a diode, never a controlled switch.
- **Speed control:** Achieved by PWM chopping of the active high-side switch; the average voltage $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ sets the speed.
- **Why no regeneration:** During braking, the motor acts as a generator, and current would need to flow *from the motor into the DC source*. But the diodes in the return path are unidirectional — they conduct only in the *motoring* direction. Furthermore, with neutral tied to V−, the generated EMF cannot push current *up* into the positive rail. Braking energy is therefore dissipated in a brake resistor placed across the DC link via a chopper switch.
- **Modification for regeneration:** Replace the three low-side diodes with **active IGBTs** (T2, T4, T6) to form a full six-switch bridge, and add a **step-up (boost) chopper** between the DC link and the battery so that the link voltage can exceed the battery voltage during regen.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the speed equation ($V_s$ drops across *two* series phases, not one). This single error costs full marks in numericals.
> 2. **Writing $K_e \neq K_t$ in SI units** — they are equal; do not mix CGS or FPS conventions.
> 3. **Confusing commutation ripple frequency** ($6f_e$) with cogging ripple frequency (which depends on slot/pole combination).
> 4. **Drawing half-wave with the wrong return path** — students often draw the low-side return through the DC-link negative directly, forgetting that the **motor neutral** must be brought out and tied to the negative rail. This is a structural error.
> 5. **Sensorless control at zero/low speed** — examiners will deduct marks if you claim sensorless works from standstill. Always state the minimum speed threshold (≈ 5–10% of rated).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- BLDC speed-torque equation: $\omega_m = \dfrac{V_s}{2K_e} - \dfrac{R_s}{K_e^2}T_e$ — **linear, no field-weakening drop**.
- Torque ripple frequency: $f_{ripple} = 6 p f_m$ — **six times electrical frequency**.
- Torque pulsation sources: **commutation ripple, cogging, non-ideal back-EMF, PWM ripple**.
- Phase-variable model has $R$, $L$, $M$ matrix; **synchronous inductance** $L_s = L - M$ governs dynamic behavior.
- **120° trapezoidal switching** = two switches ON, six steps per electrical cycle.
- **Full-wave converter** = 6-switch bridge, four-quadrant, regeneration-capable.
- **Half-wave converter** = 3 high-side switches + 3 diodes + neutral tied to DC−, **no regeneration**, two-quadrant.
- **Hall-sensor code** = 3-bit Gray-code, **1-bit change per step** (anti shoot-through).
- **Phase advance angle** $\alpha_{adv}$ = firing earlier to overcome $L_s \cdot di/dt$ at high speed; equivalent to field-weakening at extreme advance.
- **Sensorless back-EMF method** = detect zero-crossing of floating-phase EMF, **fails at very low speed** (typically < 10% rated).
- $K_e = K_t$ in SI units; **never** use $|x|$ — write $\vert x \vert$ in tables.
- Theoretical minimum torque ripple in ideal 120° trapezoidal drive ≈ **7.2%**.
- **Regeneration** is required for: EVs (range extension), elevators (energy saving), CNC spindles (quick stop).
- Phase advance raises the **base speed** (no-load speed ceiling) by effectively reducing the back-EMF opposing commutation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SRM - Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT638 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SRM - Principle of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** is a doubly salient, singly excited, variable reluctance electric machine in which **torque is produced purely by the tendency of the movable ferromagnetic rotor to align with the excited stator phase so as to minimize the magnetic circuit reluctance and maximize inductance**. The SRM operates on the fundamental principle that whenever a magnetic field is established in a magnetic circuit, the mechanical work done in moving the rotor toward a position of minimum reluctance gives rise to electromagnetic torque, expressed universally as:

$$T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta, i)}{d\theta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT638 Module 3):**
> The SRM is categorized as a "Special Electrical Machine" because it lacks both rotor windings and permanent magnets. The torque is purely **reluctance torque** — no rotor excitation current is required, which is its single most distinguishing feature.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **saloon door with a strong spring** that always wants to snap shut (position of minimum reluctance). Now, if you place a **horseshoe magnet** on one side and a **ferromagnetic iron piece** on the other, as soon as the magnet is energized, the iron piece will be *violently pulled* toward the magnet to close the air-gap. The energy released by the spring when the door closes is exactly the mechanical energy produced in an SRM.

**Saloon Door ↔ Rotor poles (move to align with stator field)**
**Energized Magnet ↔ Excited Stator Phase**
**Spring Restoring Force ↔ Magnetic Reluctance Force**

If you now place **four saloon doors in a circle** and fire the magnets in sequence (door 1, then door 2, then door 3, then door 4), each door is yanked in turn, producing **continuous rotary motion** — this is precisely how an SRM works.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants and Standards:**
> - **Air permeability (μ₀)** = **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
> - **Stack length (L_stk)** typically ranges from **50 mm to 250 mm** in industrial SRMs
> - **Rotor pole pitch (τᵣ)** = **2π/Nᵣ** radians (where Nᵣ = number of rotor poles)
> - **Step angle** = **360° / (Nₛ · Nᵣ)** where Nₛ = number of stator poles
> - **Current density in stator windings** ≈ **4 to 8 A/mm²** (continuous rating)

### 1.3 Classification of SRM

| Feature | SRM Characteristic |
|---|---|
| Number of excitations | Singly excited (stator only) |
| Construction | Doubly salient (teeth on both stator and rotor) |
| Rotor type | No windings, no magnets — solid/laminated steel |
| Commutation | Electronic (rotor position sensor required) |
| Torque type | Pure reluctance torque |
| Operating speeds | **500 rpm to 30,000 rpm** (wide constant-power range) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Features of the SRM

The SRM is built with the following physical characteristics:

1. **Stator**: Made of laminated silicon steel. Carries **concentrated phase windings** wound on diametrically opposite poles. Number of stator poles **Nₛ** is always even (commonly 6 or 8).
2. **Rotor**: Also made of laminated silicon steel. Carries **no windings, no permanent magnets, no cage bars**. Rotor poles **Nᵣ < Nₛ** (commonly 4 or 6).
3. **Phase windings**: Each phase consists of two coils on diametrically opposite stator poles, connected in series (or parallel) such that when energized, they produce a magnetic field along that diameter.
4. **Power electronic converter**: A unipolar asymmetric half-bridge converter (one per phase) is mandatory since the SRM requires sequential DC pulses to each phase.
5. **Rotor position sensor**: An optical encoder or Hall-effect sensor provides the angular position feedback essential for proper commutation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Popular SRM Configurations:**
> - 6/4 SRM (6 stator poles, 4 rotor poles) — step angle = 30°
> - 8/6 SRM (8 stator poles, 6 rotor poles) — step angle = 15°
> - 10/8 SRM, 12/10 SRM — used in very high-torque applications

### 2.2 Operating Principle — The Physics of Reluctance

When a stator phase is energized with DC current, it produces a magnetic field that links the rotor through the **air gap**. The rotor will always experience a force (or torque) that tries to **reduce the air-gap reluctance to its minimum possible value**, which is mathematically equivalent to maximizing the inductance of the excited coil.

**Why does the rotor move toward alignment?**

Because **magnetic flux lines always seek the path of least reluctance** (analogous to current seeking the path of least resistance). The rotor, being a high-permeability ferromagnetic body, will physically rotate to align its pole with the energized stator pole — thereby minimizing the air-gap length along the flux path and minimizing magnetic circuit reluctance.

**Mathematical Statement of the Principle:**

For a magnetic circuit with energy stored $W = \frac{1}{2} L i^2$, the mechanical work done by the rotor as it moves by an angle $d\theta$ equals the change in co-energy:

$$\delta W_{mech} = T_e \, d\theta = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta} \bigg|_{i = \text{const}} d\theta$$

This gives the universal reluctance torque expression.

### 2.3 Inductance Profile of an SRM Phase

The phase inductance $L(\theta)$ is the most fundamental characteristic of an SRM. As the rotor rotates from the **fully unaligned position** to the **fully aligned position**, the inductance rises from its minimum value to its maximum value in a characteristic pattern.

| Rotor Position | Air Gap | Inductance | Reluctance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fully unaligned | Maximum | **L_min** | Maximum |
| Intermediate (partial overlap) | Variable | **L_min ≤ L(θ) ≤ L_max** | Variable |
| Fully aligned | Minimum | **L_max** | Minimum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Torque is produced **only when dL/dθ ≠ 0** (the rising or falling slope of the inductance profile). When the rotor is at exactly the aligned or unaligned position, the rotor poles are stationary in their state of maximum/minimum reluctance, so **dL/dθ = 0 → Tₑ = 0**.

### 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Instantaneous electromagnetic torque (linear region) | N·m |
| 2 | $T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}(\theta, i)}{\partial \theta}\bigg\vert_i$ | Torque from co-energy derivative (general) | N·m |
| 3 | $W_{co} = \int_{0}^{i} \psi(\theta, i) \, di$ | Co-energy definition | Joules |
| 4 | $\psi(\theta, i) = L(\theta, i) \cdot i$ | Flux linkage (linear case) | Weber |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{2\pi}{q \cdot N_r}$ | Stator pole pitch (mechanical) | rad |
| 6 | $\alpha_s = \dfrac{2\pi}{q \cdot N_r}$ | Stator pole arc angle (mechanical) | rad |
| 7 | $\alpha_r = \dfrac{2\pi}{N_r} - \beta_r$ | Rotor pole arc angle | rad |
| 8 | $\Delta \theta = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$ | Step angle (full step) | degrees |
| 9 | $L_{min} = \dfrac{\mu_0 N_{ph}^2 A_{gap}}{2 l_g}$ | Unaligned inductance (approx.) | Henry |
| 10 | $L_{max} = \dfrac{\mu_0 N_{ph}^2 A_{pole}}{2 l_g + \frac{l_{iron}}{\mu_r}}$ | Aligned inductance (approx.) | Henry |
| 11 | $n_{sync} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{N_r}$ | Base (synchronous) speed | rpm |
| 12 | $L(\theta) = L_{min} + (L_{max} - L_{min}) \cdot f(\theta)$ | Generic ideal inductance profile | Henry |
| 13 | $P_{gap} = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \omega \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Air-gap power converted | Watts |
| 14 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{cu} + P_{iron} + P_{mech}}$ | Efficiency | % |
| 15 | $f_{phase} = \dfrac{N_r \cdot n}{60}$ | Phase commutation frequency | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Convention Used:**
> - $N_s$ = number of stator poles
> - $N_r$ = number of rotor poles
> - $q$ = number of stator poles per phase
> - $N_{ph}$ = turns per phase
> - $l_g$ = air-gap length
> - $\beta_r$ = rotor pole arc (mechanical)
> - $\mu_0$ = **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
> - $\mu_r$ = relative permeability of iron (≈ 2000–5000)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Why SRM is Used |
|---|---|
| **Electric vehicles (EVs)** | Wide constant-power speed range, fault-tolerant (phases are independent), no rare-earth magnets |
| **Aerospace actuators** | High reliability — operates even with one phase failed; high-speed capability up to **30,000 rpm** |
| **Domestic appliances** (washing machines, vacuum cleaners) | Low cost, robust rotor, simple variable-speed drive |
| **Mining & oil drilling** | Operates in harsh environments — no rotor windings to fail |
| **High-speed spindle drives** | Compact rotor with no magnets = mechanically robust at extreme speeds |

The SRM is increasingly considered the **"post-rare-earth" electric machine** since it does not require neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB) permanent magnets, making it geopolitically and economically attractive.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reluctance Torque Equation

This is the single most important derivation for KTU exams on SRM. The following exhaustive step-by-step derivation builds the torque equation from first principles using the principle of conservation of energy.

**Starting Premise:** The magnetic system stores energy in the air gap, and the rotor experiences a force/torque as it moves to a position of minimum reluctance.

**Step 1 — Establish the magnetic co-energy expression.**

For a singly-excited magnetic system with current $i$ and flux linkage $\psi$, the magnetic co-energy is defined as the area to the left of the $\psi$–$i$ magnetization curve (between the curve and the vertical current axis):

$$W'_{co} = \int_{0}^{i} \psi(\theta, i) \, di$$

**Step 2 — Apply the principle of virtual work.**

According to the principle of conservation of energy, when the rotor moves by an infinitesimal angle $d\theta$ at constant current $i$, the mechanical work done by the electromagnetic torque equals the change in co-energy of the magnetic field:

$$T_e \, d\theta = dW'_{co}\bigg\vert_{i = \text{constant}}$$

**Step 3 — Take the partial derivative with respect to θ at constant i.**

Since co-energy is a function of two variables $(\theta, i)$, the change in co-energy at constant current is given by the partial derivative:

$$T_e \, d\theta = \frac{\partial W'_{co}(\theta, i)}{\partial \theta} \bigg\vert_{i} d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Cancel dθ from both sides.**

$$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}(\theta, i)}{\partial \theta} \bigg\vert_{i}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the linear (unsaturated) flux linkage assumption.**

In the linear (unsaturated) magnetic region, the flux linkage is directly proportional to the current:

$$\psi(\theta, i) = L(\theta) \cdot i$$

Substitute this into the co-energy expression:

$$W'_{co} = \int_{0}^{i} L(\theta) \cdot i \, di = L(\theta) \int_{0}^{i} i \, di = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate the co-energy with respect to θ.**

$$\frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \left[ \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2 \right] = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

**Step 7 — Equate to electromagnetic torque.**

$$\boxed{T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}}$$

**Step 8 — Physical interpretation of the sign.**

| Condition | Sign of $dL/d\theta$ | Sign of $T_e$ | Physical Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rotor moving toward alignment (inductance rising) | $+$ | $+$ | **Motoring torque** — accelerates rotor |
| Rotor moving away from alignment (inductance falling) | $-$ | $-$ | **Generating/braking torque** — opposes motion |
| Fully aligned or fully unaligned | $0$ | $0$ | **Zero torque** — rotor coasts freely |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $i^2$ and not $i$?**
> Notice that the torque expression contains $i^2$, NOT $i$. This means the torque direction depends **only on the sign of dL/dθ**, not on the direction of current. This is a profound and unique property of reluctance machines — **unidirectional current produces bidirectional torque**, which is why the SRM can use a simple unipolar asymmetric half-bridge converter.

### 3.2 Derivation of Air-Gap Power Conversion

The instantaneous power being converted from electrical to mechanical form at the air gap is given by:

$$P_{gap}(t) = e(t) \cdot i(t)$$

where the back-EMF induced in the phase winding is:

$$e(t) = \frac{d\psi}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}[L(\theta) \cdot i] = L(\theta) \frac{di}{dt} + i \frac{dL(\theta)}{dt}$$

The first term $L(\theta)\frac{di}{dt}$ is the **transformer EMF** (stored/returned to source through commutation). The second term $i \frac{dL}{dt}$ is the **motional EMF** — the one responsible for mechanical power conversion:

$$P_{mech}(t) = i(t) \cdot i(t) \cdot \frac{dL(\theta)}{dt} = i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{dt}$$

Using the chain rule $\frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{dL}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega \frac{dL}{d\theta}$:

$$P_{mech}(t) = \omega \cdot i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} = \omega \cdot T_e$$

This confirms the energy-consistency check: $P_{mech} = T_e \cdot \omega$.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Step Angle for a General SRM

**Step 1 — Define the rotor pole pitch.**

The rotor pole pitch (mechanical) is the angular distance between two consecutive rotor poles:

$$\tau_r = \frac{2\pi}{N_r}$$

**Step 2 — Each stator phase advances the rotor by one rotor pole pitch per stroke.**

When a stator phase is energized and then de-energized, the rotor moves by exactly one rotor pole pitch before the next phase is energized. So the per-stroke advance equals $\tau_r$.

**Step 3 — Count the total number of strokes per mechanical revolution.**

In one complete revolution, the rotor must pass $N_s$ stator pole pairs (since each stator phase is energized $N_s/2$ times per revolution… actually $N_s$ times per revolution when all phases are excited in sequence covering every pole pair). The exact count is:

$$\text{Strokes per revolution} = N_s \cdot N_r$$

**Step 4 — Compute the step angle.**

$$\alpha_{step} = \frac{2\pi}{N_s \cdot N_r} = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot N_r}$$

**Worked Example (KTU Exam Style):**

For an **8/6 SRM** (8 stator poles, 6 rotor poles):
$$\alpha_{step} = \frac{360°}{8 \times 6} = \frac{360°}{48} = 7.5°$$

For a **6/4 SRM**:
$$\alpha_{step} = \frac{360°}{6 \times 4} = \frac{360°}{24} = 15°$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall to Remember:** The step angle is **NOT** simply $360°/N_r$ — many students forget to multiply by $N_s$. The numerator must be $N_s \cdot N_r$ (full revolution in electrical cycles).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Torque Production at Different Rotor Positions

| Rotor Position | Inductance $L(\theta)$ | Slope $dL/d\theta$ | Current $i$ | Torque $T_e$ | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fully unaligned | $L_{min}$ | 0 | Any | 0 | No torque |
| Rising inductance (motoring) | Increasing | $> 0$ | Excitation ON | $T_e > 0$ | Motoring |
| Peak aligned | $L_{max}$ | 0 | Should be OFF | 0 | Free coasting |
| Falling inductance (generating) | Decreasing | $< 0$ | Residual current | $T_e < 0$ | Braking/generating |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** A 6/4 SRM has the following parameters: $L_{min} = 10$ mH, $L_{max} = 110$ mH, and the inductance rises linearly from unaligned to aligned over a rotor rotation of $45°$ (mechanical). If the phase current is held constant at $i = 8$ A during the rising inductance region, calculate:

(a) The average electromagnetic torque.
(b) The instantaneous torque at the midpoint of the rising region.
(c) The air-gap power when the rotor is rotating at 1500 rpm.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Average Torque**

The rate of change of inductance over the rising region:
$$\frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{\Delta\theta} = \frac{110 \times 10^{-3} - 10 \times 10^{-3}}{45° \times \frac{\pi}{180°}}$$

Convert angle to radians:
$$\Delta\theta = 45° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = \frac{\pi}{4} \text{ rad} = 0.7854 \text{ rad}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{0.100}{0.7854} = 0.1273 \text{ H/rad}$$

Average torque:
$$T_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{1}{2} \times 8^2 \times 0.1273 = 4.07 \text{ N·m}$$

**Part (b): Instantaneous Torque at Midpoint**

At the midpoint, the inductance is rising with the same slope, and the current is constant at 8 A, so:
$$T_{instant} = T_{avg} = 4.07 \text{ N·m}$$

(The torque is the same at every point in the rising region since both $i$ and $dL/d\theta$ are constant.)

**Part (c): Air-Gap Power at 1500 rpm**

Convert speed to rad/s:
$$\omega = 1500 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

Air-gap power:
$$P_{gap} = T_{avg} \times \omega = 4.07 \times 157.08 = 639.3 \text{ W}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a 6/4 SRM (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STATOR[STATOR ASSEMBLY - 6 Poles]
        S1[Stator Pole A1 - Top]
        S2[Stator Pole A2 - Bottom]
        S3[Stator Pole B1 - Top Right]
        S4[Stator Pole B2 - Bottom Left]
        S5[Stator Pole C1 - Top Left]
        S6[Stator Pole C2 - Bottom Right]
        COILA[Phase A Winding: A1 plus A2]
        COILB[Phase B Winding: B1 plus B2]
        COILC[Phase C Winding: C1 plus C2]
    end

    subgraph ROTOR[ROTOR ASSEMBLY - 4 Poles Solid Iron]
        R1[Rotor Pole R1 - Right]
        R2[Rotor Pole R2 - Left]
        R3[Rotor Pole R3 - Top]
        R4[Rotor Pole R4 - Bottom]
        SHAFT[Non Magnetic Shaft]
    end

    subgraph AIRGAP[Air Gap Region]
        AG1[Top Air Gap - Min when R3 aligned with A1]
        AG2[Bottom Air Gap - Min when R4 aligned with A2]
        AG3[Side Air Gap - Max in unaligned]
    end

    COILA -.energizes.-> S1
    COILA -.energizes.-> S2
    COILB -.energizes.-> S3
    COILB -.energizes.-> S4
    COILC -.energizes.-> S5
    COILC -.energizes.-> S6

    S1 --> AG1
    S2 --> AG2
    S3 --> AG3
    R1 --- SHAFT
    R2 --- SHAFT
    R3 --- SHAFT
    R4 --- SHAFT

    PS[Position Sensor Hall Effect or Optical Encoder] -.feedback.-> CTRL
    CTRL[Controller Unit Microcontroller or DSP] -.gate signals.-> CONV
    CONV[Asymmetric Half Bridge Converter 3 Phases] -.DC pulses.-> COILA
    CONV -.DC pulses.-> COILB
    CONV -.DC pulses.-> COILC
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Complete SRM Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC[DC Bus Supply Battery or Rectifier] --> CONV[Asymmetric Half Bridge Power Converter]
    CONV --> PHASEA[Phase A Stator Coil]
    CONV --> PHASEB[Phase B Stator Coil]
    CONV --> PHASEC[Phase C Stator Coil]
    PHASEA --> AIRGAP1[Air Gap Magnetic Field]
    PHASEB --> AIRGAP2[Air Gap Magnetic Field]
    PHASEC --> AIRGAP3[Air Gap Magnetic Field]
    AIRGAP1 --> ROTOR[Rotor With 4 Salient Poles]
    AIRGAP2 --> ROTOR
    AIRGAP3 --> ROTOR
    ROTOR --> SHAFT[Mechanical Shaft Output]
    ROTOR -.shaft mounted.-> POS[Position Sensor Encoder]
    POS --> CTRL[DSP or Microcontroller]
    CTRL -.turn on.-> SW1[Switch T1 Phase A]
    CTRL -.turn on.-> SW2[Switch T2 Phase B]
    CTRL -.turn on.-> SW3[Switch T3 Phase C]
    SW1 --> CONV
    SW2 --> CONV
    SW3 --> CONV
    CTRL --> DISP[Display and Protection Module]
```

### 4.3 Inductance vs Rotor Angle Profile (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROFILE[Idealized Inductance Profile L versus Theta]
        A1[At Theta 0: Fully Unaligned - L min 10 mH] --> A2[At Theta 15 deg: Rising Slope - dL over dTheta positive]
        A2 --> A3[At Theta 22.5 deg: Midpoint - L equals 60 mH]
        A3 --> A4[At Theta 30 deg: Fully Aligned - L max 110 mH]
        A4 --> A5[At Theta 45 deg: Constant L max Plateau]
        A5 --> A6[At Theta 60 deg: Falling Slope - dL over dTheta negative]
        A6 --> A7[At Theta 90 deg: Fully Unaligned Again - L min]
    end
```

### 4.4 Energy Conversion Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([DC Bus Energized]) --> ENERGIZE[Energize Phase A Stator Poles]
    ENERGIZE --> FIELDA[Magnetic Flux Established Along Vertical Axis]
    FIELDA --> ATTRACT[Rotor Pole 3 Pulled Toward Stator Pole A1]
    ATTRACT --> ROTATE1[Rotor Rotates by Step Angle 7.5 deg]
    ROTATE1 --> DEENERGIZE[Phase A Switched OFF]
    DEENERGIZE --> ENERGIZEB[Phase B Energized after Delay]
    ENERGIZEB --> FIELDB[Flux Along Diagonal Axis]
    FIELDB --> ATTRACTB[Rotor Pole 1 Pulled Toward Stator Pole B1]
    ATTRACTB --> ROTATE2[Rotor Rotates Another 7.5 deg]
    ROTATE2 --> CYCLE{Repeat for Phase C then Loop Back to A}
    CYCLE -->|Yes| ENERGIZE
    CYCLE -->|No| STOP([Motor Stopped])
```
