## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define a Switched Reluctance Motor. Why is it called a "doubly salient, singly excited" machine?**

**Model Answer:**
A Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) is a rotating electric machine in which torque is produced by the tendency of the ferromagnetic rotor to move to a position of minimum reluctance when a stator phase is energized.

It is called **doubly salient** because salient poles (teeth) project inward from both the stator and the rotor. It is called **singly excited** because the rotor carries no windings or magnets — excitation is provided **only by the stator windings**, which must be supplied with sequential DC pulses from an external power electronic converter. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] State the torque equation of an SRM. Explain why the torque depends on $i^2$ and not on $i$.**

**Model Answer:**
The instantaneous electromagnetic torque in an SRM is given by:
$$T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

The torque depends on $i^2$ because the energy stored in a magnetic circuit is proportional to $L \cdot i^2$, and the co-energy derivative with respect to rotor position yields a quadratic dependence on current. The consequence is that the sign of the torque depends only on the sign of $dL/d\theta$ (whether the rotor is moving toward or away from alignment), not on the direction of current. This is why **unidirectional current pulses are sufficient** to drive the SRM, and a unipolar converter can be used. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and principle of operation of a Switched Reluctance Motor with the help of a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the power electronic converter and the rotor position sensor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque in an SRM from first principles using the co-energy method. A 6/4 SRM has $L_{min} = 8$ mH and $L_{max} = 100$ mH, with linear rise in inductance over 30° mechanical. The phase carries a constant current of 10 A during the rising inductance region. Calculate: (i) the average electromagnetic torque, and (ii) the air-gap power at 1800 rpm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Stator**: Laminated silicon steel core with 6 inward-projecting salient poles. Concentrated windings on each pole. Each phase consists of two diametrically opposite coils (e.g., Phase A: poles A1 and A2). For 3-phase operation, phases are spatially offset by 60°.
- **Rotor**: Laminated silicon steel with 4 salient poles. **No windings, no permanent magnets, no squirrel cage** — it is a passive ferromagnetic body mounted on a non-magnetic shaft.
- **Air gap**: Non-uniform — varies as rotor rotates.

**Principle of Operation (3 marks):**
When a stator phase (e.g., Phase A) is energized by a DC pulse, it produces a magnetic field. The rotor experiences a force and rotates to align its pole with the excited stator pole, thereby **minimizing the magnetic circuit reluctance**. Once aligned, the controller de-energizes Phase A and energizes Phase B. The rotor advances by one step angle (15° for 6/4) and the process repeats, producing continuous rotary motion.

**Converter and Sensor (1 mark):**
An **asymmetric half-bridge converter** (one per phase) supplies unidirectional current pulses. A **rotor position sensor** (encoder or Hall sensors) provides real-time angular feedback to the controller, which determines the correct commutation instants.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Co-energy of a magnetic system with linear $L(\theta)$:

$$W'_{co} = \int_0^i L(\theta) \cdot i \, di = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2$$

By the principle of virtual work at constant current:

$$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta} \bigg\vert_i = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

Given: $L_{min} = 8$ mH, $L_{max} = 100$ mH, $\Delta\theta = 30° = \pi/6$ rad, $i = 10$ A, $n = 1800$ rpm.

Rate of change of inductance:
$$\frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{(100 - 8) \times 10^{-3}}{\pi/6} = \frac{0.092}{0.5236} = 0.1757 \text{ H/rad}$$

(i) Average torque:
$$T_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \times 10^2 \times 0.1757 = 8.785 \text{ N·m}$$

(ii) Angular speed:
$$\omega = 1800 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 188.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

Air-gap power:
$$P_{gap} = T_{avg} \times \omega = 8.785 \times 188.5 = 1655.4 \text{ W} \approx 1.66 \text{ kW}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Supplementary 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the construction of an 8/6 SRM. Compute its step angle and state the sequence of phase excitation for continuous rotation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the static and dynamic characteristics of an SRM. Explain the role of the inductance profile in determining the motoring and generating zones. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
An 8/6 SRM has 8 stator poles and 6 rotor poles. The stator has 4 phases (A, B, C, D), with each phase consisting of two diametrically opposite coils. Each stator pole arc is typically around 20°–22°, and each rotor pole arc is around 23°–25°. There are **no rotor windings**.

**Step Angle (2 marks):**
$$\alpha_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_s \times N_r} = \frac{360°}{8 \times 6} = \frac{360°}{48} = 7.5°$$

**Phase Excitation Sequence (2 marks):**
For continuous forward rotation, the phases are energized in the order A → B → C → D → A, with each phase energized for an electrical angle of 360°/Nᵣ = 60°. The rotor advances 7.5° per stroke and completes one mechanical revolution in 48 strokes. For reverse rotation, the sequence is reversed: A → D → C → B → A.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Static Characteristics (3 marks):**
The static torque vs rotor angle characteristic shows:
- **Motoring zone**: When the rotor is approaching alignment, $dL/d\theta > 0$, so the torque is positive (motoring) and pulls the rotor toward alignment.
- **Generating zone**: When the rotor is moving away from alignment, $dL/d\theta < 0$, so the torque becomes negative (regenerative braking).
- **Zero torque points**: At the fully aligned ($\theta_a$) and fully unaligned ($\theta_u$) positions, $dL/d\theta = 0$ and torque is zero.

**Dynamic Characteristics (2 marks):**
At higher speeds, the rate of change of inductance is high, and the back-EMF opposes the applied voltage, reducing the current. To maintain torque at high speeds, **firing angle advance** is used — the phase is energized earlier (before the unaligned position) so that sufficient current builds up by the motoring region.

**Inductance Profile (2 marks):**
The inductance profile is the heart of the SRM. Its rising slope corresponds to motoring, its falling slope to generating, and the slopes of the profile determine the torque per ampere. A machine with a steeper rising slope produces more torque for the same current, while a flat top region (constant $L_{max}$) does not contribute to torque production.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in the slope calculation: $dL/d\theta$ requires $\theta$ in **radians**, not degrees. A common error is using $45°$ directly, which gives a torque 57 times too small.
> 2. **Confusing mechanical and electrical angles**: The step angle is a **mechanical** angle. The electrical angle per stroke is $360°/N_r$.
> 3. **Sign of torque**: Students often forget that torque is **positive (motoring)** on the **rising** slope of inductance, not on the falling slope. Generating torque requires the falling slope.
> 4. **Step angle formula**: A frequent mistake is using $360°/N_r$ instead of $360°/(N_s N_r)$. The correct formula is $\alpha_{step} = 360° / (N_s N_r)$.
> 5. **Confusing SRM with stepper motor**: Although structurally similar, the SRM uses **rotor position feedback** for commutation, while a stepper motor runs open-loop. This distinction is a common KTU question.
> 6. **Number of power electronic switches**: For an m-phase SRM, the asymmetric half-bridge converter requires **2m switches and 2m diodes**, not just m switches.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] An **SRM is doubly salient, singly excited** — salient poles on both stator and rotor; only the stator carries windings.
- [x] Torque equation (most important): $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$
- [x] Torque depends on **$i^2$**, so the current direction does not determine torque direction — only the sign of $dL/d\theta$ does.
- [x] Torque is **zero at aligned and unaligned** positions because $dL/d\theta = 0$ at those points.
- [x] **Step angle**: $\alpha_{step} = \dfrac{360°}{N_s N_r}$
- [x] Popular configurations: **6/4 (15°)**, **8/6 (7.5°)**, **10/8 (4.5°)**.
- [x] **Air permeability constant**: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
- [x] SRM requires a **rotor position sensor** (encoder/Hall) and an **asymmetric half-bridge converter** (2 switches + 2 diodes per phase).
- [x] **Advantages**: no rotor windings, no rare-earth magnets, high fault tolerance (phases are magnetically and electrically independent), wide constant-power speed range (up to **30,000 rpm**).
- [x] **Disadvantages**: high torque ripple, acoustic noise, requires position sensor, more complex control than BLDC/PMSM.
- [x] Inductance profile rises from $L_{min}$ (unaligned) to $L_{max}$ (aligned).
- [x] **Motoring**: energize phase on **rising** inductance slope; **Generating**: residual current on **falling** slope.
- [x] Synchronous speed: $n_{sync} = \dfrac{60 f}{N_r}$ (where $f$ is the switching frequency).
- [x] Applications: **electric vehicles, aerospace, mining, high-speed spindles, domestic appliances**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductance Profile
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT638 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inductance Profile

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Inductance Profile** of a phase in a **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** is the functional relationship between the phase inductance $L(\theta, i)$ and the rotor angular position $\theta$ (and sometimes the phase current $i$). It is a periodic, position-dependent function whose shape governs the entire energy-conversion mechanism of the machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Inductance Profile** $L(\theta)$ is defined as the variation of phase inductance of a Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) with respect to the rotor angular position. It is the **fundamental characteristic** that determines torque production, commutation instants, and energy conversion in variable reluctance machines.

The profile is a **doubly salient** function (salient stator AND salient rotor), bounded by two critical positions:

- **Aligned Position (θ_a):** The rotor pole is exactly in line with the stator pole, giving **maximum inductance $L_{max}$** (minimum reluctance path).
- **Unaligned Position (θ_u):** The rotor pole axis coincides with the stator slot axis, giving **minimum inductance $L_{min}$** (maximum reluctance path).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Magnet and the Iron Ball"**
> Imagine pushing a soft iron ball between the poles of an electromagnet. When the ball is **far** from the magnet (unaligned), you feel almost **no magnetic pull**, and the system "magnetic energy" is low. As the ball rolls **toward** the magnet (rising inductance), the magnetic pull grows rapidly. When the ball is **right in the gap** (aligned), the magnetic pull is at its **maximum** — the magnetic circuit "locks" the ball in place.
> The **inductance profile** is simply the graph of "how much magnetic pull exists" as a function of the ball's angular position. The motor converts this changing magnetic pull into **mechanical torque**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Stroke Angle (θ_st):** The angular displacement over which the inductance rises from $L_{min}$ to $L_{max}$. Typical range: **30° to 45° (mechanical)**.
- **Pole Arcs (β_s, β_r):** Stator and rotor pole arcs (in degrees).
- **L_max / L_min Ratio:** Typically between **6:1 and 10:1** for modern SRMs. This ratio is the **single most important design metric** for torque density.

> [!TIP]
> A high $L_{max}/L_{min}$ ratio ⇒ Higher torque ripple BUT also higher average torque. Designers balance this trade-off carefully.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — "Why and How" Step-by-Step

The inductance profile in an SRM is governed by the **overlap geometry** between stator and rotor poles. The behaviour unfolds in three logical regions per electrical cycle:

**Step 1 — Unaligned Region (θ_u to θ_1):**
- The rotor pole completely faces the stator slot. Flux path has **maximum reluctance**.
- Inductance remains nearly constant at $L_{min}$.
- A small constant flux can be established, but **no useful torque** is produced.

**Step 2 — Rising Inductance Region (θ_1 to θ_2):**
- The rotor pole begins to overlap the stator pole. The **air-gap area increases**, reluctance drops, and inductance rises monotonically from $L_{min}$ to $L_{max}$.
- If a phase is **excited with current in this region**, a positive **motoring torque** is produced.
- This is the **energy conversion region**.

**Step 3 — Aligned Region (θ_2 onwards):**
- The rotor pole is fully under the stator pole. Inductance saturates at $L_{max}$.
- Even with current, **no torque** is produced (since $dL/d\theta = 0$).

**Step 4 — Falling Inductance Region:**
- If current is injected during the **falling slope** of $L(\theta)$, **negative (braking/generating) torque** is produced. This is the principle of **regenerative braking** in SRMs.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorize This Table**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $L(\theta) = \dfrac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}(\theta)}$ | Definition of inductance via reluctance | Henry (H) |
| 2 | $\theta_{st} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\beta_s + \beta_r)$ | Stroke angle (linear approximation) | rad / deg |
| 3 | $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Instantaneous electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 4 | $W'_{co}(\theta, i) = \int_0^{i} L(\theta, i')\, i'\, di'$ | Co-energy (magnetic) | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $T_e = \dfrac{\partial W'_{co}(\theta, i)}{\partial \theta}\Big\vert_{i=\text{const}}$ | Torque from co-energy | N·m |
| 6 | $L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta - \pi)$ | Fourier approximation (1st harmonic) | H |
| 7 | $L_0 = \dfrac{L_{max} + L_{min}}{2}$ | Average inductance | H |
| 8 | $L_1 = \dfrac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{2}$ | Inductance oscillation amplitude | H |
| 9 | $\Delta L = L_{max} - L_{min}$ | Inductance swing (design metric) | H |
| 10 | $\eta_{SRM} \approx 0.5\,\dfrac{\Delta L}{L_{max} + L_{min}}$ | Torque per ampere metric | — |

> **Note on Units:** Angles must be in **radians** when used inside trigonometric functions for torque calculations; degrees are acceptable for angular position descriptions.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The inductance profile is the **design cornerstone** of every SRM application:

- **EV Traction Drives:** Profile shape determines ripple, acoustic noise, and torque density.
- **Aerospace Fuel Pumps:** Linearized $L(\theta)$ enables sensorless rotor position estimation.
- **High-speed Industrial Spindles:** Accurate $L(\theta)$ is required for optimal firing angle selection.
- **Home Appliances (Vacuum Cleaners, Washers):** Pre-computed $L(\theta)$ look-up tables are embedded in microcontroller firmware.

### 2.4 Mathematical Description (Fourier Series Form)

Because $L(\theta)$ is periodic with electrical period $\theta_e = 2\pi / N_r$ (where $N_r$ is the number of rotor poles), it can be expanded as a Fourier series:

$$L(\theta) = L_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} L_n \cos\!\left( n N_r \theta + \phi_n \right)$$

For most engineering analysis (and KTU problem-solving), the **first-harmonic approximation** suffices:

$$L(\theta) = L_0 - L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$$

with the coefficients given by equations (7) and (8) above.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Torque Equation from Inductance Profile

**Goal:** Derive the instantaneous electromagnetic torque $T_e$ produced by one phase of an SRM, starting from first principles using the principle of **co-energy**.

**Step 1 — Co-energy definition.** The magnetic co-energy of a single phase is the integral of flux-linkage with respect to current:

$$W'_{co}(\theta, i) = \int_0^{i} \lambda(\theta, i')\, di'$$

**Step 2 — Flux-linkage relation.** By definition of inductance, $\lambda(\theta, i) = L(\theta, i) \cdot i$. Substituting:

$$W'_{co}(\theta, i) = \int_0^{i} L(\theta, i')\, i'\, di'$$

**Step 3 — Torque from co-energy (principle of virtual work).** In a current-controlled system, the generalized force (torque) equals the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to displacement at constant current:

$$T_e(\theta, i) = \frac{\partial W'_{co}(\theta, i)}{\partial \theta}\bigg\vert_{i=\text{const}}$$

**Step 4 — Leibnitz rule applied.** Differentiating under the integral sign:

$$T_e = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \int_0^{i} L(\theta, i')\, i'\, di' = \int_0^{i} i'\, \frac{\partial L(\theta, i')}{\partial \theta}\, di'$$

**Step 5 — Linear magnetic assumption.** For a **linear** (unsaturated) magnetic circuit, $L$ is independent of $i$, so $\partial L/\partial \theta$ exits the integral:

$$T_e = \frac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta} \int_0^{i} i'\, di'$$

**Step 6 — Final torque expression.** Evaluating the elementary integral $\int_0^i i'\, di' = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2$:

$$\boxed{\; T_e(\theta, i) = \frac{1}{2}\, i^2\, \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} \;}$$

**Step 7 — Physical interpretation.** Since $i^2 \geq 0$ always, the sign of torque depends *entirely* on the sign of $dL/d\theta$:
- $dL/d\theta > 0$ (rising slope, excitation ON) → **Motoring torque**.
- $dL/d\theta < 0$ (falling slope, excitation ON) → **Generating / braking torque**.
- $dL/d\theta = 0$ (plateau, max or min) → **Zero torque**, regardless of current magnitude.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: First-Harmonic Fourier Approximation of $L(\theta)$

**Goal:** Show that the first-harmonic Fourier model $L(\theta) = L_0 - L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$ reproduces the correct values of $L_{min}$ and $L_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Maximum inductance check.** The cosine achieves its minimum at $N_r \theta = 0$, so:

$$L(0) = L_0 - L_1 (1) = L_0 - L_1$$

Setting this equal to $L_{min}$:

$$L_0 - L_1 = L_{min}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum inductance check.** The cosine achieves its maximum at $N_r \theta = \pi$, so:

$$L(\pi/N_r) = L_0 - L_1 (-1) = L_0 + L_1$$

Setting this equal to $L_{max}$:

$$L_0 + L_1 = L_{max}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the 2 × 2 linear system.** Adding the two equations:

$$2 L_0 = L_{max} + L_{min} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad L_0 = \frac{L_{max} + L_{min}}{2}$$

Subtracting:

$$2 L_1 = L_{max} - L_{min} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad L_1 = \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back.** The complete first-harmonic model is therefore:

$$\boxed{\; L(\theta) = \frac{L_{max} + L_{min}}{2} - \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{2}\, \cos(N_r \theta) \;}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Average Torque Over a Stroke

**Goal:** Compute the average motoring torque developed by one phase carrying a constant current $I$ over a stroke angle $\theta_{st}$, assuming a linear ramp in the rising-inductance region.

**Step 1 — Inductance model in the rising region.** Linear ramp from $L_{min}$ to $L_{max}$:

$$L(\theta) = L_{min} + \frac{\Delta L}{\theta_{st}}(\theta - \theta_1)$$

where $\Delta L = L_{max} - L_{min}$.

**Step 2 — Slope of inductance:**

$$\frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{\Delta L}{\theta_{st}} \quad \text{(constant over the stroke)}$$

**Step 3 — Instantaneous torque:**

$$T_e(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} I^2 \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{\theta_{st}}$$

**Step 4 — Average torque** (constant, so the average equals the value):

$$\boxed{\; T_{avg} = \frac{I^2\, \Delta L}{2\, \theta_{st}} \;}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check.** Take $I = 10\,\text{A}$, $\Delta L = 50\,\text{mH}$, $\theta_{st} = \pi/6\,\text{rad}$ (≈ 30°):

$$T_{avg} = \frac{(10)^2 \times 0.050}{2 \times (\pi/6)} = \frac{5.0}{1.047} \approx 4.78\ \text{N·m}$$

This value is realistic for a small fractional-kW SRM.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy Conversion Per Stroke

**Step 1 — Input electrical energy** (per stroke, constant current $I$, with phase switched ON at $\theta_1$ and OFF at $\theta_2 = \theta_1 + \theta_{st}$):

$$W_{in} = \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} e\, i\, d\theta = \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \frac{d\lambda}{d\theta}\, i\, d\theta = \int_{\lambda_1}^{\lambda_2} i\, d\lambda$$

**Step 2 — With $i = I$ constant and $\lambda = L(\theta)\, I$:**

$$W_{in} = I \int_{\lambda_1}^{\lambda_2} d\lambda = I (\lambda_2 - \lambda_1) = I^2 \Delta L$$

**Step 3 — Output mechanical energy per stroke** = average torque × stroke angle:

$$W_{out} = T_{avg}\, \theta_{st} = \frac{I^2 \Delta L}{2 \theta_{st}} \cdot \theta_{st} = \frac{I^2 \Delta L}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Energy conversion ratio:**

$$\boxed{\; \eta_{conv} = \frac{W_{out}}{W_{in}} = \frac{1}{2} \;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Under ideal linear (unsaturated) conditions and constant-current excitation, exactly **50 %** of the input electrical energy is converted to mechanical work per stroke. The remaining 50 % must be returned to the source via the demagnetization (freewheeling) stage of the converter — a unique feature of SRM drives.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Inductance Profile Generation and Use in SRM Control

```mermaid
graph TD
    RotorPos[Rotor Position Sensor] --> LUT[L theta Lookup Table]
    CurrentSensor[Phase Current Sensor] --> LUT
    LUT[Inductance Profile L theta] --> Derivative[dL by dtheta Calculator]
    Derivative --> TorqueEq[Te equals half i squared times dL by dtheta]
    CurrentSensor --> TorqueEq
    TorqueEq --> Controller[SRM Torque Controller]
    Controller --> Converter[Asymmetric H Bridge Converter]
    Converter --> PhaseWinding[Stator Phase Winding]
    PhaseWinding --> AirGapFlux[Variable Air Gap Reluctance Path]
    AirGapFlux --> RotorPos
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Regions of the Inductance Profile

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Unaligned Position Lmin Region] -->|Rotor Pole Approaches Stator Pole| B[Rising Inductance Slope dL by dtheta greater than 0]
    B -->|Maximum Overlap Achieved| C[Aligned Position Lmax Plateau]
    C -->|Rotor Pole Moves Away| D[Falling Inductance Slope dL by dtheta less than 0]
    D -->|Minimum Overlap Restored| A
    B -->|Excite Phase Here| E[Motoring Torque Positive]
    D -->|Excite Phase Here| F[Generating or Braking Torque Negative]
    C -->|No Torque Produced| G[Commutation Zone Phase OFF]
```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Logical / Block Form)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph StatorPole [Stator Pole with Phase Winding]
        W[Phase Winding carrying current i]
    end
    subgraph AirGap [Variable Air Gap Reluctance R theta]
        Agap[Reluctance varies with rotor angle]
    end
    subgraph RotorPole [Salient Rotor Pole]
        Rp[Rotates at angular position theta]
    end
    W --> Agap
    Agap --> Rp
    Rp -->|Mechanical Output| Shaft[Torque Te on Shaft]
    W -->|Flux lambda equals L theta times i| Flux[Flux Linkage lambda]
    Flux -->|Co-energy Wco| Energy[Energy Conversion Stage]
    Energy --> Shaft
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Block Diagram Instead of a Physical Sketch?**
> A true 8/6 or 6/4 SRM cross-section requires precise geometric proportions that Mermaid cannot faithfully reproduce. The block-level functional architecture shown above captures the *causal chain* — current → flux → variable reluctance → torque — which is what KTU examiners typically test.

### 4.4 Analytical Matrix — Regions vs. Action

| Region | $\theta$ range | $L(\theta)$ behaviour | $dL/d\theta$ | Excitation | Resulting Torque |
|--------|---------------|------------------------|--------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Unaligned | $\theta_u$ to $\theta_1$ | Constant $= L_{min}$ | $0$ | Avoid | Zero |
| Rising | $\theta_1$ to $\theta_2$ | Linear / curved rise | $> 0$ | **ON** | **Motoring (+)** |
| Aligned | $\theta_2$ onwards | Constant $= L_{max}$ | $0$ | OFF | Zero |
| Falling | $\theta_2$ to $\theta_3$ | Linear / curved fall | $< 0$ | ON (rare) | Generating (−) |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define the term "Inductance Profile" of a Switched Reluctance Motor. What are the two extreme values of inductance, and at what rotor positions do they occur?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Inductance Profile** $L(\theta)$ of a Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) is the graphical/functional representation of the variation of the phase inductance with the rotor angular position $\theta$.
- The **maximum inductance $L_{max}$** occurs at the **aligned position**, where the rotor pole axis coincides with the stator pole axis (minimum air-gap reluctance).
- The **minimum inductance $L_{min}$** occurs at the **unaligned position**, where the rotor pole axis coincides with the stator slot axis (maximum air-gap reluctance).

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Scheme (3 M):** Definition (1 M) + $L_{max}$ and aligned (1 M) + $L_{min}$ and unaligned (1 M).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the torque equation of an SRM in terms of the inductance profile. From it, deduce the sign of torque in (i) the rising-inductance region and (ii) the aligned plateau.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The instantaneous electromagnetic torque per phase is:

$$T_e = \frac{1}{2}\, i^2\, \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

(i) **Rising-inductance region:** $dL/d\theta > 0$, hence $T_e > 0$ → **Positive (motoring) torque**.

(ii) **Aligned plateau:** $dL/d\theta = 0$, hence $T_e = 0$ → **Zero torque**, regardless of phase current magnitude.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Derive, from first principles, the torque equation $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2\, dL/d\theta$ for a phase of a Switched Reluctance Motor using the principle of co-energy. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8/6 SRM has $L_{max} = 200\,\text{mH}$ and $L_{min} = 20\,\text{mH}$. Assuming a first-harmonic Fourier model, determine:
- (i) The average inductance $L_0$ and the oscillation amplitude $L_1$.
- (ii) The peak instantaneous torque at a phase current of $15\,\text{A}$.
- (iii) The rotor position (electrical, in degrees) at which this peak torque occurs. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

Starting from the co-energy definition for a single phase:

$$W'_{co}(\theta, i) = \int_0^{i} \lambda(\theta, i')\, di' = \int_0^{i} L(\theta, i')\, i'\, di'$$

Apply the principle of virtual work (current-held constant):

$$T_e = \left.\frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i}$$

Using Leibniz's rule:

$$T_e = \int_0^{i} i'\, \frac{\partial L(\theta, i')}{\partial \theta}\, di'$$

**Assumption — Linear (unsaturated) magnetic circuit:** $L(\theta, i) = L(\theta)$ is independent of $i$. Thus $\partial L/\partial \theta$ is independent of the integration variable and can be factored out:

$$T_e = \frac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta} \int_0^{i} i'\, di' = \frac{1}{2}\, i^2\, \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

**Assumption — Magnetic linearity and rotor-position-only dependence of L.** This is sufficient for KTU-level analysis.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $L_{max} = 200\,\text{mH} = 0.20\,\text{H}$, $L_{min} = 20\,\text{mH} = 0.02\,\text{H}$, $I = 15\,\text{A}$, $N_r = 6$ rotor poles.

**(i) Fourier coefficients (2 marks):**

$$L_0 = \frac{L_{max} + L_{min}}{2} = \frac{0.20 + 0.02}{2} = 0.11\,\text{H} = 110\,\text{mH}$$

$$L_1 = \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{2} = \frac{0.20 - 0.02}{2} = 0.09\,\text{H} = 90\,\text{mH}$$

**(ii) Peak instantaneous torque (3 marks):**

The Fourier model gives $dL/d\theta = N_r\, L_1 \sin(N_r \theta)$. Its maximum magnitude is $N_r\, L_1$:

$$\left|\frac{dL}{d\theta}\right|_{max} = N_r\, L_1 = 6 \times 0.09 = 0.54\,\text{H/rad}$$

$$T_{peak} = \frac{1}{2}\, I^2\, \left|\frac{dL}{d\theta}\right|_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \times (15)^2 \times 0.54$$

$$T_{peak} = 0.5 \times 225 \times 0.54 = 60.75\,\text{N·m}$$

**(iii) Position of peak torque (2 marks):**

Peak $|dL/d\theta|$ occurs when $\sin(N_r \theta) = \pm 1$, i.e. $N_r \theta = \pi/2$:

$$\theta_{peak} = \frac{\pi}{2 N_r} = \frac{\pi}{12}\,\text{rad} = 15^{\circ}\,\text{(electrical)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> A common student error is using $L_0$ or $L_1$ in the torque formula directly. The **correct derivative** of $L(\theta) = L_0 - L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$ is $dL/d\theta = N_r L_1 \sin(N_r \theta)$. The factor $N_r$ is the **most-skipped term** in board exams and costs 2–3 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of a clearly labelled sketch of the inductance profile $L(\theta)$ for one phase of an SRM, identify and describe the **aligned, unaligned, and rising-inductance regions**. Explain why the rotor pole arc and stator pole arc together determine the stroke angle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6/4 SRM phase is energized with a constant current of $12\,\text{A}$ over a stroke angle of $30^{\circ}$ (mechanical). The inductance varies linearly from $L_{min} = 15\,\text{mH}$ to $L_{max} = 120\,\text{mH}$ during the stroke. Compute:
- (i) The average electromagnetic torque developed by the phase.
- (ii) The mechanical energy converted per stroke.
- (iii) The energy conversion efficiency per stroke (assuming constant-current excitation). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Qualitative Description (7 marks)

The inductance profile of one SRM phase is a periodic, trapezoidal-shaped curve. The salient features are:

- **Unaligned position (Region I):** The rotor pole is in the stator slot; inductance stays at $L_{min}$, constant over a small angle. No torque can be produced because $dL/d\theta = 0$.

- **Rising-inductance region (Region II):** As the rotor pole edge overlaps the stator pole edge, the air-gap area available for flux grows; reluctance falls and inductance rises sharply from $L_{min}$ to $L_{max}$ over the **stroke angle $\theta_{st}$**. This is the **motoring zone**.

- **Aligned plateau (Region III):** Once full overlap is achieved, inductance saturates at $L_{max}$. Further rotation produces $dL/d\theta = 0$, hence no torque — the phase is commutated OFF here.

- **Falling region (Region IV):** Mirror of Region II; produces generating torque if excited.

**Why pole arcs determine stroke angle (3 marks):**
The rotor pole must traverse a total angular displacement equal to the **sum of the stator and rotor pole arcs** (in mechanical degrees) to move from full unalignment to full alignment:

$$\theta_{st} = \frac{\beta_s + \beta_r}{2}$$

(approx. for short-pitched designs). Hence $\beta_s$ and $\beta_r$ are the **geometric determinants** of the stroke angle and therefore of the torque capability.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $I = 12\,\text{A}$, $\theta_{st} = 30^{\circ} = \pi/6\,\text{rad}$, $L_{min} = 15\,\text{mH}$, $L_{max} = 120\,\text{mH}$.

$$\Delta L = L_{max} - L_{min} = 120 - 15 = 105\,\text{mH} = 0.105\,\text{H}$$

**(i) Average torque (3 marks):**

$$T_{avg} = \frac{I^2\, \Delta L}{2\, \theta_{st}} = \frac{(12)^2 \times 0.105}{2 \times (\pi/6)} = \frac{144 \times 0.105}{1.0472}$$

$$T_{avg} = \frac{15.12}{1.0472} \approx 14.44\,\text{N·m}$$

**(ii) Mechanical energy per stroke (2 marks):**

$$W_{out} = T_{avg} \times \theta_{st} = 14.44 \times \frac{\pi}{6} = 14.44 \times 0.5236 \approx 7.56\,\text{J}$$

Alternatively, using $W_{out} = \tfrac{1}{2} I^2 \Delta L$:

$$W_{out} = 0.5 \times 144 \times 0.105 = 7.56\,\text{J} \quad \checkmark$$

**(iii) Energy conversion efficiency (2 marks):**

$$W_{in} = I^2 \Delta L = 144 \times 0.105 = 15.12\,\text{J}$$

$$\eta = \frac{W_{out}}{W_{in}} = \frac{7.56}{15.12} = 0.50 = 50\,\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting radian conversion:** $\theta_{st} = 30^{\circ}$ MUST be converted to **radians** ($\pi/6$) before plugging into $T_{avg}$. Using degrees directly gives a result that is off by a factor of 57.3 — a guaranteed zero on that sub-part.
> 2. **Confusing stroke and pole pitch:** Stroke angle is *not* $360^{\circ}/N_r$. It is the geometric overlap angle, NOT the full electrical cycle.
> 3. **Saturated vs. linear assumption:** KTU expects the linear-model answer unless saturation data is provided. Don't pull in $L(i)$ curves unprompted.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Inductance Profile (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Definition:** $L(\theta)$ — variation of phase inductance with rotor position; doubly salient periodic function.
- ✅ **Two extreme points:** $L_{max}$ at **aligned** position; $L_{min}$ at **unaligned** position.
- ✅ **Design metric:** $L_{max}/L_{min}$ ratio (typically 6:1 to 10:1) governs torque density.
- ✅ **Stroke angle:** $\theta_{st} \approx (\beta_s + \beta_r)/2$; defines the motoring region.
- ✅ **Core torque equation:** $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 (dL/d\theta)$ — note $i^2$, NOT $i$ (no reversal possible with current sign).
- ✅ **Torque sign rule:** $dL/d\theta > 0$ → motoring; $dL/d\theta < 0$ → generating; $dL/d\theta = 0$ → no torque.
- ✅ **Fourier model:** $L(\theta) = L_0 - L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$, with $L_0 = (L_{max}+L_{min})/2$ and $L_1 = (L_{max}-L_{min})/2$.
- ✅ **Derivative trap:** $dL/d\theta = N_r L_1 \sin(N_r \theta)$ — the factor $N_r$ is **mandatory**.
- ✅ **Energy conversion ratio:** Under ideal linear, constant-current excitation, exactly **50 %** of input electrical energy is converted to mechanical work per stroke.
- ✅ **Co-energy method:** Use $T_e = \partial W'_{co}/\partial \theta$ at constant $i$ for general (non-linear) magnetic models.
- ✅ **Always convert degrees to radians** before plugging into torque equations.
- ✅ **Higher $L_{max}/L_{min}$** ⇒ higher average torque BUT higher torque ripple and acoustic noise.
- ✅ **Saturation reduces** effective $L_{max}$ at high currents — KTU problems often use the linear model to avoid this.
- ✅ **Per-phase torque is unipolar** (no negative current needed), simplifying the power converter to unidirectional switches with one diode per switch.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque Equation
Motoring and Regeneration. Low Speed and High Speed Operation - Characteristics
Energy Conversion Loop - Energy Effectiveness. Power Controllers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT638 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Conversion Loop - Energy Effectiveness. Power Controllers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Energy Conversion Loop — Formal Definition

The **Energy Conversion Loop** is the fundamental electromagnetic pathway through which a machine exchanges energy between the electrical domain (source/load) and the mechanical domain (shaft/load). For any rotating electrical machine, it is mathematically expressed as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The Energy Conversion Loop represents the closed-cycle linkage between the **input electrical energy** drawn from the source, the **stored field energy** in the air-gap magnetic field, the **mechanical work** delivered at the shaft, and the **losses** dissipated as heat. In KTU terminology, this loop governs *why and how* energy flows from the stator winding → rotating magnetic field → rotor → mechanical output.

The governing energy balance for any electromechanical energy conversion device is:

$$\boxed{dW_e = dW_f + dW_m}$$

Where:
- $dW_e$ = differential electrical energy input (joules)
- $dW_f$ = differential change in magnetic field (co-)energy stored
- $dW_m$ = differential mechanical work output

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Water Wheel Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine a **water wheel connected between two reservoirs**:
> - The **upper reservoir** = the electrical source (voltage source).
> - The **water falling on the wheel** = electrical current (flow of electrons).
> - The **spinning wheel** = the rotor doing mechanical work.
> - The **lower reservoir** = the mechanical load receiving energy.
> - **Water lost to splashing & friction** = losses (copper, iron, friction, windage).
>
> Just as water continuously cycles (pumped up → falls → spins wheel → drains → pumped up again), electrical energy continuously cycles through the magnetic coupling, performing work at every revolution. **Energy effectiveness** is simply: *how much useful "wheel-rotation" you get per bucket of water poured in.*

### 1.3 Energy Effectiveness — Definition

**Energy Effectiveness (η)** is the dimensionless ratio of useful output energy (or power) to the total input energy (or power), expressed as a percentage:

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{P_{in} - \sum P_{losses}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Special Machines context, the standard benchmark efficiencies are:
> - **BLDC Motor:** 85–92%
> - **PMSM:** 90–96%
> - **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM):** 85–90%
> - **Stepper Motor:** 30–70% (low due to holding losses)
> - **Synchronous Reluctance Motor:** 88–92%

### 1.4 Power Controllers — Definition

**Power Controllers** are static (or semi-static) power electronic converter circuits that condition the input supply voltage/current/frequency to match the operating requirements of special electrical machines. They form the *controlling arm* of the energy conversion loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy for Power Controllers:**
> Think of a **dimmer switch + gear-shifter combined into one electronic brain**:
> - It adjusts **how much power** reaches the motor (like a dimmer).
> - It adjusts **how the power is delivered** (DC pulses or AC waveforms — like a gear-shifter).
> - Without it, the motor is like a sports car without a gearbox — either off or full-throttle.

The four major families used in KTU PERAT638 syllabus:

| Controller Family | Application | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| DC Chopper (Buck/Boost/Buck-Boost) | Stepper, SRM, DC series motors | $V_o = k \cdot V_s$ |
| Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) | BLDC, PMSM, Induction | 3-φ PWM output |
| Current Source Inverter (CSI) | High-power SRM drives | $I_o$ controlled |
| Cycloconverter | Low-speed large Synchronous | Direct AC→AC |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Energy Conversion Loop

The loop is broken down into **four sequential stages**:

1. **Electrical Input Stage** — Supply delivers voltage $V_s$ and current $I_s$ to the controller.
2. **Conversion Stage** — Power controller modulates supply into machine-compatible waveform.
3. **Electromechanical Coupling Stage** — Magnetic field in air-gap converts electrical to mechanical form.
4. **Mechanical Output Stage** — Shaft torque $T_e$ at speed $\omega_m$ delivers power $P_m = T_e \cdot \omega_m$.

The complete energy flow follows:

$$P_{in} \xrightarrow{\text{controller}} P_{elec,machine} \xrightarrow{\text{air-gap}} P_{mech} \xrightarrow{\text{shaft}} P_{out}$$

### 2.2 Field Energy & Co-Energy — The Theoretical Core

The **magnetic field energy** stored in the air-gap of a singly-excited system with flux linkage $\psi$ and current $i$ is:

$$W_f = \int_0^{\psi} i(\psi)\, d\psi$$

The **co-energy** $W_f'$ (a mathematical construct that simplifies torque/force derivation) is:

$$\boxed{W_f' = i\psi - W_f = \int_0^{i} \psi(i)\, di}$$

**Why Co-Energy?** Because torque is the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to angular position at constant current — which is much easier to measure/control than field energy at constant flux:

$$\boxed{T_e = \left.\frac{\partial W_f'(i, \theta)}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i = \text{const}}}$$

For a linear magnetic system with inductance $L(\theta)$:

$$W_f' = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta)\, i^2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they form ~70% of all Module-3 numerical questions.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy Balance | $dW_e = dW_f + dW_m$ | J | Any EM device |
| 2 | Field Energy | $W_f = \int_0^{\psi} i\,d\psi$ | J | Field energy |
| 3 | Co-energy | $W_f' = i\psi - W_f = \int_0^i \psi\,di$ | J | Co-energy |
| 4 | Torque (co-energy) | $T_e = \partial W_f'/\partial \theta \vert_i$ | N·m | Constant current |
| 5 | Linear-system torque | $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ | N·m | Linear magnetics |
| 6 | Mechanical Power | $P_m = T_e \cdot \omega_m$ | W | Shaft output |
| 7 | Air-gap Power | $P_{ag} = T_e \cdot \omega_s$ | W | Synchronous machines |
| 8 | Efficiency | $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} = 1 - \sum P_L/P_{in}$ | % | All machines |
| 9 | Buck Chopper | $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ | V | $0 < D < 1$ |
| 10 | Boost Chopper | $V_o = V_s/(1-D)$ | V | $0 < D < 1$ |
| 11 | Buck-Boost | $V_o = -V_s \cdot D/(1-D)$ | V | Polarity inversion |
| 12 | PWM Duty Ratio | $D = T_{on}/T_s$ | — | All choppers |
| 13 | RMS Chopper Output | $V_{rms} = V_s\sqrt{D}$ | V | Resistive load |
| 14 | Total Losses | $\sum P_L = P_{cu} + P_{core} + P_{mech} + P_{stray}$ | W | All machines |
| 15 | Copper Loss | $P_{cu} = I^2 R$ | W | Stator/rotor |
| 16 | Core Loss | $P_{core} = P_h + P_e$ | W | Hysteresis + eddy |
| 17 | SRM Torque | $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$ | N·m | Salient pole machine |
| 18 | Energy Ratio | $\eta_E = W_{m,out}/W_{m,in}$ | — | Cycle-based metric |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Design:** Engineers compute $\eta$ across the energy conversion loop (battery → inverter → motor → wheels) to maximize range. A 5% efficiency gain translates to ~30 km extra range.
- **Industrial Robotics:** Stepper motor controllers use chopper circuits to microstep, dramatically reducing torque ripple and improving energy effectiveness.
- **Wind Energy Systems:** PMSG with full-power converter achieves energy effectiveness >96%, making it the gold standard for offshore wind turbines.
- **Spacecraft Actuators:** SRM drives are favored because the energy conversion loop has no rotor copper losses — critical for vacuum applications.

### 2.5 Power Controller Deep-Dive

**A) DC Chopper (Switch-Mode DC-DC Converter)**

The most basic power controller for SRM and stepper drives. Operating principle:

- A semiconductor switch (IGBT/MOSFET) connects the source to the load for a fraction $D$ of the switching period $T_s$.
- Energy storage elements ($L$ and $C$) smooth the pulsed output into a quasi-DC level.

**Three Topologies:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Buck (Step-Down):** $V_o = DV_s$ — output is *less than* input.
> **Boost (Step-Up):** $V_o = V_s/(1-D)$ — output is *greater than* input.
> **Buck-Boost:** $V_o = -DV_s/(1-D)$ — output is *inverted* and variable magnitude.

**B) Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)**

Used for BLDC, PMSM, and induction motor drives. The DC link voltage is switched in a specific PWM sequence to generate a 3-phase AC output. Two control methods:

- **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM):** Compares reference sine wave with triangular carrier.
- **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM):** Treats the 3-phase system as a single rotating vector — provides ~15% better DC bus utilization than SPWM.

**C) Cycloconverter**

Directly converts AC at one frequency to AC at another (lower) frequency without an intermediate DC link. Used for **low-speed, high-power synchronous and wound-rotor induction motor drives** (e.g., cement mills, ore crushers).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Conversion Equation from First Principles

**Starting Point:** Consider a singly-excited magnetic system with:
- Terminal voltage $v(t)$
- Current $i(t)$
- Flux linkage $\psi(i, \theta)$ — depends on both current and rotor position
- Mechanical displacement $\theta$

**Step 1:** Write the electrical input energy over differential time $dt$:

$$dW_{elec} = v \cdot i \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Express terminal voltage using Faraday's law. The flux linkage changes due to *both* current change *and* rotor motion:

$$v = \frac{d\psi}{dt} = \frac{\partial\psi}{\partial i}\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{\partial\psi}{\partial \theta}\frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by $i\,dt$ to get electrical energy input:

$$dW_{elec} = i \cdot \frac{\partial\psi}{\partial i}di + i \cdot \frac{\partial\psi}{\partial \theta}d\theta$$

**Step 4:** The first term on the right is the differential field energy:

$$dW_f = i \cdot \frac{\partial\psi}{\partial i}di \quad\Rightarrow\quad W_f = \int_0^\psi i\,d\psi$$

**Step 5:** The second term is the differential mechanical work (using $d\theta$):

$$dW_m = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta} d\theta = T_e \, d\theta$$

**Step 6:** Combine to obtain the energy conservation equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
dW_{elec} &= dW_f + dW_m \\
dW_{elec} &= dW_f + T_e \, d\theta
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** This is the **fundamental energy conversion equation** that governs *every* electromagnetic energy conversion device — DC machines, induction machines, synchronous machines, SRM, stepper motors, and linear actuators.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque Expression from Co-Energy

**Step 1:** Start from the co-energy definition:

$$W_f'(i, \theta) = \int_0^i \psi(i', \theta)\, di'$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate co-energy with respect to $\theta$ at constant current:

$$\left.\frac{\partial W_f'}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i} = \int_0^i \frac{\partial\psi(i', \theta)}{\partial \theta}\, di'$$

**Step 3:** For a linear magnetic system, $\psi(i, \theta) = L(\theta) \cdot i$. Substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left.\frac{\partial W_f'}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i} &= \int_0^i \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}[L(\theta) i']\, di' \\
&= \int_0^i i' \cdot \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}\, di' \\
&= \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{i^2}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Identify the result as torque (mechanical work = torque × angular displacement):

$$\boxed{T_e = \left.\frac{\partial W_f'}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i} = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for SRM:** In switched reluctance motors, $L(\theta)$ varies between $L_{min}$ and $L_{max}$ — and this torque equation tells us that *torque is produced only when inductance is changing with rotor position*. This is why SRMs are inherently variable-reluctance machines with no rotor windings or permanent magnets.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Buck Chopper Output Voltage

**Step 1:** Define the duty cycle $D$ as the fraction of switching period during which the switch is ON:

$$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply volt-second balance to the inductor (average inductor voltage over one cycle must be zero for steady-state operation):

$$V_L \cdot T_{on} + (V_L - V_s) \cdot T_{off} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $T_{on} = D \cdot T_s$ and $T_{off} = (1-D) \cdot T_s$:

$$V_o \cdot D \cdot T_s + (V_o - V_s)(1-D) T_s = 0$$

**Step 4:** Simplify and divide by $T_s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_o \cdot D + V_o(1-D) - V_s(1-D) &= 0 \\
V_o [D + (1-D)] &= V_s (1-D) \\
V_o \cdot 1 &= V_s (1-D)
\end{aligned}$$

Wait — this gives the **boost** result. Let me correct for **buck**:

**Buck Chopper:** During $T_{on}$, diode is reverse-biased, inductor sees $V_s - V_o$. During $T_{off}$, freewheeling diode conducts, inductor sees $-V_o$.

$$\begin{aligned}
(V_s - V_o) \cdot D \cdot T_s + (-V_o)(1-D) T_s &= 0 \\
V_s \cdot D - V_o \cdot D - V_o(1-D) &= 0 \\
V_s \cdot D &= V_o [D + (1-D)] = V_o
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{V_o = D \cdot V_s}$$

**Step 5:** RMS value for resistive load:

$$V_{o,rms} = V_s \sqrt{D}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Energy Effectiveness (Efficiency) of a Complete Drive

**Scenario:** A 3-phase BLDC motor drive powered by 48 V DC battery.

**Given Data:**
- Input: $V_{in} = 48$ V, $I_{in} = 30$ A
- Stator copper loss: $P_{cu} = 250$ W
- Iron/core loss: $P_{core} = 80$ W
- Friction + windage: $P_{fw} = 60$ W
- Stray load loss: $P_{stray} = 40$ W

**Step 1:** Calculate input power:

$$P_{in} = V_{in} \cdot I_{in} = 48 \times 30 = 1440 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Sum all losses:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum P_L &= P_{cu} + P_{core} + P_{fw} + P_{stray} \\
&= 250 + 80 + 60 + 40 \\
&= 430 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute output (shaft) power:

$$P_{out} = P_{in} - \sum P_L = 1440 - 430 = 1010 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4:** Compute energy effectiveness:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{1010}{1440} \times 100\% = 70.14\%$$

**Step 5:** Loss distribution analysis (loss-tree):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Copper loss fraction} &= \frac{250}{1440} = 17.36\% \\
\text{Core loss fraction} &= \frac{80}{1440} = 5.56\% \\
\text{Mechanical loss fraction} &= \frac{60}{1440} = 4.17\% \\
\text{Stray loss fraction} &= \frac{40}{1440} = 2.78\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Insight:** Copper loss dominates at high currents. To improve effectiveness, use thicker windings, higher-grade copper, or operate at lower currents with better cooling.

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERAT638]:**
*Define the term "Energy Conversion Loop" as applied to special electrical machines. List the four sequential stages through which energy flows.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Energy Conversion Loop** is the closed pathway of energy flow from electrical input to mechanical output in any electromagnetic device, governed by the energy balance equation $dW_e = dW_f + dW_m$.

**The four sequential stages are:**

1. **Electrical Input Stage** — Supply delivers voltage and current to the power controller.
2. **Conversion Stage** — Power controller modulates supply (e.g., DC chopper, VSI).
3. **Electromechanical Coupling Stage** — Magnetic field in the air-gap converts electrical energy to mechanical form.
4. **Mechanical Output Stage** — Shaft delivers torque at angular velocity to the load.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PERAT638]:**
*Define "Energy Effectiveness" of a drive system. Mention the typical efficiency ranges for PMSM and Stepper Motor drives.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Energy Effectiveness (η)** is the ratio of mechanical output power to electrical input power, expressed as a percentage:
> $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

**Typical ranges:**
- **PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor):** 90 – 96% (high efficiency due to rotor field from permanent magnets — no rotor copper losses).
- **Stepper Motor:** 30 – 70% (low efficiency due to continuous holding current and open-loop operation causing high copper losses).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PERAT638]:**

**(a)** Derive the **general energy conversion equation** $dW_e = dW_f + dW_m$ for a singly-excited electromagnetic system starting from the terminal voltage equation. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM)** has the following inductance profile:
- $L(\theta) = L_{min} = 10$ mH for $0° \leq \theta \leq 45°$
- $L(\theta) = L_{min} + (L_{max} - L_{min})\left(\frac{\theta - 45°}{45°}\right)$ for $45° \leq \theta \leq 90°$
- $L_{max} = 50$ mH

The phase current is maintained constant at $I = 8$ A during the rising inductance region. Calculate:
(i) Average torque during $45° \leq \theta \leq 90°$
(ii) Mechanical work done per stroke
(iii) If the rotor speed is 1500 rpm, compute the air-gap power and shaft power assuming mechanical losses of 50 W. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a)** — See **Derivation 1 in Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. Key points the examiner expects:

- Faraday's law applied to flux linkage: $v = d\psi/dt$
- Partial differentiation: $v = \frac{\partial\psi}{\partial i}\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{\partial\psi}{\partial \theta}\frac{d\theta}{dt}$
- Multiply by $i\,dt$: $dW_e = i\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial i}di + i\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial \theta}d\theta$
- Recognize: $dW_f = i\frac{\partial\psi}{\partial i}di$ and $dW_m = T_e\, d\theta$
- Final boxed result: $dW_e = dW_f + dW_m$
- Assumption: singly-excited system; magnetic field is the only energy storage medium; mechanical losses are excluded from $dW_m$.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $\frac{dL}{d\theta}$ in the active region ($45° \leq \theta \leq 90°$):

$$\frac{dL}{d\theta} = \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{45°} = \frac{50 - 10}{45} = \frac{40}{45} \text{ mH/deg}$$

Convert to per radian (since torque is in N·m):

$$\frac{dL}{d\theta}\bigg|_{rad} = \frac{40 \text{ mH}}{45° \times \pi/180} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{0.7854} = 50.93 \text{ mH/rad} = 0.05093 \text{ H/rad}$$

**Step 2:** Compute average torque using $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$:

$$T_{e,avg} = \frac{1}{2} \times (8)^2 \times 0.05093 = \frac{1}{2} \times 64 \times 0.05093 = 1.630 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3:** Compute mechanical work per stroke. Stroke angle = 45° = $\pi/4$ rad:

$$W_{m,stroke} = T_{e,avg} \times \Delta\theta = 1.630 \times \frac{\pi}{4} = 1.630 \times 0.7854 = 1.280 \text{ J}$$

**Step 4:** Compute strokes per second. At 1500 rpm with 4 strokes per revolution:

$$n_{strokes} = \frac{1500 \times 4}{60} = 100 \text{ strokes/sec}$$

**Step 5:** Compute air-gap power:

$$P_{ag} = W_{m,stroke} \times n_{strokes} = 1.280 \times 100 = 128 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6:** Compute shaft power:

$$P_{shaft} = P_{ag} - P_{mech,loss} = 128 - 50 = 78 \text{ W}$$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Average Torque | **1.630 N·m** |
| Mechanical work per stroke | **1.280 J** |
| Air-gap Power | **128 W** |
| Shaft Power | **78 W** |

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PERAT638]:**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** used for BLDC motor drives. Compare SPWM and SVPWM techniques. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Buck Chopper** feeds a separately excited DC motor from a 220 V DC source. The chopper operates at switching frequency $f_s = 2$ kHz with duty ratio $D = 0.4$. The motor armature resistance is $R_a = 0.5$ Ω and back-EMF constant $K_b = 1.2$ V·s/rad. The motor runs at a constant load torque of 20 N·m.

Calculate:
(i) Average output voltage of chopper
(ii) Motor speed in rpm
(iii) Input current drawn from source (assuming chopper is lossless)
(iv) Overall energy effectiveness if rotational losses = 100 W **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Comparison:**

Refer to **Mermaid Diagram 2 and 5** in Section 4 of these notes for the architecture.

**VSI Operation:**
- 3-phase VSI uses **6 IGBT switches** arranged in 3 legs.
- DC link capacitor provides stiff DC voltage.
- PWM signals generated by comparing reference sine waves with triangular carrier wave.
- For BLDC: 6-step commutation (120° conduction, 60° commutation intervals) — only two switches conduct at any time.

**SPWM vs SVPWM Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | SPWM | SVPWM |
|---|---|---|
| Modulation Index Range | 0 to 1 | 0 to 1.155 |
| DC Bus Utilization | 78.5% | 90.6% |
| Switching Losses | Higher | Lower |
| Implementation Complexity | Simple | Complex (requires sector identification) |
| Harmonic Content | Moderate | Lower |
| Best Use | Low-cost drives | High-performance drives |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Average output voltage**

$$V_o = D \cdot V_s = 0.4 \times 220 = 88 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Armature current at steady state**

At constant torque, armature current $I_a = T_L / K_t$. For DC motor, $K_t = K_b = 1.2$ N·m/A:

$$I_a = \frac{T_L}{K_t} = \frac{20}{1.2} = 16.67 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Back-EMF**

$$E_b = V_o - I_a R_a = 88 - 16.67 \times 0.5 = 88 - 8.33 = 79.67 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Motor speed**

$$E_b = K_b \cdot \omega \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega = \frac{E_b}{K_b} = \frac{79.67}{1.2} = 66.39 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N = \omega \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 66.39 \times 9.549 = 633.9 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5: Input current (lossless chopper: $P_{in} = P_o$)**

$$P_o = V_o \cdot I_a = 88 \times 16.67 = 1466.7 \text{ W}$$

$$I_{in} = \frac{P_{in}}{V_s} = \frac{1466.7}{220} = 6.67 \text{ A}$$

**Step 6: Energy effectiveness**

Mechanical output power:

$$P_{out} = T_L \cdot \omega - P_{rot,loss} = (20 \times 66.39) - 100 = 1327.8 - 100 = 1227.8 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{1227.8}{1466.7} \times 100\% = 83.71\%$$

**Final Answer Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Output Voltage | **88 V** |
| Motor Speed | **633.9 rpm** |
| Input Current | **6.67 A** |
| Energy Effectiveness | **83.71%** |

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deduction in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Forgetting to convert degrees to radians when computing torque. $dL/d\theta$ in H/**rad** must be used with torque in N·m. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Missing "Constant Current" Tag:** When applying $T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}$, the *current* must be constant. Failing to state this condition explicitly = **1 mark lost**.
> 3. **Confusing Field Energy with Co-Energy:** $W_f = \int i\,d\psi$ vs $W_f' = \int \psi\,di$. Remember: **co-energy is used at constant current** to derive torque; field energy is used at constant flux.
> 4. **Buck Chopper Polarity:** In buck-boost, students forget the **negative sign** indicating inverted output polarity. This loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Efficiency Definition Mismatch:** Some students write $\eta = P_{in}/P_{out}$. **Always** $P_{out}/P_{in}$.
> 6. **PWM Method Mix-up:** In SVPWM, the maximum linear modulation index is **1.155** (not 1.0). Confusing this with SPWM loses **2 marks**.
> 7. **Ignoring Mechanical Losses:** Many students compute $\eta$ as $(P_{in}-P_{cu})/P_{in}$, forgetting friction, windage, and stray losses. Always show **complete loss decomposition**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ⚡ **Energy Conversion Equation:** $dW_e = dW_f + dW_m$ — applies to ALL EM devices.
- ⚡ **Co-energy definition:** $W_f' = i\psi - W_f = \int_0^i \psi\,di$ — used at constant current.
- ⚡ **Torque formula:** $T_e = \partial W_f'/\partial\theta \vert_i = \frac{1}{2} i^2 (dL/d\theta)$ — for linear magnetics.
- ⚡ **SRM key fact:** Torque produced only during **rising inductance** region; no rotor copper losses.
- ⚡ **PMSM key fact:** Highest efficiency (90–96%) due to PM-generated rotor field.
- ⚡ **Stepper motor efficiency:** Low (30–70%) — open loop, continuous holding current.
- ⚡ **Buck Chopper:** $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ where $0 < D < 1$.
- ⚡ **Boost Chopper:** $V_o = V_s/(1-D)$.
- ⚡ **Buck-Boost Chopper:** $V_o = -D \cdot V_s/(1-D)$ — note negative polarity.
- ⚡ **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in} = 1 - \sum P_L/P_{in}$.
- ⚡ **VSI used for:** BLDC, PMSM, induction motor drives (all 3-phase AC motors).
- ⚡ **Cycloconverter used for:** Low-speed high-power synchronous motors.
- ⚡ **DC Chopper used for:** SRM, stepper, and DC motor drives.
- ⚡ **Total losses in machine:** $P_L = P_{cu} + P_{core} + P_{mech} + P_{stray}$.
- ⚡ **SVPWM advantage:** ~15% higher DC bus utilization than SPWM.
- ⚡ **Air-gap power:** $P_{ag} = T_e \cdot \omega_s$ for synchronous machines.
- ⚡ **Mechanical power developed:** $P_m = T_e \cdot \omega_m$.
- ⚡ **Energy effectiveness** is a dimensionless ratio; always express as percentage.
- ⚡ **Switching losses** in power controllers depend on $f_s$, $V$, and $I$ — increase with frequency.
- ⚡ **For KTU 2024:** Always show units in every numerical step; partial marks awarded for correct setup.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) Drive — The Power Electronics Context

A Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) is a doubly-salient, singly-excited electrical machine that produces torque purely from the tendency of its rotor to move to a position of minimum reluctance. Because there is no permanent magnet or rotor winding, the SRM requires an external power electronic converter that **must** perform three essential jobs every electrical cycle:

1. **Excite** the stator phase winding (Magnetization).
2. **Freewheel** the stored magnetic energy to control the rate of current rise/fall (Freewheeling — optional).
3. **De-energize** the phase quickly to remove torque-producing current before the negative torque region (Demagnetization).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The student must be able to draw and explain the **power circuit**, the **switching sequence / modes of operation**, and the **relative merits/drawbacks** of the following SRM converter topologies: Six-Switch (Asymmetric H-Bridge), Split DC Supply, and R-Dump.

---

### 1.2 The Three Converter Topologies — Conceptual Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Water Tap & Tank Model):**
> Think of the DC bus as a pressurized overhead water tank, the phase winding as a flexible rubber pipe, and the rotor saliency as a mechanical spring pulling on the pipe.
> - **Magnetizing** = opening the tap to fill the pipe (energy flows *into* the winding).
> - **Freewheeling** = closing the tap but leaving the pipe in a closed loop (energy circulates, slowly leaking).
> - **Demagnetizing** = opening a drain valve and forcing the water *back into the tank or into a heat-dissipating radiator* (energy leaves the winding).

| Converter | # Switches / Phase | # Diodes / Phase | Energy-Recovery Method | Best-Suited Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Six-Switch (Asymmetric H-Bridge)** | 2 | 2 | Full regeneration back to DC source | High-performance, wide-speed-range industrial drives |
| **Split DC Supply** | 1 | 1 | Partial regeneration to the *opposite* DC-link capacitor | Medium-performance, cost-sensitive industrial drives |
| **R-Dump** | 1 | 1 | Dissipation in a dump resistor (heat) | Low-cost, low-power, battery-operated appliances (e.g., vacuum cleaners, hand tools) |

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (KTU-Mandated Symbols)

- **V_dc** — DC-link supply voltage (**V**)
- **i_ph** — Instantaneous phase current (**A**)
- **L(θ)** — Phase inductance as a function of rotor position (**H**)
- **L_min, L_max** — Minimum and maximum unsaturated inductance (**H**)
- **R_ph** — Phase winding resistance (**Ω**)
- **θ_on, θ_off** — Turn-on and turn-off rotor angles (**rad / deg**)
- **T_ph** — Phase torque (**N·m**)
- **E_dump** — Energy dissipated in the dump resistor per stroke (**J**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Generalized Voltage Equation of a Phase Winding

For any SRM converter, the per-phase dynamic equation is **identical**:

$$v_{ph} = R_{ph} \cdot i_{ph} + \frac{d\psi(\theta, i_{ph})}{dt}$$

For a **linear magnetic model** (neglecting saturation and R_ph):

$$v_{ph} = L(\theta) \cdot \frac{di_{ph}}{dt} + i_{ph} \cdot \omega_r \cdot \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

Where:
- $L(\theta)\dfrac{di_{ph}}{dt}$ → **Transformer EMF** (rate of change of current).
- $i_{ph}\,\omega_r\,\dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ → **Motional EMF** (speed-dependent back EMF).

---

### 2.2 Topology-1: Six-Switch (Asymmetric H-Bridge) Converter

- Each phase (A, B, C, D) has its own dedicated H-bridge made of **2 IGBTs + 2 freewheeling diodes**.
- Total devices: **6 switches + 6 diodes** for a 4-phase machine.

**Three Modes of Operation:**

| Mode | Switches ON | Diode Path | Phase Voltage $v_{ph}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Magnetization (Hard Chop)** | S1, S2 | — | $+V_{dc}$ |
| **Freewheeling (Soft Chop)** | S1 only (or S2 only) | D1, D2 carry | $0$ V |
| **Demagnetization** | None (both off) | D1, D2 freewheel to source | $-V_{dc}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Chopping** = both switches toggle together (fast current change, high ripple).
> **Soft Chopping** = one switch held ON, the other modulated (slower current change, lower ripple, lower switching loss).
> The asymmetric bridge is the **most flexible and most widely used industrial SRM converter**.

---

### 2.3 Topology-2: Split DC Supply Converter (SDC)

- The DC bus is split by two equal capacitors **C1 and C2** with the midpoint **M** grounded (or used as a reference).
- Each phase has only **1 switch (S) and 1 diode (D)**.
- The phase winding is connected between the switch and the midpoint M.

**Modes of Operation:**

| Mode | Switch S | Diode D | Phase Voltage $v_{ph}$ | Energy Path |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Magnetization** | ON | OFF | $+V_{dc}/2$ (approx.) | Source → C1 → Switch → Phase → M |
| **Demagnetization** | OFF | ON | $-(V_{dc}/2 + V_C)$ | Phase → M → C2 → Diode → Phase (recovered) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Demagnetization Voltage = $-(V_{dc}/2 + V_C)$** — the phase sees *more* than half the bus voltage in reverse, so commutation is reasonably fast.
> A **PWM-controlled dump resistor** (or auxiliary switch across the lower capacitor) is needed to keep the two capacitor voltages balanced.

---

### 2.4 Topology-3: R-Dump Converter (RDC)

- One switch **S** and one diode **D** per phase.
- A common **dump resistor R_d** (with optional small capacitor C_d for snubbing) is connected across the DC bus through a common diode.

**Modes of Operation:**

| Mode | Switch S | Diode D | Phase Voltage $v_{ph}$ | Energy Path |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Magnetization** | ON | OFF | $+V_{dc}$ | Source → Switch → Phase → Source |
| **Demagnetization** | OFF | ON | $-(V_{dc} + V_{Rd})$ | Phase → Diode → R_d → Phase (dissipated as heat) |

> [!WARNING]
> All demagnetization energy $E_{dump} = \tfrac{1}{2} L(\theta_{off}) \, i_{off}^2$ is **burned as heat** in R_d. This makes the topology **inefficient** and unsuitable for high-power or high-speed applications.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_{ph} = R_{ph} i_{ph} + L(\theta)\dfrac{di_{ph}}{dt} + i_{ph}\omega_r\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Generalized per-phase dynamic equation |
| 2 | $i_{mag}(t) = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{L(\theta)}\bigl(t - t_{on}\bigr)$ | Approx. linear current rise during magnetization (linear region) |
| 3 | $i_{demag}(t) = i_{off} - \dfrac{V_{dc}}{L(\theta_{off})}\bigl(t - t_{off}\bigr)$ | Approx. linear current fall during demagnetization |
| 4 | $E_{dump} = \tfrac{1}{2} L(\theta_{off})\, i_{off}^2$ | Energy dissipated per electrical stroke (R-Dump) |
| 5 | $T_{ph} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, i_{ph}^2\, \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Instantaneous electromagnetic torque (linear model) |
| 6 | $T_{avg} = \dfrac{N_r V_{dc}^2}{\omega_r^2}\, f(\theta_{on},\theta_{off})$ | Average torque for single-pulse operation (qualitative KTU form) |
| 7 | $v_{ph,SSC}^{demag} = -V_{dc}$ | Demag voltage of Six-Switch converter |
| 8 | $v_{ph,SDC}^{demag} = -\bigl(V_{dc}/2 + V_C\bigr)$ | Demag voltage of Split DC Supply converter |
| 9 | $v_{ph,RDC}^{demag} = -(V_{dc} + V_{R_d})$ | Demag voltage of R-Dump converter (worst case) |
| 10 | $P_{loss,RDC} = \dfrac{1}{2} L\, i_{off}^2 \, f_{sw}$ | Per-phase switching power loss in R-Dump |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units convention:** All inductances in **H**, currents in **A**, time in **s**, angles in **rad** (use $\pi/180$ to convert from degrees), and speeds in **rad/s**.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Six-Switch Converter** → Used in **electric vehicles (e.g., Tesla Roadster inverter variant), aerospace actuators, and high-performance servo drives** because of full energy recovery and soft-chopping flexibility.
- **Split DC Supply** → Used in **washing-machine motors, small industrial pumps, and integrated appliance drives** where cost matters but some regeneration is needed.
- **R-Dump** → Found in **cordless power tools, kitchen appliances, low-cost domestic fans, and battery-operated SRM drives** (e.g., Dyson BLDC-style digital motors in vacuum cleaners, where simple, rugged drive electronics are preferred over efficiency).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Energy Stored in a Phase at Turn-Off (Used for R-Dump Sizing)

We start from the general flux-linkage definition:

$$\psi(\theta, i) = L(\theta) \cdot i_{ph}$$

Step 1 — Instantaneous stored magnetic energy in a linear inductor:

$$E_{stored}(t) = \int_0^{\psi} i \, d\psi = \int_0^{i} L(\theta) \cdot i \, di$$

Step 2 — Integrate with respect to current (L is held constant for an instant of rotor position):

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta)\, i_{ph}^2$$

Step 3 — At the turn-off instant $t = t_{off}$, the phase current is $i_{off}$ and the inductance is $L_{off} = L(\theta_{off})$. So:

$$\boxed{\,E_{dump} = \frac{1}{2} \, L_{off} \, i_{off}^{\,2}\,}$$

Step 4 — **Physical interpretation:** This entire energy *must* go somewhere every stroke. In the **R-Dump**, it is dissipated in $R_d$ as:

$$P_{loss,RDC} = E_{dump} \cdot f_{sw} = \frac{1}{2}\, L_{off}\, i_{off}^{\,2} \cdot f_{sw}$$

This is the **exact equation a designer uses to size the dump resistor's thermal rating**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Demagnetization Time for the Six-Switch Converter

The per-phase equation during demagnetization (both switches OFF, both diodes conducting) is:

$$-V_{dc} = L(\theta_{off})\, \frac{di_{ph}}{dt}$$

(The motional EMF is neglected during fast commutation; this is the standard KTU assumption.)

Step 1 — Separate variables:

$$L(\theta_{off})\, di_{ph} = -V_{dc}\, dt$$

Step 2 — Integrate from $t_{off}$ to $t_{end}$ (where current falls to zero):

$$\int_{i_{off}}^{0} L(\theta_{off})\, di_{ph} = \int_{t_{off}}^{t_{end}} -V_{dc}\, dt$$

Step 3 — Evaluate:

$$-L(\theta_{off})\, i_{off} = -V_{dc}\,(t_{end} - t_{off})$$

Step 4 — Solve for the commutation (fall) time:

$$\boxed{\,t_{c,SSC} = \frac{L(\theta_{off})\, i_{off}}{V_{dc}}\,}$$

> **Engineering insight:** The Six-Switch converter gives the **shortest commutation time** because it applies the full $-V_{dc}$ across the phase.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Demagnetization Time for the Split DC Supply Converter

During demag, the phase sees:

$$-v_{ph} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2} + V_C \;\approx\; V_{dc}$$

(if the two capacitors are balanced, $V_C \approx V_{dc}/2$).

Using the same procedure as §3.2:

$$\boxed{\,t_{c,SDC} \;\approx\; \frac{L(\theta_{off})\, i_{off}}{V_{dc}}\,}$$

> **Engineering insight:** SDC and SSC have **comparable commutation speed** in the ideal balanced-capacitor case, but SDC uses **half the switches**, so the trade-off is favorable for cost-driven applications.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Demagnetization Time for the R-Dump Converter

When the switch turns off, the phase current is forced through the dump resistor $R_d$. Applying KVL around the phase + dump loop:

$$L(\theta_{off})\, \frac{di_{ph}}{dt} + (R_{ph} + R_d)\, i_{ph} + V_{dc} = 0$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. The solution (assuming $R_{ph} \ll R_d$ so it is dropped) is:

$$i_{ph}(t) = i_{off}\, e^{-(t - t_{off})/\tau_d} + \frac{V_{dc}}{R_d}\Bigl(e^{-(t - t_{off})/\tau_d} - 1\Bigr)$$

where the dump-circuit time constant is:

$$\tau_d = \frac{L(\theta_{off})}{R_d}$$

Step 1 — The current decays approximately exponentially. The "turn-off time" $t_{c,RDC}$ is set by the time for $i_{ph}$ to fall to a small fraction (often 5 %) of $i_{off}$.

Step 2 — Setting $i_{ph}(t_c) = 0.05\, i_{off}$ and neglecting the $V_{dc}/R_d$ offset:

$$0.05 \approx e^{-t_c / \tau_d}$$

Step 3 — Taking the natural logarithm:

$$-t_c / \tau_d = \ln(0.05) \approx -3.0$$

Step 4 — Solve for the commutation time:

$$\boxed{\,t_{c,RDC} \;\approx\; 3\,\tau_d \;=\; \frac{3\,L(\theta_{off})}{R_d}\,}$$

> **Engineering insight:** Increasing $R_d$ speeds up commutation, **but** it also increases the voltage stress across the switch to $V_{S,max} = V_{dc} + i_{off} R_d$ and **wastes more energy** in the form of heat $i^2 R_d$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Two-Mark Favourite)

| Parameter | Six-Switch (SSC) | Split DC Supply (SDC) | R-Dump (RDC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Switches / phase** | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| **Diodes / phase** | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| **Magnetization voltage** | $+V_{dc}$ | $+V_{dc}/2$ | $+V_{dc}$ |
| **Demag voltage (magnitude)** | $V_{dc}$ | $\approx V_{dc}$ (split-bus) | $V_{dc} + i_{off} R_d$ |
| **Freewheeling (zero-voltage loop) possible?** | Yes (soft chop) | No | No |
| **Energy recovery** | Full to source | Partial to bus cap | None — heat in R_d |
| **Switching loss** | Medium | Low | Low (one switch) |
| **Conduction loss** | High (2 switches in series) | Low (1 switch) | Low (1 switch) |
| **Torque ripple** | Lowest (best control) | Medium | Highest (limited control) |
| **Cost** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| **Best application** | EV, aerospace, servo | Appliances, pumps | Hand-tools, fans |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Six-Switch (Asymmetric H-Bridge) Converter — Per-Phase Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    DCPos[V_dc Positive Rail] --- SA1[Switch S1 - Top Left]
    DCPos --- SB1[Switch S3 - Top Right]
    DCPos --- SC1[Switch S5]
    DCPos --- SD1[Switch S7]
    SA1 --- DA1[Diode D1 - Anti-parallel S1]
    SB1 --- DB1[Diode D3]
    SC1 --- DC1[Diode D5]
    SD1 --- DD1[Diode D7]
    SA1 --- PhaA((Phase A Winding))
    SB1 --- PhaB((Phase B Winding))
    SC1 --- PhaC((Phase C Winding))
    SD1 --- PhaD((Phase D Winding))
    PhaA --- SA2[Switch S2 - Bottom Left]
    PhaB --- SB2[Switch S4 - Bottom Right]
    PhaC --- SC2[Switch S6]
    PhaD --- SD2[Switch S8]
    SA2 --- DA2[Diode D2 - Anti-parallel S2]
    SB2 --- DB2[Diode D4]
    SC2 --- DC2[Diode D6]
    SD2 --- DD2[Diode D8]
    SA2 --- DCNeg[V_dc Negative Rail]
    SB2 --- DCNeg
    SC2 --- DCNeg
    SD2 --- DCNeg
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each phase is **fully isolated** and forms its own H-bridge. Turning ON S1 & S2 magnetizes the phase; turning OFF both and letting D1 & D2 conduct demagnetizes the phase (full $-V_{dc}$).

---

### 4.2 Split DC Supply Converter — Power Circuit

```mermaid
graph TD
    DCPos[V_dc Positive Rail] --- C1[Capacitor C1 - Upper Half Bus]
    C1 --- Mid[Midpoint M - Capacitor Junction]
    Mid --- C2[Capacitor C2 - Lower Half Bus]
    C2 --- DCNeg[V_dc Negative Rail]
    DCPos --- SA[Switch S_A - Phase A]
    SA --- PhaA((Phase A Winding))
    PhaA --- Mid
    Mid --- DA[Diode D_A - Phase A Return]
    DA --- DCPos
    DCPos --- SB[Switch S_B - Phase B]
    SB --- PhaB((Phase B Winding))
    PhaB --- Mid
    Mid --- DB[Diode D_B]
    DB --- DCPos
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation:** When $S_A$ is ON, current flows $V_{dc}^+ \to S_A \to \text{Phase A} \to M$ (magnetization at $\approx V_{dc}/2$).  
> When $S_A$ is OFF, the phase current freewheels through $D_A$ back to the positive rail, and the stored energy is dumped into the *upper* capacitor C1 — hence **partial regeneration**.

---

### 4.3 R-Dump Converter — Power Circuit

```mermaid
graph TD
    DCPos[V_dc Positive Rail] --- SA[Switch S_A - Phase A]
    SA --- PhaA((Phase A Winding))
    PhaA --- DCNeg[V_dc Negative Rail]
    PhaA --- DA[Diode D_A]
    DA --- RdNode[Dump Node]
    RdNode --- Rd[Dump Resistor R_d]
    Rd --- DCNeg
    DCPos --- SB[Switch S_B - Phase B]
    SB --- PhaB((Phase B Winding))
    PhaB --- DCNeg
    PhaB --- DB[Diode D_B]
    DB --- RdNode
    DCPos --- SC[Switch S_C - Phase C]
    SC --- PhaC((Phase C Winding))
    PhaC --- DCNeg
    PhaC --- DC[Diode D_C]
    DC --- RdNode
```

> [!NOTE]
> All phase demagnetization currents converge on a **single common dump resistor** $R_d$. The resistor must be sized for the **sum of worst-case energies** from all phases.

---

### 4.4 Functional Control-Flow Architecture (All Three Topologies)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Ref[Speed / Torque Reference] --> Ctrl[Controller - Hysteresis or PWM]
    Ctrl --> AngleCalc[Rotor Position Decoder theta_on, theta_off]
    AngleCalc --> TopSel{Topology Selection}
    TopSel --> SSC[Six-Switch Converter]
    TopSel --> SDC[Split DC Supply Converter]
    TopSel --> RDC[R-Dump Converter]
    SSC --> SRM4[(4-Phase SRM)]
    SDC --> SRM4
    RDC --> SRM4
    SRM4 --> PosSen[Encoder / Resolver]
    PosSen --> AngleCalc
    SRM4 --> Meas[Current and Voltage Sensors]
    Meas --> Ctrl
```

> [!TIP]
> The **controller, position decoder, and feedback loop are identical** for all three topologies — only the **power stage** differs. This is why a course on SRM drives dedicates so much time to the converter comparison.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Bank]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the operation of the **Six-Switch (Asymmetric H-Bridge) converter** for a 4-phase SRM drive in the **magnetization and demagnetization modes**. State the phase voltages in each mode. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

> The Six-Switch converter is a per-phase asymmetric H-bridge with 2 IGBTs ($S_1, S_2$) and 2 freewheeling diodes ($D_1, D_2$) per phase.
>
> - **Magnetization mode:** $S_1$ and $S_2$ are both turned ON. Current rises from the DC source through $S_1 \to$ phase winding $\to S_2$ back to the source. The applied phase voltage is $v_{ph} = +V_{dc}$.
> - **Demagnetization mode:** Both $S_1$ and $S_2$ are turned OFF simultaneously. The stored inductive current freewheels through $D_1$ and $D_2$, returning energy to the DC bus. The applied phase voltage is $v_{ph} = -V_{dc}$.
>
> This topology also supports a **freewheeling (soft-chop) mode** by keeping one switch ON and modulating the other, giving $v_{ph} = 0$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Repeated Pattern]**
*Compare the **Split DC Supply** and **R-Dump** converters for an SRM drive in terms of number of switches, energy-recovery mechanism, and demagnetization voltage. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Split DC Supply (SDC) | R-Dump (RDC) |
|---|---|---|
| Switches per phase | 1 (with 1 diode) | 1 (with 1 diode) |
