T_e \;=\; \frac{3}{2}\,p\,(L_d - L_q)\,I_d\,I_q \\
\;=\; \frac{3}{2}\times 2 \times (0.4 - 0.05) \times 5 \times 7 \\
\;=\; 3 \times 0.35 \times 35 \\
\;=\; 36.75 \;\text{N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(c)** MTPA requires $I_q = (\xi - 1)\,I_d = 7 \times 5 = 35$ A. But our $I_q = 7$ A, so MTPA is *not* satisfied. The drive should ideally set $I_q = 35$ A — but limited by inverter rating. The current ratio actually used is:

$$\frac{I_q}{I_d} \;=\; \frac{7}{5} \;=\; 1.4$$

$$\xi - 1 \;=\; 7$$

So the operating point lies **below** the MTPA locus — meaning torque-per-ampere is suboptimal.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — SynRM Drive System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rectA1[Three Phase AC Mains Supply 50 Hz] --> rectA2[Diode Bridge Rectifier]
    rectA2 --> rectA3[DC Link Capacitor Bus]
    rectA3 --> rectA4[Voltage Source Inverter VSI IGBT]
    rectA4 --> rectA5[SynRM Stator Three Phase Windings]
    rectA6[Encoder or Resolver Position Sensor] --> rectA7[Field Oriented Controller DSP FPGA]
    rectA7 --> rectA8[Space Vector PWM SVPWM Generator]
    rectA8 --> rectA4
    rectA5 --> rectA9[Rotor Salient Anisotropic Ld vs Lq]
    rectA9 --> rectA10[Variable Reluctance Torque Te]
    rectA10 --> rectA11[Mechanical Load Pump Fan Compressor]
    rectA7 --> rectA12[MTPA Look Up Table Using Ld and Lq]
    rectA12 --> rectA7
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Rotor Construction Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rectB1[Rotor Construction Methods] --> rectB2[Salient Pole Simple Lamination]
    rectB1 --> rectB3[Transversely Laminated Anisotropic TLA]
    rectB1 --> rectB4[Axially Laminated Anisotropic ALA]

    rectB2 --> rectB5[Low Saliency Ratio xi less than 3]
    rectB2 --> rectB6[Easy Manufacturing Low Cost]

    rectB3 --> rectB7[High Saliency Ratio xi 4 to 7]
    rectB3 --> rectB8[Fluid Barriers Insulated Steel Sheets]

    rectB4 --> rectB9[Highest Saliency Ratio xi 8 to 12]
    rectB4 --> rectB10[Lamination Stacks Rolled and Bent]
    rectB4 --> rectB11[Best Performance Industrial Standard]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Inductance Variation vs Rotor Angle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rectC1[Rotor Electrical Angle theta e from 0 to 360 deg] --> rectC2{Inductance L theta Calculation}
    rectC2 --> rectC3[At theta e equals 0 deg L equals Ld Maximum]
    rectC2 --> rectC4[At theta e equals 45 deg L equals Mean of Ld and Lq]
    rectC2 --> rectC5[At theta e equals 90 deg L equals Lq Minimum]
    rectC2 --> rectC6[At theta e equals 135 deg L equals Mean Again]
    rectC2 --> rectC7[At theta e equals 180 deg L equals Ld Again Period Half Cycle]
    rectC3 --> rectC8[Sinusoidal L theta pattern with period 180 deg electrical]
    rectC4 --> rectC8
    rectC5 --> rectC8
    rectC6 --> rectC8
    rectC7 --> rectC8
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Vector Phasor and MTPA Control Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rectD1[Stator Current Vector Is] --> rectD2[Decompose in dq Frame]
    rectD2 --> rectD3[Id Component along d Axis]
    rectD2 --> rectD4[Iq Component along q Axis]
    rectD3 --> rectD5[Flux Producing Component]
    rectD4 --> rectD6[Pure Torque Producing Component]
    rectD5 --> rectD7[Optimal Ratio Iq equals xi minus 1 times Id]
    rectD6 --> rectD7
    rectD7 --> rectD8[MTPA Operating Point on Constant Torque Hyperbola]
    rectD8 --> rectD9[Minimum Current for Given Torque]
    rectD8 --> rectD10[Best Achievable Power Factor cos phi equals xi minus 1 over 2 sqrt xi]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**"Why is a perfectly cylindrical rotor unsuitable for a Synchronous Reluctance Motor?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A SynRM develops torque entirely through the *variable reluctance* principle. The electromagnetic torque per phase is given by:

$$T_e \;=\; -\frac{1}{2}\,i^{2}\,(L_d - L_q)\,\sin(2\theta_e)$$

If the rotor is **cylindrical** (non-salient), it has *magnetic isotropy*: $L_d = L_q$, so $(L_d - L_q) = 0$. Substituting this into the torque equation yields $T_e = 0$ regardless of stator current or rotor position. Hence, no torque is produced and the motor cannot operate. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define saliency ratio of a SynRM and state its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **saliency ratio** $\xi$ is defined as the ratio of direct-axis to quadrature-axis synchronous inductances:

$$\xi \;=\; \frac{L_d}{L_q}$$

**Significance:**
- Higher $\xi$ ⇒ greater reluctance torque for a given current.
- Higher $\xi$ ⇒ improved power factor (best PF $\cos\phi = (\xi-1)/(2\sqrt{\xi})$).
- Modern ALA rotors achieve $\xi$ of 8–12. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> - Students **omit the negative sign** in the torque equation — KTU examiners *will* deduct ½ mark.
> - Confusing **electrical angle $\theta_e$** with **mechanical angle $\theta_m$** — always remember $\theta_e = p\,\theta_m$. A 1-mark penalty is typical.
> - Skipping the **co-energy method** and directly quoting the formula — examiners want at least 2–3 derivation steps shown.
> - Forgetting the factor of **3** when going from *per-phase* to *3-phase* torque — costs 1 mark.
> - Drawing the **d–q phasor diagram incorrectly** — make sure Id is along the rotor d-axis and Iq is 90° ahead.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choose ONE)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a **Synchronous Reluctance Motor**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for electromagnetic torque of a salient-pole SynRM in terms of $L_d$, $L_q$, current $i$, and rotor angle $\theta_e$. Hence, obtain the condition for maximum torque. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction & Working Principle — 7 marks**

The SynRM consists of:

1. **Stator:** A conventional 3-phase, distributed, short-pitched AC winding housed in slotted silicon-steel laminations. When supplied with balanced 3-phase AC, it produces a rotating sinusoidal air-gap MMF $F_s$.

2. **Rotor:** A salient, anisotropic ferromagnetic body **without any rotor winding or permanent magnet**. The rotor geometry is deliberately shaped to create two distinct magnetic axes:
   - **Direct axis (d-axis)** — the axis of *minimum reluctance* (easiest flux path), aligned along the rotor's "preferred" direction.
   - **Quadrature axis (q-axis)** — the axis of *maximum reluctance* (hardest flux path), perpendicular to d-axis.

The two dominant rotor construction types are **Transversely Laminated Anisotropic (TLA)** and **Axially Laminated Anisotropic (ALA)**, both producing high $L_d/L_q$ ratio.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

- The rotating stator MMF establishes a sinusoidal flux distribution in the air gap.
- Because flux always follows the path of *minimum reluctance*, the rotor experiences a torque that attempts to align its d-axis with the stator MMF axis.
- This torque is called **reluctance torque** $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,i^{2}\,\tfrac{dL}{d\theta_e}$.
- The rotor therefore rotates at the **synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f / P$ — there is no slip (unlike an induction motor) and no rotor field winding (unlike a conventional synchronous motor).
- Any temporary mechanical displacement creates a restoring torque proportional to $\sin(2\theta_e)$, making SynRM a self-starting machine when fed by a VFD.

**(b) Derivation — 7 marks**

(Use the **complete derivation** shown in **Section 3.1 and 3.2** of these notes. Key checkpoint results the examiner expects:)

Step 1: Co-energy in single phase — $W_{co} = \tfrac{1}{2}L(\theta_e)i^{2}$ ............ **(1 mark)**

Step 2: Torque from virtual work — $T_e = p\,\tfrac{dW_{co}}{d\theta_e} = \tfrac{1}{2}i^{2}\tfrac{dL}{d\theta_e}$ ............ **(1 mark)**

Step 3: Express $L(\theta_e) = \tfrac{L_d+L_q}{2} + \tfrac{L_d - L_q}{2}\cos(2\theta_e)$ ............ **(1 mark)**

Step 4: Differentiate — $\tfrac{dL}{d\theta_e} = -(L_d - L_q)\sin(2\theta_e)$ ............ **(1 mark)**

Step 5: Final boxed result:
$$T_e \;=\; -\tfrac{1}{2}\,i^{2}\,(L_d - L_q)\,\sin(2\theta_e)$$ ............ **(1 mark)**

Step 6: Maximum-torque condition: $\tfrac{dT_e}{d\theta_e}=0 \Rightarrow 2\theta_e = 90^\circ$ ............ **(1 mark)**

Step 7: $T_{e,\max} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,i^{2}\,(L_d - L_q)$ at $\theta_e = 45^\circ$ electrical ............ **(1 mark)**

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** With the aid of the d-q reference frame, derive the **vector-controlled torque expression** for a 3-phase SynRM:

$$T_e \;=\; \tfrac{3}{2}\,p\,(L_d - L_q)\,I_d\,I_q$$

State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Maximum Torque Per Ampere (MTPA)**. For a SynRM with $L_d = 0.3$ H and $L_q = 0.05$ H, compute: (i) the saliency ratio, (ii) the MTPA current ratio $I_q/I_d$, (iii) the best achievable power factor, (iv) the electromagnetic torque when $I_d = 4$ A and $I_q = 24$ A for a 6-pole machine. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Vector-Controlled d-q Torque Derivation — 7 marks**

**Assumptions:** **(1 mark)**
- Stator windings are sinusoidally distributed.
- Magnetic linearity (no saturation).
- Stator reference frame transformed to rotor-aligned d-q frame.
- Currents Id, Iq are DC in steady state.

**Derivation:** **(6 marks)**

Step 1: Inductance matrix in d-q frame:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \lambda_d \\ \lambda_q \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} L_d & 0 \\ 0 & L_q \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} I_d \\ I_q \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 2: Co-energy per phase: $W_{co}^{ph} = \tfrac{1}{2}(L_d I_d^2 + L_q I_q^2)$ ............ **(1 mark)**

Step 3: Total 3-phase co-energy: $W_{co}^{3ph} = \tfrac{3}{2}(L_d I_d^2 + L_q I_q^2)$ ............ **(1 mark)**

Step 4: Torque from $T_e = p\,\tfrac{\partial W_{co}}{\partial \theta_e}$. Since $L_d$, $L_q$ are constants and the cross term appears through the rotation matrix, the cross-coupled term survives:

$$T_e \;=\; \frac{3}{2}\,p\,(L_d - L_q)\,I_d\,I_q$$ ............ **(3 marks)** — boxed final result **(1 mark)**

**(b) MTPA & Numerical — 7 marks**

**(i)** Saliency ratio:
$$\xi = \frac{L_d}{L_q} = \frac{0.30}{0.05} = 6.0$$ ............ **(1.5 marks)**

**(ii)** MTPA current ratio:
$$\frac{I_q}{I_d} = \xi - 1 = 5.0$$ ............ **(1.5 marks)**

**(iii)** Best achievable power factor:
$$\cos\phi_{\text{best}} = \frac{\xi - 1}{2\sqrt{\xi}} = \frac{5}{2\sqrt{6}} = \frac{5}{4.899} \approx 1.020$$
The result exceeds 1 — meaning this particular motor design **cannot achieve unity PF**; the formula represents an *aspirational upper bound* and the actual PF in this case is clamped at **unity (1.0)**, indicating PF limitation is reached. **(2 marks)** *(Many KTU students lose marks here by blindly applying the formula. Examiner's tip: always cross-check the physical bound $\cos\phi \le 1$.)*

**(iv)** Electromagnetic torque, $p = 3$, $I_d = 4$ A, $I_q = 24$ A:
$$\begin{aligned}
T_e \;=\; \frac{3}{2}\,p\,(L_d - L_q)\,I_d\,I_q \\
\;=\; 1.5 \times 3 \times 0.25 \times 4 \times 24 \\
\;=\; 4.5 \times 0.25 \times 96 \\
\;=\; 108 \;\text{N·m}
\end{aligned}$$ ............ **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist (KTU Board — Last-Minute Notes):**

- ☐ SynRM has **no rotor winding, no PM, no brushes** → only stator copper loss and iron loss.
- ☐ Torque exists **only if $L_d \ne L_q$**; cylindrical rotor ⇒ zero torque.
- ☐ Saliency ratio $\xi = L_d / L_q$ is the *figure of merit*. Aim: $\xi \ge 8$.
- ☐ Per-phase torque: $T_e = -\tfrac{1}{2}\,i^{2}\,(L_d - L_q)\sin(2\theta_e)$.
- ☐ Maximum torque per phase at $\theta_e = 45^\circ$ electrical.
- ☐ 3-phase FOC torque: $T_e = \tfrac{3}{2}\,p\,(L_d - L_q)\,I_d\,I_q$.
- ☐ MTPA current ratio: $I_q = (\xi - 1)\,I_d$.
- ☐ Best PF: $\cos\phi = (\xi - 1)/(2\sqrt{\xi})$ — capped at 1.0.
- ☐ **Rotor runs at synchronous speed** — no slip.
- ☐ SynRM is **self-starting** only via VFD; on direct-online 50 Hz it cannot start (rotor inertia + no damping).
- ☐ Construction types: TLA (cheap), ALA (high-performance), salient-pole (obsolete).
- ☐ Applications: pumps, fans, compressors, EV traction (Renault, BMW i3), HVAC, servo drives.
- ☐ Advantages: robust, magnet-free, low rotor losses, high-speed capable.
- ☐ Disadvantages: lower PF than PMSM, lower torque density than PMSM, requires position sensor for FOC.

**Mnemonic for remembering the d-q torque formula:**
> *"**Three halves, p, delta L, Id, Iq**"* → $\tfrac{3}{2}\,p\,(L_d - L_q)\,I_d\,I_q$.

**Mnemonic for the L(θ) variation:**
> *"**Mean plus half difference cosine of two-theta**"* → $\tfrac{L_d+L_q}{2} + \tfrac{L_d-L_q}{2}\cos(2\theta_e)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Good Luck for your KTU Board Exam!** Focus on derivations, sign conventions, and the **MTPA locus** — these are examiner-favourites year after year.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Permanent Magnet Synchronous Reluctance Motors - Constructional Details
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT638 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Permanent Magnet Synchronous Reluctance Motors - Constructional Details

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# PERMANENT MAGNET SYNCHRONOUS RELUCTANCE MOTORS — CONSTRUCTIONAL DETAILS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Reluctance Motor (PMSynRM or PMaSynRM)** is a salient-pole synchronous AC machine whose rotor contains **both high-energy permanent magnets (PMs)** and **anisotropic flux barriers/air pockets**. The magnets are embedded inside the flux barriers of a laminated reluctance rotor, enabling the motor to develop **two simultaneous torque components** — a *magnet torque* (from PM–stator field interaction) and a *reluctance torque* (from the rotor's geometric saliency, $L_d \neq L_q$).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is classified as a **hybrid synchronous drive machine** that unites the **high power density of PMSM** with the **field-weakening capability and robustness of synchronous reluctance motors (SynRM)**.

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a **bicycle that has both paddles AND an electric motor**:
- The **paddles** (reluctance torque) work harder when you are on an inclined road — this is the *saliency* giving extra pull.
- The **electric motor** (PM torque) gives you effortless cruising on flat roads — this is the *permanent magnet assistance*.

Together, they make the ride smoother, more efficient, and capable of climbing steeper slopes than either one alone. Similarly, the PMSynRM uses **two torque-producing mechanisms** in the same rotor, giving it the best of both worlds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Construction:**
> - **Saliency Ratio** $\xi = L_q / L_d$ — typically between **2 to 7** (higher = better reluctance torque)
> - **Number of Poles** $2p$ — commonly **4, 6, or 8**
> - **Magnet Material** — **NdFeB (Neodymium-Iron-Boron)** or **SmCo (Samarium-Cobalt)**
> - **Lamination Steel** — **M19 / M36 grain-oriented silicon steel** (0.35 mm or 0.5 mm thick)
> - **Air-gap length** $g$ — usually **0.5 mm to 1.5 mm**
> - **Lamination Stack Length** $L_{stk}$ — **50 mm to 250 mm** for industrial drives

### Why Constructional Details Matter

The **performance index** (torque density, power factor, flux-weakening range, demagnetization withstand) of a PMSynRM is **almost entirely determined by the rotor's geometry** — specifically the **shape, number, and angular placement of flux barriers and magnets**. The stator, by contrast, is largely standard across most AC machines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Logic of the Rotor

The constructional philosophy of a PMSynRM rotor can be broken into four structural pillars:

1. **Salient Pole Rotor Body** — Built from **anisotropic electrical steel laminations**. The lamination pattern deliberately creates *direct-axis (d-axis)* and *quadrature-axis (q-axis)* magnetic paths of *different* reluctance.
   - **d-axis**: path *through* the magnet (low reluctance due to flux concentration).
   - **q-axis**: path *across* the flux barriers (high reluctance due to air gaps).

2. **Multi-Barrier Flux Channels** — Within each pole, **2, 3, or 4 flux barriers** are punched out of each lamination. These act as "magnetic insulators" that block q-axis flux while allowing d-axis flux.

3. **Embedded Permanent Magnets** — Each barrier houses a thin strip of **sintered NdFeB magnet**, magnetized in the d-direction. Magnets act as a **secondary flux source**, boosting average air-gap flux density $B_{avg}$ from ~0.5 T (pure SynRM) to **0.8–1.1 T**.

4. **Ribs & Bridges** — Tiny **mechanical bridges** (typically 0.5 mm–1.2 mm thick) connect the lamination segments for structural integrity. They saturate magnetically under load and behave like air.

## 2.2 Constructional Components — Step-by-Step

### A. STATOR (Standard 3-phase AC Stator)

| Sub-Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| Stator core | Stacked, **interlocking** or **welded** silicon-steel laminations, M19/M36 grade |
| Slots | **Semi-closed, rectangular or trapezoidal**, with **36, 48, or 60 slots** typical |
| Winding | **Double-layer, short-pitched, distributed winding**; 5/6 or 2/3 chorded |
| Insulation | **Class F or H** (155 °C / 180 °C) — epoxy-mica or polyester |
| Cooling | **Totally Enclosed Fan Cooled (TEFC)** or **water jacket** in high-power drives |

> The stator is identical to a standard **PMSM or 3-phase induction motor** of the same frame size. This is a major *manufacturing advantage* — only the **rotor** is bespoke.

### B. ROTOR (The Custom Heart of PMSynRM)

| Sub-Component | Specification |
|---|---|
| Rotor lamination | **M19 / M36 / NO20 electrical steel**, 0.35 mm lamination |
| Flux barriers | **U-shaped, V-shaped, or arc-shaped** punched cavities, typically 2–4 per pole |
| Magnets | Sintered **NdFeB (N35SH–N42UH grade)** or **Sm₂Co₁₇** for high-temperature apps |
| Magnet placement | Transverse, radial, or tangential inside barriers |
| Bridges | **Mechanical support ribs**, 0.5–1.2 mm wide |
| Shaft | **Non-magnetic stainless steel (SS-304/316)** to prevent axial flux shunting |
| Rotor OD | Sized for **air gap $g$ ≈ 0.5–1.5 mm** |
| Balancing | Precision grade **G2.5 or G1.0** (ISO 1940) |

### C. END-CAPS, BEARINGS & SENSORS

- **End-caps**: Aluminum or cast iron, with **resolver / optical encoder mount** at non-drive end (NDE).
- **Bearings**: Deep-groove ball bearings, **sealed-for-life** (2RS type).
- **Position Sensor**: **Resolver** (preferred for harsh environments) or **incremental encoder** with $\sin/\cos$ tracks.

## 2.3 Why This Construction? — The Engineering Rationale

- **PMs inside barriers** → protect magnets from centrifugal forces at high speed (no need for carbon-fiber sleeves).
- **Multi-barrier geometry** → raises $\xi$ from 4 (single barrier) to **6–8** in optimized designs.
- **Asymmetric magnet placement (V-shape)** → increases d-axis flux linkage while keeping q-axis inductance high.
- **Stator re-use** → lowers tooling cost; only rotor dies need investment.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU ESE and Module Internal exams.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $n_s = 120 f / 2p$ | rpm |
| 2 | Electrical angular frequency | $\omega_e = 2\pi f = (2p/2)\omega_m$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Saliency ratio | $\xi = L_q / L_d$ | dimensionless, typically **2 to 7** |
| 4 | Characteristic current | $I_{ch} = \psi_m / L_d$ | A, governs flux-weakening |
| 5 | PM flux linkage | $\psi_m$ | Wb (Weber) |
| 6 | d-axis inductance | $L_d$ | H (Henry) |
| 7 | q-axis inductance | $L_q$ | H (Henry) |
| 8 | Magnet torque | $T_{pm} = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{p}{2} \cdot \psi_m I_q$ | N·m |
| 9 | Reluctance torque | $T_{rel} = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{p}{2} \cdot (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q$ | N·m |
| 10 | **Total electromagnetic torque** | $T_e = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{p}{2} \cdot [\psi_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q]$ | N·m |
| 11 | Power output | $P_{out} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$ | W |
| 12 | Back-EMF (RMS per phase) | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \phi_m \, k_w$ | V |
| 13 | Air-gap flux density (PM) | $B_g \approx B_r \cdot \dfrac{A_m}{A_g}$ | T |
| 14 | Demagnetization margin | $M = \dfrac{B_g}{B_r}$ | Must be < 0.4 at worst case |

> **Critical pipe-escaping note**: All inline math uses standard LaTeX delimiters; values such as $\vert L_d - L_q \vert$ are written using `\vert` to prevent markdown-table collision.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

This construction is the **industry standard for EV traction drives** (Renault Zoe, BMW i3 compressor drives), **HVAC compressors**, **industrial servo drives**, and **high-speed spindle motors** — wherever high efficiency, wide constant-power speed range, and *no rotor copper losses* are simultaneously required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Total Electromagnetic Torque

The torque expression is the *single most important equation* for PMSynRM in KTU exams. We derive it from first principles using the **co-energy method** in the synchronous d–q reference frame.

### Step 1 — Define Stator Current in d-q Frame

For a balanced 3-phase sinusoidal supply, transform to the rotating reference frame (Park's transformation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_d &= I_s \sin\beta \\
i_q &= I_s \cos\beta
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $I_s = \sqrt{i_d^2 + i_q^2}$ is the stator current magnitude,
- $\beta$ is the **current angle** (advance angle relative to q-axis).

### Step 2 — Write Stator Flux Linkages

The stator flux linkages in the rotor reference frame combine the magnet contribution and the armature reaction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi_d &= L_d \, i_d + \psi_m \\
\psi_q &= L_q \, i_q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:**
- $\psi_m$ appears *only* on the d-axis because PMs are magnetized along d.
- $L_d$ and $L_q$ are *different* due to flux barriers — this is the **source of saliency**.

### Step 3 — Co-energy Function

For a linear magnetic system, the magnetic co-energy stored in the air gap is:

$$
W'_{co} = \tfrac{1}{2} \left( L_d \, i_d^2 + L_q \, i_q^2 \right) + \psi_m \, i_d
$$

**Reasoning:** First two terms = field energy in inductances; last term = coupling between magnet and d-axis armature current.

### Step 4 — Torque from Co-energy

For a smooth air-gap machine with no saliency in stator, torque is obtained as the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to mechanical angle $\theta_m$, keeping currents constant. In the d-q frame this reduces to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta_m} \bigg|_{i_d, i_q} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{p}{2} \left[ \psi_m \, i_q + (L_d - L_q) \, i_d \, i_q \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Separate the Two Torque Components

Expanding the bracketed term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \underbrace{\tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{p}{2} \cdot \psi_m \, i_q}_{T_{pm} \text{ (Magnet torque)}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{p}{2} \cdot (L_d - L_q) \, i_d \, i_q}_{T_{rel} \text{ (Reluctance torque)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Analytical Insight:**
- $T_{pm}$ is proportional to **$i_q$ alone** (cross-coupling with magnet).
- $T_{rel}$ is proportional to **$i_d \cdot i_q$** — a product term that produces torque only when **both** d- and q-axis currents exist. This is **why we must inject $i_d \neq 0$** in PMSynRM control (MTPA strategy).

### Step 6 — MTPA (Maximum Torque Per Ampere) Operating Condition

Setting $\partial T_e / \partial \beta = 0$ gives the optimum current angle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_{MTPA} = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\frac{-\psi_m + \sqrt{\psi_m^2 + 8(L_d-L_q)^2 I_s^2}}{4(L_d-L_q)I_s}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

At this angle, both torque components are maximized simultaneously for the same stator current — giving the best efficiency.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Constructional Features vs. Performance

| Feature | Effect on $\psi_m$ | Effect on $L_d$ | Effect on $L_q$ | Effect on $\xi = L_q/L_d$ | Net Torque Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Add magnet in d-axis | $\uparrow\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | unchanged | $\downarrow$ slightly | $T_{pm}\uparrow\uparrow$ |
| More flux barriers | unchanged | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow\downarrow$ | $\uparrow\uparrow$ | $T_{rel}\uparrow\uparrow$ |
| Thicker barriers | unchanged | $\downarrow\downarrow$ | $\downarrow\downarrow\downarrow$ | $\uparrow\uparrow\uparrow$ | $T_{rel}\uparrow$ |
| V-shape vs. U-shape | $\uparrow\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | Both torques $\uparrow$ |
| Tangential PM placement | $\uparrow\uparrow\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | unchanged | $\downarrow$ | $T_{pm}\uparrow\uparrow$ |
| Wider bridges | unchanged | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | Both torques $\downarrow$ |

> **Engineering takeaway:** V-shaped or tangential PM placement is the *premium* construction in modern PMSynRMs because it boosts **both** torque components simultaneously while raising $\xi$.

## 3.3 Air-Gap Flux Density Derivation

The average air-gap flux density due to permanent magnets in a PMSynRM rotor is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_g &= B_r \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_g} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \mu_{rec} \cdot \frac{g \cdot l_m}{A_m \cdot \mu_0}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $B_r$ = **remanence** of the magnet (T),
- $A_m$ = magnet cross-sectional area per pole (m²),
- $A_g$ = air-gap area per pole (m²),
- $g$ = mechanical air-gap length (m),
- $l_m$ = magnet thickness (m),
- $\mu_{rec}$ = recoil permeability of magnet (≈ 1.05 $\mu_0$ for NdFeB).

**Interpretation:** Increasing $A_m/A_g$ (more magnet area) or decreasing $g$ raises $B_g$ but reduces $L_d$. Designers must *trade off* magnet torque against reluctance torque — this is the **central design tension** in PMSynRM constructional optimization.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["STATOR ASSEMBLY"] --> A1["Three Phase Stator Core Lamination Stack"]
    A --> A2["Distributed Double Layer Copper Winding"]
    A --> A3["Slot Insulation Class F or Class H"]
    A --> A4["Stator Frame with Cooling Fins"]
    B["AIR GAP MECHANICAL 0.5 to 1.5 MM"] --> C["ROTOR ASSEMBLY"]
    C --> C1["Laminated Anisotropic Rotor Core M19 Silicon Steel"]
    C1 --> C1A["Pole 1 Flux Barriers U Shape or V Shape"]
    C1 --> C1B["Pole 2 Flux Barriers U Shape or V Shape"]
    C1 --> C1C["Pole 3 Flux Barriers U Shape or V Shape"]
    C1 --> C1D["Pole 4 Flux Barriers U Shape or V Shape"]
    C --> C2["Embedded Permanent Magnets NdFeB or SmCo"]
    C --> C3["Mechanical Bridges and Ribs"]
    C --> C4["Non Magnetic Stainless Steel Shaft"]
    D["BEARING AND SENSOR END"] --> D1["Resolver Encoder at Non Drive End"]
    D --> D2["Deep Groove Ball Bearings Sealed"]
    E["HOUSING AND COOLING"] --> E1["Cast Iron or Aluminum End Caps"]
    E --> E2["TEFC Fan or Water Jacket Cooling"]
    F["TORQUE OUTPUT MECHANISM"] --> F1["Combined PM Torque and Reluctance Torque"]
    F1 --> F2["Shaft Output to Load"]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Architecture (4-Pole, 3-Barrier Per Pole)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SP["STATOR PERIPHERY 36 Slots Distributed Winding"]
        s1["Slot 1 Phase A"]
        s2["Slot 2 Phase B"]
        s3["Slot 3 Phase C"]
    end
    subgraph AG["MECHANICAL AIR GAP 1 MM"]
        ag["Uniform Radial Clearance"]
    end
    subgraph RP["ROTOR 4 POLE WITH 3 FLUX BARRIERS PER POLE"]
        p1["Pole 1 d axis Flux Path Through Magnet"]
        b1["Barrier 1 Houses PM1"]
        b2["Barrier 2 Houses PM2"]
        b3["Barrier 3 Houses PM3"]
        p2["Pole 2 d axis Flux Path Through Magnet"]
    end
    SP --> AG --> RP
    p1 --> b1 --> b2 --> b3 --> p2
```

## 4.3 Flux Path Anatomy — D-axis vs. Q-axis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    d1["Stator d axis Teeth"] --> d2["Rotor Pole Body High Permeability Iron"]
    d2 --> d3["Embedded PM NdFeB Magnetizes in d Direction"]
    d3 --> d4["Opposite Pole Body Iron"]
    d4 --> d5["Stator d axis Teeth Opposite Side"]
    q1["Stator q axis Teeth"] --> q2["Rotor q axis Path CROSSES Barriers"]
    q2 --> q3["Barrier 1 Air Pocket HIGH Reluctance"]
    q3 --> q4["Barrier 2 Air Pocket HIGH Reluctance"]
    q4 --> q5["Opposite Stator q axis Teeth"]
    d1 -. SAME ROTOR .-> q1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *d-axis* flux takes the *easy path* through iron + magnet (low reluctance). The *q-axis* flux is *forced through the air pockets* in the flux barriers (high reluctance). The ratio $L_q / L_d > 1$ produces the reluctance torque.

```mermaid

    L1["End Spacer Lamination 0.5 mm"]

    L4["Rotor Lamination N Punched Barriers 0.35 mm"]
    L5["End Spacer Lamination 0.5 mm"]
    L6["Keyway and Magnet Slot Adhesive"]
    L7["Magnets Inserted in Barriers After Stacking"]
    L8["Sleeve Optional Carbon Fiber For Ultra High Speed"]
    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 --> L7 --> L8
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any four salient constructional features of the rotor of a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Reluctance Motor.**

**Model Answer (4 points × 0.75 ≈ 3 marks):**

1. The rotor is **salient-pole** in construction, built from **anisotropic electrical steel laminations** with deliberately punched **flux barriers** to create $L_d \neq L_q$.
2. **Permanent magnets** (typically NdFeB) are **embedded inside the flux barriers**, magnetized along the **d-axis**, providing magnet torque.
3. Each pole contains **multiple flux barriers** (2 to 4 typically), shaped as **U, V, or arc profiles**, to maximize the saliency ratio $\xi = L_q / L_d$.
4. The shaft is made of **non-magnetic stainless steel** to prevent axial short-circuiting of magnet flux, and **mechanical bridges/ribs** (0.5–1.2 mm) hold the lamination segments together.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is meant by saliency ratio, and why is it important in PMSynRM rotor design?**

**Model Answer:**

The **saliency ratio** is defined as $\xi = L_q / L_d$, where $L_d$ and $L_q$ are the d-axis and q-axis synchronous inductances respectively.

**Importance:**
- It **directly determines the reluctance torque** $T_{rel} = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{p}{2} \cdot (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q$, since the difference $(L_q - L_d)$ scales with $\xi$.
- A **higher $\xi$** (typically **6–8** in optimized PMSynRMs) means stronger field-weakening capability and higher torque density without increasing magnet mass.
- It is **controlled by rotor geometry** — number, shape, and angular position of flux barriers — making it the *primary design lever* for rotor construction.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice Option]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat labelled cross-sectional sketch, describe the constructional details of the rotor and stator of a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Reluctance Motor. Explain the role of flux barriers and embedded magnets.

#### Model Solution:

**Stator Construction (3 marks):**

- **Core**: Stacked silicon-steel laminations (M19/M36 grade, 0.5 mm thickness), slotted with semi-closed slots.
- **Slots**: 36, 48, or 60 slots typically, in a peripheral distribution.
- **Winding**: Three-phase, double-layer, short-pitched, distributed winding (5/6 or 2/3 chorded) for sinusoidal MMF.
- **Insulation**: Class F (155 °C) or Class H (180 °C); slot liners and phase separators of epoxy-mica.
- **Frame**: Cast iron or fabricated steel housing with integral cooling fins or water jacket.
- **End windings**: Properly braced and lashed to withstand short-circuit forces.

**Rotor Construction (3 marks):**

- **Lamination**: M19 electrical steel, 0.35 mm thick, with **anisotropic flux barrier pattern** punched by progressive die or laser.
- **Pole count**: $2p = 4, 6$, or $8$ poles.
- **Barriers per pole**: 2 to 4, arranged symmetrically about the d-axis.
- **Barrier shapes**: U-shaped (simple), V-shaped (concentrates flux), arc-shaped (best flux guidance).
- **Magnets**: Sintered NdFeB bars inserted into barrier pockets, magnetized radially in d-direction.
- **Shaft**: Non-magnetic stainless steel (SS-304/316) for axial flux isolation.
- **Bridges**: 0.5–1.2 mm wide iron strips between barriers for mechanical integrity (saturate under load).
- **Banding**: Optional carbon-fiber sleeve for ultra-high-speed (> 15,000 rpm) operation.

**Role of Flux Barriers and Embedded Magnets (1 mark):**

- Flux barriers **block q-axis flux**, raising $L_q / L_d$ and producing reluctance torque.
- Embedded magnets **supply d-axis flux**, adding magnet torque and boosting $B_g$.
- Together they yield **field-weakening range** $I_{ch} = \psi_m / L_d$ close to rated current — enabling wide constant-power operation (up to 4–5× base speed).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for total electromagnetic torque of a PMSynRM. Hence, identify the two torque components and explain the MTPA operating condition.

#### Model Solution:

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Step 1 — Park's transformation of stator current:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_d &= I_s \sin\beta \\
i_q &= I_s \cos\beta
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Flux linkages in rotor frame:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi_d &= L_d \, i_d + \psi_m \\
\psi_q &= L_q \, i_q
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Magnetic co-energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W'_{co} &= \tfrac{1}{2} L_d i_d^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} L_q i_q^2 + \psi_m i_d
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Torque via co-energy derivative:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta_m}\bigg|_{i_d,i_q} \\
&= \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{p}{2} \Big[\psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 5 — Identify components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \underbrace{\tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{p}{2} \cdot \psi_m \, i_q}_{T_{pm}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{p}{2} \cdot (L_d - L_q) \, i_d \, i_q}_{T_{rel}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**MTPA Condition (2 marks):**

For a given current magnitude $I_s$, torque is maximized by choosing the optimal advance angle $\beta_{MTPA}$, found from $\partial T_e / \partial \beta = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_{MTPA} = \cos^{-1}\!\left[\frac{-\psi_m + \sqrt{\psi_m^2 + 8(L_d - L_q)^2 I_s^2}}{4(L_d - L_q) I_s}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

At MTPA, **both torque components operate at their peak ratio**, yielding minimum stator current for a demanded torque — critical for efficiency and inverter rating.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Alternative Internal Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various flux barrier geometries (U, V, arc, tangential) used in PMSynRM rotors. Compare their effects on saliency ratio and torque production.

#### Model Solution:

**U-Shape Barriers (1.5 marks):**
- Simplest geometry; rectangular pocket with curved ends.
- $\xi$ typically 3–5; modest reluctance torque.
- Easy to manufacture; used in low-cost industrial drives.

**V-Shape Barriers (1.5 marks):**
- Barriers angled toward the q-axis, creating a *flux-concentrating* path.
- $\xi$ rises to **5–7**; $L_d$ increases due to magnet concentration.
- Both $T_{pm}$ and $T_{rel}$ improved; **most common in EV traction**.

**Arc-Shape Barriers (1.5 marks):**
- Curved pockets following the air-gap circle; minimum stress concentration.
- $\xi \approx 6$–$8$ (highest); magnets align with flux lines.
- Premium solution, harder to manufacture, laser-cut laminations needed.

**Tangential Magnet Placement (1.5 marks):**
- Magnets oriented tangentially (perpendicular to d-axis), instead of radially.
- Flux from two adjacent poles adds in series → $\psi_m$ **doubles** effectively.
- Best magnet-torque contribution; used in direct-drive wind turbines and large PMSynRMs.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Barrier | $\xi$ | $T_{pm}$ | $T_{rel}$ | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| U-shape | 3–5 | Medium | Medium | Low |
| V-shape | 5–7 | High | High | Medium |
| Arc-shape | 6–8 | High | High | High |
| Tangential PM | 4–6 | Very High | Medium | High |

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 6-pole, 50 Hz, three-phase PMSynRM has $\psi_m = 0.45$ Wb, $L_d = 12$ mH, $L_q = 48$ mH. The motor is operated at MTPA with $I_s = 20$ A. Compute (i) the magnet torque, (ii) the reluctance torque, and (iii) the total electromagnetic torque in N·m.

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- $2p = 6 \Rightarrow p = 3$
- $f = 50$ Hz, $\psi_m = 0.45$ Wb
- $L_d = 12$ mH $= 0.012$ H, $L_q = 48$ mH $= 0.048$ H
- $I_s = 20$ A, MTPA operation

**Step 1 — Compute $\beta_{MTPA}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(L_d - L_q)^2 &= (0.012 - 0.048)^2 = (0.036)^2 = 0.001296 \\
8(L_d - L_q)^2 I_s^2 &= 8 \times 0.001296 \times 400 = 4.1472 \\
\psi_m^2 &= 0.2025 \\
\psi_m^2 + 8(L_d - L_q)^2 I_s^2 &= 0.2025 + 4.1472 = 4.3497 \\
\sqrt{4.3497} &= 2.0856
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos\beta_{MTPA} &= \frac{-0.45 + 2.0856}{4 \times (-0.036) \times 20} = \frac{1.6356}{-2.88} = -0.5679
\end{aligned}
$$

Since we want a positive torque, take $\beta_{MTPA} \approx 124.6°$ — but for torque calculation use $|\cos\beta|$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_d &= I_s \sin\beta \approx 20 \times 0.8232 = 16.46 \text{ A} \\
i_q &= I_s \cos\beta \approx 20 \times (-0.5679) = -11.36 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The negative sign indicates current vector lies in the *flux-weakening* quadrant; for MTPA in motoring mode we consider magnitudes. Standard textbook convention gives:
> $i_d = 16.46$ A, $i_q = 11.36$ A (magnitudes).

**Step 2 — Magnet Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{pm} &= \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{p}{2} \cdot \psi_m \, i_q \\
&= 1.5 \times 1.5 \times 0.45 \times 11.36 \\
&= 2.25 \times 5.112 = 11.50 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Reluctance Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rel} &= \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{p}{2} \cdot (L_d - L_q) \, i_d \, i_q \\
&= 1.5 \times 1.5 \times (-0.036) \times 16.46 \times 11.36 \\
&= 2.25 \times (-0.036) \times 186.97 \\
&= 2.25 \times (-6.731) = -15.15 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking magnitudes: $T_{rel} \approx 15.15$ N·m.

**Step 4 — Total Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= T_{pm} + T_{rel} = 11.50 + 15.15 = 26.65 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answers:** (i) $T_{pm} = 11.50$ N·m, (ii) $T_{rel} = 15.15$ N·m, (iii) $T_e = 26.65$ N·m.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> - **Sign errors** in $L_d - L_q$ (since $L_q > L_d$, the difference is negative). Always carry magnitude consistently.
> - **Missing factor** of $p/2$ in torque equation — KTU deducts 1 mark per occurrence.
> - **Wrong current-angle sign** in MTPA formula — students often forget that for **motoring** mode, $\beta > 90°$ in PMSynRM (unlike PMSM where $\beta < 90°$).
> - **Confusing $L_d$ and $L_q$** — flux barriers make q-axis inductance LARGER than d-axis in PMSynRM (opposite of salient-pole SM).
> - **Ignoring the 3/2 Park transformation factor** — another classic 1-mark deduction.
> - **Units**: Wb × A = V·s × A; must include $p/2$ and 3/2 multipliers to get N·m.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — PMSynRM Constructional Details**

- ✅ PMSynRM = **PM torque + Reluctance torque** hybrid synchronous machine.
- ✅ **Stator is standard** PMSM/induction-motor stator (36/48/60 slots, double-layer winding).
- ✅ **Rotor is bespoke** — anisotropic lamination with punched flux barriers.
- ✅ **Flux barriers** = U, V, or arc shaped; 2–4 per pole; magnetize d-axis path.
- ✅ **Magnets** = NdFeB (N35SH–N42UH) or SmCo, embedded inside barriers, magnetized in **d-direction**.
- ✅ **Saliency ratio** $\xi = L_q / L_d > 1$ (key design metric, range **2–8**).
- ✅ **Total torque** $T_e = \tfrac{3}{2} \cdot \tfrac{p}{2} \cdot [\psi_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q]$.
- ✅ **MTPA condition** requires both $i_d \neq 0$ and $i_q \neq 0$ simultaneously.
- ✅ **Shaft** = non-magnetic stainless steel (axial flux isolation).
- ✅ **Bridges/ribs** = 0.5–1.2 mm mechanical support; saturate magnetically.
- ✅ **Tangential PM placement** doubles effective $\psi_m$; preferred for large machines.
- ✅ **V-shape barriers** are the *industry-preferred* geometry (EV traction, servo drives).
- ✅ **Field-weakening range** governed by characteristic current $I_{ch} = \psi_m / L_d$.
- ✅ **Position feedback** mandatory: resolver or encoder at NDE.
- ✅ **Cooling**: TEFC for < 50 kW; water jacket for > 50 kW industrial drives.
- ✅ **Air-gap length** $g$ = 0.5–1.5 mm; smaller $g$ boosts $B_g$ but reduces $L_d$.
- ✅ **Lamination thickness**: 0.35 mm for high-frequency drives; 0.5 mm for 50/60 Hz industrial.
- ✅ **No rotor copper losses** → higher efficiency than induction motor of same rating.
- ✅ **Speed range**: typically up to **5× base speed** in constant-power region.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT638 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND DRIVES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Principle of Operation # Principle of Operation — Stepper Motors (Module 4 Core Concept) ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A **Stepper Motor** is a brushless, synchronous electric machine that converts a train of input electrical pulses into a precisely defined angular (or linear) mechanical displacement called a *step*. Unlike conventional DC/AC motors that rotate continuously, the rotor of a stepper motor moves in **discrete angular increments** (typical step angles: 1.8°, 7.5°, 15°, 30°, 45°, 90°), with each pulse advancing the rotor by one step. The **principle of operation** is rooted in the interaction between two fundamental electromagnetic laws: 1. **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** — a current-carrying coil produces a magnetomotive force (MMF). 2. **The Principle of Minimum Reluctance** — magnetic flux always seeks the path of **lowest reluctance**; the rotor aligns itself to minimize the air-gap reluctance of the magnetic circuit. | Term | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Significance | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | Step Angle | $\theta_s$ | degrees (°) / rad | Angular displacement per pulse | | Stepping Frequency | $f_s$ | pulses per second (pps) | Rate of input pulses | | Holding Torque | $T_h$ | N·m | Maximum static torque with phases energized | | Detent Torque | $T_d$ | N·m | Residual torque with phases unenergized (PM motors) | | Synchronous Speed | $N_s$ | rpm | Mechanical speed at given $f_s$ | | **Air-Gap Flux Density** | $B_g$ | **Tesla (T)** | Flux density in working air-gap | | **Permeability of free space** | $\mu_0$ | **$4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m** | Fundamental magnetic constant | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Compass and the Lock":** > Imagine you have **four compass needles** (representing the rotor teeth) placed inside a **square tube** (the stator). Now imagine four electromagnets placed around the outside of the tube, pointing inward. When you switch on **Electromagnet 1**, all compasses snap to point at it. When you switch it OFF and switch on **Electromagnet 2** (located 90° around), the compasses snap to point at the new magnet, rotating 90° in the process. By sequencing the electromagnets (1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 1...), the rotor "steps" around the stator — exactly like a clock hand moving one tick at a time. This is precisely the working principle of a **Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor**. A simpler analogy: a stepper motor is like a **digital-to-mechanical converter** — the *digital pulses* in (the on/off switching of stator phases) are converted into *analog angular position* out (the discrete mechanical rotation of the shaft). ## 1.3 Physical Layout & Operating Context The stator carries a set of **multi-toothed pole pieces** (concentrated windings excited by a DC supply, sequentially switched by an electronic driver). The rotor is either: - a **plain toothed soft-iron rotor** (Variable Reluctance type), or - a **permanent-magnet rotor** (PM type), or - a **hybrid combination** (axially magnetized rotor with two toothed caps displaced by half a tooth pitch — Hybrid type). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Exam Highlight:** > A stepper motor is classified as an **open-loop position control device**. No feedback sensor (encoder/resolver) is required for position control — the rotor position is *inferred* directly from the pulse count. This makes it indispensable in CNC machines, 3D printers, robotics, disk drives, and X-Y plotters. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Logic The principle of operation of any stepper motor (regardless of type) follows this four-stage logic: 1. **Energization of a Stator Phase** → An MMF is established in the stator pole. By Faraday's law, the phase current produces a magnetic field. 2. **Flux Path Formation** → Magnetic flux crosses the air-gap from stator tooth to rotor tooth along a path of minimum reluctance. 3. **Rotor Alignment (Torque Production)** → The rotor experiences a torque that drives it to the position where the air-gap reluctance is **minimum** (i.e., where the rotor tooth is *directly aligned* with the energized stator tooth). 4. **Pulse Sequencing** → The driver de-energizes the present phase and energizes the next phase in sequence. The flux path shifts, and the rotor "steps" to the new minimum-reluctance position. > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" of Stepper Motion:** > The rotor is essentially "chasing" the moving magnetic field of the stator — but in **discrete jumps**, not continuously. The size of each jump is determined by the *number of stator poles* and *number of rotor teeth* (or magnetic pole-pairs, in PM motors). ## 2.2 Step Angle — The Heart of Stepper Math The **step angle** is the most critical parameter. For a multi-stack or toothed stepper motor: $$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_r \times N_s}$$ where: - $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth (or rotor pole-pairs in PM type) - $N_s$ = number of stator teeth (or stator pole-pairs) > [!TIP] > **Alternative form for Variable Reluctance (VR) Motors:** > $$\theta_s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s \cdot N_r} \times 360°$$ > where $N_s$ and $N_r$ are the *number of teeth on stator and rotor* respectively. This expression is **highly tested** in KTU 2024 university exams. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **board-exam-grade** and have appeared in previous KTU ESE papers. Master these — they cover 80% of numerical questions in Module 4. | # | Formula | Description / Use Case | Units | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r \, N_s}$ | Step angle (multi-stack / hybrid) | degrees | | 2 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{(N_s - N_r) \cdot 360°}{N_s \, N_r}$ | Step angle (single-stack VR motor) | degrees | | 3 | $N = f_s \times \theta_s \times \dfrac{60}{360} = \dfrac{f_s \, \theta_s}{6}$ | Rotor speed in rpm | rpm | | 4 | $T_h = \dfrac{1}{2} \, i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Holding torque (general reluctance form) | N·m | | 5 | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l_g}{\mu_0 \, A_g}$ | Air-gap reluctance | A-t/Wb | | 6 | $W_{co} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mathcal{R} \phi^2$ | Co-energy stored in air-gap | Joules | | 7 | $T_e = \dfrac{\partial W_{co}}{\partial \theta} \bigg\rvert_{i=\text{const}}$ | Electromagnetic torque (energy method) | N·m | | 8 | $f_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K_t}{J}}$ | Natural mechanical resonance frequency | Hz | | 9 | $P_{\text{out}} = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Mechanical output power | Watts | | 10 | $n_{\text{steps/rev}} = \dfrac{360°}{\theta_s}$ | Number of steps per revolution | dimensionless | > **Constants to memorize (highlighted by KTU examiners):** > - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$ > - $1 \text{ Tesla} = 1 \text{ Wb/m}^2$ > - $\theta_s \text{ in rad} = \theta_s \text{ (in °)} \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}$ ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Stepper motors are the **workhorse of open-loop precision motion control**. Their principle of operation enables: - **Digital CNC machines** — every "G-code step" → one electrical pulse → one mechanical step. - **3D printers** — precise filament extrusion with sub-millimeter accuracy. - **Robotic actuators** — joint positioning without expensive feedback sensors. - **Quartz watch timing** — a 180 pps pulse train drives a 1.8° stepper at 6 rpm. - **Hard disk head positioning** — accurate read/write head placement on tracks. > [!TIP] > **Industrial Insight:** > The fact that stepper motors operate **open-loop** is a double-edged sword: it reduces system cost and complexity, but a missed step (due to excessive load torque) results in **permanent position error** that the controller cannot detect — this is the #1 reason servo motors are preferred in high-precision industrial robotics. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Step Angle of a Multi-Stack / Hybrid Stepper Motor **Given:** A hybrid stepper motor has $N_r$ rotor teeth per stack and $N_s$ stator teeth per stack. Two stacks are axially displaced by half a tooth pitch $\left( \dfrac{\tau}{2} \right)$. **To Derive:** The step angle $\theta_s$. **Step 1 — Define the rotor tooth pitch (mechanical angle between adjacent teeth):** $$\theta_r = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$ **Step 2 — Define the stator tooth pitch:** $$\theta_s^{\text{(pitch)}} = \frac{360°}{N_s}$$ > A single tooth pitch is the angular distance from one stator tooth center to the next. **Step 3 — Compute the angular misalignment per step:** When the energization shifts by one stator tooth, the rotor must move by the difference between the stator and rotor tooth pitches: $$\Delta\theta = \theta_s^{\text{(pitch)}} - \theta_r = \frac{360°}{N_s} - \frac{360°}{N_r}$$ **Step 4 — Algebraic simplification:** $$\Delta\theta = 360° \left( \frac{1}{N_s} - \frac{1}{N_r} \right) = 360° \cdot \frac{N_r - N_s}{N_s \, N_r}$$ **Step 5 — Recognize that each electrical pulse produces an increment of $\Delta\theta$:** $$\boxed{\theta_s = 360° \cdot \frac{N_r - N_s}{N_s \, N_r} = \frac{360°}{N_s} - \frac{360°}{N_r}}$$ **Step 6 — Magnitude convention (drop the sign, since angle is positive):** $$\theta_s = \left| 360° \cdot \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s \, N_r} \right|$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **standard KTU formula** for the step angle of a single-stack variable reluctance (VR) stepper motor. For a hybrid motor, the formula $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r N_s}$ is used (where $N_r$ and $N_s$ are the number of rotor and stator teeth, and the half-tooth offset is already built in). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Synchronous Rotor Speed from Stepping Frequency **Given:** A stepper motor has step angle $\theta_s$ (in degrees) and is fed by a pulse train of frequency $f_s$ (pulses per second). **To Derive:** The rotor speed in rpm. **Step 1 — Number of pulses per second = $f_s$.** Each pulse produces one step of $\theta_s$ degrees. **Step 2 — Angular velocity in degrees per second:** $$\omega_{\text{deg/s}} = f_s \cdot \theta_s$$ **Step 3 — Convert degrees per second to revolutions per second:** $$\omega_{\text{rev/s}} = \frac{f_s \cdot \theta_s}{360°}$$ **Step 4 — Convert revolutions per second to revolutions per minute (multiply by 60):** $$\boxed{N \text{ (rpm)} = \frac{f_s \cdot \theta_s \cdot 60}{360} = \frac{f_s \cdot \theta_s}{6}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Electromagnetic Torque from the Reluctance Principle **Given:** A stator phase winding has inductance $L(\theta)$ that varies with rotor position $\theta$. The phase carries current $i$. **To Derive:** The electromagnetic torque $T_e$. **Step 1 — Energy stored in the magnetic field of one phase:** $$W_f = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \, i^2$$ **Step 2 — Co-energy (defined for convenient partial differentiation in terms of $i$ and $\theta$):** $$W_{co} = \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \, i^2 \quad \text{(for linear magnetic system)}$$ **Step 3 — Electromagnetic torque is the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to angular displacement (at constant current):** $$T_e = \frac{\partial W_{co}}{\partial \theta} \bigg\rvert_{i = \text{const}}$$ **Step 4 — Substituting and differentiating:** $$T_e = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} \left( \frac{1}{2} L(\theta) \, i^2 \right) = \frac{1}{2} \, i^2 \, \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$ $$\boxed{T_e = \frac{1}{2} i^2 \frac{dL}{d\theta}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Torque is proportional to the **square of current** (so direction of current doesn't matter — this is why a unipolar DC drive works) AND to the **rate of change of inductance** with respect to rotor position. The motor naturally develops a positive (or negative) torque depending on whether $\dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ is positive or negative. ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Board-Exam Style) **Problem:** A 3-stack variable reluctance stepper motor has 12 rotor teeth and 8 stator teeth per stack. The motor is fed with a pulse train of 240 pulses per second. Calculate: 1. The step angle. 2. The rotor speed in rpm. 3. The number of steps required to complete one full revolution. ### Solution **Part (a) — Step Angle:** Using the formula $\theta_s = 360° \cdot \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s \, N_r}$: $$\theta_s = 360° \times \frac{|8 - 12|}{8 \times 12} = 360° \times \frac{4}{96} = 360° \times 0.04167$$ $$\boxed{\theta_s = 15°}$$ **Part (b) — Rotor Speed:** $$N = \frac{f_s \cdot \theta_s}{6} = \frac{240 \times 15}{6} = \frac{3600}{6}$$ $$\boxed{N = 600 \text{ rpm}}$$ **Part (c) — Steps per Revolution:** $$n = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{15°}$$ $$\boxed{n = 24 \text{ steps/rev}}$$ > [!TIP] > **Verification:** $N = f_s / n = 240 / 24 = 10$ rev/s $= 600$ rpm. ✓ (Consistent with Part b) ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Stepper Motor Types > [!IMPORTANT] > This comparison table is **frequently asked** in KTU 2024 ESE as a 7-mark sub-question. | Parameter | Variable Reluctance (VR) | Permanent Magnet (PM) | Hybrid (HB) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Rotor** | Soft iron, toothed (no magnet) | Permanent-magnet (2 poles typical) | Toothed soft-iron with axially magnetized core | | **Stator** | Laminated, multi-toothed | Multi-toothed with concentrated coils | Multi-toothed with concentrated coils | | **Detent Torque** | None (no PM) | High (due to rotor magnet) | Medium (small detent) | | **Step Angle Range** | 15°, 30°, 45° (coarse) | 45°, 90° (very coarse) | 0.9°, 1.8°, 3.75° (fine) | | **Holding Torque** | Low–Medium | Medium | **Highest** | | **Inertia** | Lowest | Highest | Medium | | **Step Resolution** | Low (coarse steps) | Very Low | **High (fine steps)** | | **Application** | Hard disk head positioning, low-cost printers | Inexpensive fans, indicators | CNC, robotics, 3D printers, medical equipment | | **Operating Principle** | Minimum reluctance seeking | Magnet-pole alignment with stator field | Combined: reluctance + magnetic alignment | | **Cost** | Lowest | Medium | **Highest** | # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stepper Motor Drive System The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete open-loop stepper motor drive system, illustrating how the "principle of operation" is realized end-to-end in a real industrial system. ```mermaid flowchart TD PULSE_SRC[Step Pulse Generator
Microcontroller OR PLC] --> COUNTER[Pulse Counter
Tracks Position] PULSE_SRC --> DRIVER[Power Driver Stage
Transistor H-Bridge Array] DRIVER --> PHASE_A[Stator Phase A
Winding Energized] DRIVER --> PHASE_B[Stator Phase B
Winding Energized] DRIVER --> PHASE_C[Stator Phase C
Winding Energized] DRIVER --> PHASE_D[Stator Phase D
Winding Energized] PHASE_A --> MAG_FIELD[Rotating Stator MMF
Establishes Magnetic Field] PHASE_B --> MAG_FIELD PHASE_C --> MAG_FIELD PHASE_D --> MAG_FIELD MAG_FIELD --> AIR_GAP[Air-Gap Flux Coupling
Minimum Reluctance Path] AIR_GAP --> ROTOR[Rotor
Toothed PM OR Iron Core] ROTOR --> SHAFT[Mechanical Shaft
Outputs Stepped Motion] ROTOR -.-> TORQUE[Electromagnetic Torque
Te = 0.5 i2 dL by dTheta] COUNTER -.-> FEEDBACK[Open-Loop Position
Inferred From Pulse Count] SHAFT --> LOAD[Mechanical Load
CNC Spindle / Printer Head] ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram:** Follow the solid arrows for the **power flow** (pulse → driver → phases → magnetic field → rotor → shaft → load). The dotted arrows indicate the **information flow** (torque generation and open-loop position tracking). No physical feedback wire exists from the rotor to the controller — this is what defines the stepper motor as an **open-loop actuator**. ## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Variable Reluctance Stepper (2-Phase, 4-Pole) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph STATOR[Stator Assembly 4 Salient Poles] S1[Stator Pole N1
Phase A Coil] S2[Stator Pole S1
Phase A Coil] S3[Stator Pole N2
Phase B Coil] S4[Stator Pole S2
Phase B Coil] end subgraph ROTOR[Rotor Assembly Toothed Iron] T1[Rotor Tooth 1] T2[Rotor Tooth 2] T3[Rotor Tooth 3] T4[Rotor Tooth 4] end S1 ==Min Reluctance Path==> T1 S2 ==Flux Return Path==> T2 S3 ==When Energized==> T3 S4 ==When Energized==> T4 T1 --> ROT_MOVE[Rotor Rotates
By Step Angle Theta S] T2 --> ROT_MOVE T3 --> ROT_MOVE T4 --> ROT_MOVE ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid:** When Phase A is energized (S1 = North, S2 = South), the rotor tooth T1 aligns with S1 and T2 aligns with S2 — achieving minimum reluctance. When Phase B is energized next, the rotor advances by 90° (in a 4-pole configuration) to align T3 with S3 and T4 with S4. ## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — Step Sequencing Logic ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Phase_A_Active Phase_A_Active --> Phase_B_Active : Pulse 2 Received Phase_B_Active --> Phase_C_Active : Pulse 3 Received Phase_C_Active --> Phase_D_Active : Pulse 4 Received Phase_D_Active --> Phase_A_Active : Pulse 5 Cycle Repeats Phase_A_Active --> Phase_D_Active : Reverse Pulse Phase_D_Active --> Phase_C_Active : Reverse Pulse Phase_C_Active --> Phase_B_Active : Reverse Pulse Phase_B_Active --> Phase_A_Active : Reverse Pulse Phase_A_Active --> [*] : Power Removed ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** This is a 4-phase full-step excitation sequence. The forward direction (1→2→3→4→1) produces **clockwise rotation**; the reverse direction produces **counter-clockwise rotation**. Half-step excitation is achieved by inserting intermediate states where two adjacent phases are energized simultaneously. ## 4.4 Torque vs. Stepping Frequency Characteristic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Pull-In Torque Curve
Safe Operating Envelope] --> A1[Low Frequency Region
Torque is Maximum] A --> A2[Mid Frequency Region
Torque Drops Smoothly] A --> A3[High Frequency Region
Near Resonance Notch] B[Slew Range Torque Curve
Maximum Acceleration Limit] --> A C[Sleeve Region - Torque Limited] --> B D[Start Stop Region] --> C E[Pull Out Torque Maximum] --> D style A fill:#E8F4FD style B fill:#FDF2E9 style C fill:#E8F8E8 style D fill:#FDEEEE style E fill:#FFF9E6 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation (KTU favorite):** The pull-in curve defines the **safe start/stop region** (motor can start, run, and stop synchronously without losing steps). The pull-out curve defines the **slewing region** (motor is already rotating; it can accelerate/decelerate up to this torque). The two curves merge at the **maximum pull-out torque** point. Exceeding the pull-in curve results in **lost synchronism** — the rotor stalls. # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Model answers are calibrated to the **exact depth** expected by a KTU 2024 external examiner for 3-mark sub-questions (approximately 80–120 words, one or two key formulas, no lengthy derivations). ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a stepper motor. What is meant by "step angle" and "holding torque"? **Model Answer:** A **stepper motor** is a brushless synchronous electric machine that converts discrete electrical pulses into precise angular mechanical displacements. The **step angle** $\theta_s$ is the angular increment by which the rotor moves for each input pulse, given by $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r \, N_s}$ (or $\dfrac{(N_s - N_r) \times 360°}{N_s \, N_r}$ for a VR motor). The **holding torque** $T_h$ is the maximum static torque the motor can develop on its shaft with the rated current flowing in the energized phase winding while the rotor is stationary. It is also called the **static torque** and represents the maximum load torque the motor can resist without rotation in the energized state. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the principle of operation of a variable reluctance (VR) stepper motor. **Model Answer:** The **variable reluctance (VR) stepper motor** operates on the **principle of minimum reluctance**. When a stator phase winding is energized, it produces a magnetomotive force (MMF) that drives magnetic flux across the air-gap. The flux always seeks the path of **lowest reluctance** ($\mathcal{R} = l_g / \mu_0 A_g$). The toothed soft-iron rotor therefore experiences a torque and rotates to align one of its teeth with the energized stator tooth, minimizing the air-gap reluctance. As the driver sequentially energizes different stator phases, the rotor continuously "seeks" the new minimum-reluctance position, producing discrete steps of rotation. The step angle depends on the number of rotor and stator teeth. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation**, each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student must answer **one full question** (with both sub-parts). An **internal choice** is provided between two full questions. --- ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **A(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working principle of a Permanent Magnet (PM) stepper motor**. Derive the expression for the step angle. **(7 Marks)** **A(b)** A **4-phase permanent magnet stepper motor** has 50 rotor teeth. The stator is excited with a pulse frequency of 500 pulses per second. Calculate: 1. The step angle in degrees. 2. The rotor speed in rpm. 3. The time taken for the shaft to complete one revolution. 4. The number of pulses required to rotate the shaft through 75°. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution — A(a)** **Construction of a PM Stepper Motor:** A PM stepper motor consists of a **stator** with a set of multi-toothed salient poles carrying concentrated windings, and a **rotor** that is a **radially magnetized permanent magnet** (typically 2-pole, but can be 4-pole or more for finer steps). The stator has two (or four) phase windings, each wound on diametrically opposite poles. The rotor magnet produces its own constant field. **Working Principle:** When a stator phase is energized with DC current, the stator produces an electromagnetic field of a defined polarity (N or S). The rotor PM aligns its **South pole** with the stator **North pole** (and vice versa), producing a torque that rotates the rotor to the new alignment position. When the next phase is energized, the rotor advances by one **step angle** to align with the new field. **Step Angle Derivation for PM Motor:** For a multi-pole PM stepper motor with $N_r$ rotor poles (pole-pairs): $$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_r \times N_{\text{phases}}}$$ For the simplest 2-pole, 2-phase PM motor: $\theta_s = 90°$. > [!TIP] > **Remember:** PM stepper motors have **large step angles** (typically 45° to 90°) because the rotor has only a small number of magnetic poles. This makes them suitable for low-resolution applications like fans and indicators. #### **Model Solution — A(b)** **Given:** $N_r = 50$ rotor teeth, $N_s = 4$ stator phases, $f_s = 500$ pps. For a multi-pole PM stepper motor with rotor teeth $N_r$ and phases, treating rotor teeth as discrete pole pairs: **Part 1 — Step Angle:** $$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_r} = \frac{360°}{50}$$ $$\boxed{\theta_s = 7.2°}$$ **Part 2 — Rotor Speed:** $$N = \frac{f_s \cdot \theta_s}{6} = \frac{500 \times 7.2}{6}$$ $$\boxed{N = 600 \text{ rpm}}$$ **Part 3 — Time for One Revolution:** $$T = \frac{60}{N} = \frac{60}{600}$$ $$\boxed{T = 0.1 \text{ seconds} = 100 \text{ ms}}$$ **Part 4 — Number of Pulses for 75° Rotation:** $$n = \frac{75°}{\theta_s} = \frac{75°}{7.2°}$$ $$\boxed{n = 10.42 \approx 11 \text{ pulses}}$$ --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **B(a)** Explain the **construction and principle of operation of a Hybrid (HB) stepper motor** with a suitable diagram. Compare its performance with VR and PM stepper motors. **(7 Marks)** **B(b)** A **hybrid stepper motor** has 200 rotor teeth and 200 stator teeth arranged in a 2-stack configuration. Calculate: 1. The step angle. 2. The number of steps required for one complete revolution. 3. The pulse frequency required to achieve a rotor speed of 1200 rpm. 4. The shaft position (in degrees) after receiving 350 pulses. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution — B(a)** **Construction of Hybrid Stepper Motor:** A hybrid stepper motor combines features of both VR and PM designs. The rotor consists of **two toothed soft-iron caps** mounted on a **shaft**, with a **permanent magnet** sandwiched between them. The magnet axially magnetizes the two rotor caps in opposite polarities (one cap is North, the other is South). The two caps are mechanically displaced by **half a tooth pitch** ($\tau/2$) relative to each other. The stator has multi-toothed salient poles with concentrated windings. **Principle of Operation:** When a stator phase is energized, the resulting magnetic field interacts with **both** rotor caps. The N-cap and S-cap experience torques in opposite rotational directions, but because they are offset by $\tau/2$, the net rotor torque is **non-zero** and the rotor advances by a **small step angle** (typically 1.8° for a 200-tooth rotor). The fine tooth geometry provides **high resolution**. **Comparison Table:** (Refer to Section 3.5 Analytical Matrix above for the complete 10-row comparison.) **Key distinguishing points for the 7-mark answer:** - **HB > VR** in holding torque and step resolution. - **HB > PM** in step resolution and finer steps. - **HB has detent torque** (small, due to PM) — useful for position holding at zero current. - **HB is more expensive** than both VR and PM. #### **Model Solution — B(b)** **Given:** $N_r = 200$, $N_s = 200$, 2-stack hybrid configuration. **Part 1 — Step Angle (using hybrid formula $\theta_s = 360° / (N_r N_s)$, where the half-tooth offset is inherent):** $$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_r} = \frac{360°}{200}$$ $$\boxed{\theta_s = 1.8°}$$ > [!NOTE] > For a 2-stack hybrid motor with matched teeth counts, the simplified form $\theta_s = 360°/N_r$ is used because the second stack contributes a half-tooth advance that effectively doubles the resolution — making 200 rotor teeth yield 200 steps/rev at 1.8° each. (This is the industry-standard NEMA 17 / NEMA 23 motor specification.) **Part 2 — Number of Steps per Revolution:** $$n = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{1.8°}$$ $$\boxed{n = 200 \text{ steps/rev}}$$ **Part 3 — Pulse Frequency for $N = 1200$ rpm:** Rearranging $N = f_s \cdot \theta_s / 6$: $$f_s = \frac{6 \cdot N}{\theta_s} = \frac{6 \times 1200}{1.8}$$ $$\boxed{f_s = 4000 \text{ pulses per second (pps)}}$$ **Part 4 — Shaft Position after 350 Pulses:** $$\theta_{\text{total}} = 350 \times 1.8° = 630°$$ Reducing modulo 360° (since one full revolution = 360°): $$\theta_{\text{final}} = 630° - 360° = 270°$$ $$\boxed{\theta_{\text{final}} = 270°}$$ (Equivalently, $350 \mod 200 = 150$ steps past one full revolution, so angle = $150 \times 1.8° = 270°$.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign error in step angle formula:** The expression $\theta_s = \dfrac{(N_s - N_r) \cdot 360°}{N_s N_r}$ uses the **absolute value**. Students often forget the modulus and get a negative answer. > 2. **Unit mismatch in $f_s$:** Pulse frequency is in **pulses per second (pps)**, not pulses per minute. Mixing these units gives answers off by a factor of 60. > 3. **Forgetting that PM and HB motors have detent torque** but **VR motors do not.** This is a 1-mark conceptual question that catches many students. > 4. **Confusing the open-loop nature:** Stepper motors do **not** need encoders. Writing about feedback sensors in a stepper answer is a marking red flag. > 5. **Sketch quality:** KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for poorly drawn or unlabeled diagrams. Always label the stator poles, rotor teeth, MMF direction (using arrows), and the energization sequence. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Yield Bullet Summary for Last-Minute Revision:** - **Stepper motor = open-loop digital-to-mechanical converter.** No feedback sensor required. - **Operating principle** = electromagnetic torque drives rotor to **minimum reluctance** (VR type) OR **magnetic alignment with PM poles** (PM type) OR **both** (HB type). - **Step angle formulas (MUST memorize):** - VR motor: $\theta_s = 360° (N_s - N_r) / (N_s N_r)$ - HB / multi-stack: $\theta_s = 360° / (N_r N_s)$ or $\theta_s = 360° / N_r$ (for 2-stack with offset) - PM motor: $\theta_s = 360° / N_{\text{pole-pairs}}$ - **Speed relation:** $N \text{ (rpm)} = f_s \cdot \theta_s / 6$ - **Torque equation:** $T_e = 0.5 \, i^2 \, dL/d\theta$ (reluctance form) - **Co-energy method:** $T_e = \partial W_{co} / \partial \theta \, \vert_{i=\text{const}}$ - **Three types:** VR (lowest cost, coarse steps, no detent) — PM (medium cost, very coarse steps, has detent) — HB (highest cost, finest steps, has detent). - **Detent torque** is present only in motors with a permanent magnet rotor (PM and HB). - **Holding torque** is the static torque with one or more phases energized. - **Pull-in curve** defines the safe start-stop region; **pull-out curve** defines the slewing region. - **Lost synchronism** occurs when load torque exceeds pull-in or pull-out torque. - **Air-gap reluctance:** $\mathcal{R} = l_g / (\mu_0 A_g)$ — **always minimize this** for the energized phase. - **Constant to remember:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$. - **Industrial applications:** CNC machines, 3D printers, robotics, disk drives, plotters, quartz watches. - **Hybrid motors are the industry standard** for modern precision motion control (NEMA 17, NEMA 23 frames). - **KTU 2024 favourite numerical:** "Given $N_r$ and $N_s$, find $\theta_s$ and the rpm at a given $f_s$." Practice at least 3 variations. - **Always draw the construction diagram** with labeled poles, teeth, MMF arrows, and rotor direction in descriptive answers.

topics.title: Different Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT638 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Different Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Special Electrical Machines and Drives**, the term **"Different Topologies"** refers to the various power electronic converter configurations, winding arrangements, and circuit architectures used to excite and control **Stepper Motors**, **Switched Reluctance Motors (SRM)**, **Permanent Magnet Brushless DC (BLDC) motors**, and **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM)**. 

A *topology* defines the structural interconnection of switching devices (transistors, MOSFETs, IGBTs), freewheeling diodes, DC link components, and phase windings that determines:
1. The current waveform shape impressed on the phase winding
2. The magnitude and direction of torque produced
3. The energy recovery mechanism during phase commutation
4. The number of switches per phase (cost vs. performance trade-off)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** Module 4 specifically focuses on the **drive-side power converter topologies** that interface the low-voltage logic control signals with the high-current machine phase windings. The choice of topology directly affects dynamic response, torque ripple, switching losses, and hardware complexity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Plumbing Network**
> Think of a special electrical machine as a **water pump system** and the drive topology as the **valve and pipe arrangement** that controls water flow:
> - The **DC bus** is the *main water tank* (energy reservoir at fixed pressure).
> - The **machine phase winding** is a *delivery pipe* that needs precisely controlled flow.
> - The **switches (transistors)** are *electronic valves* that open/close to direct water.
> - The **freewheeling diodes** are *check valves* that allow water to recirculate safely when the main valve shuts.
> - The **capacitors/inductors** are *buffers* that smooth pressure surges.
>
> Just as different plumbing layouts (single pipe vs. dual pipe vs. H-pipe network) deliver water differently, different **H-bridge, half-bridge, or bifilar topologies** deliver current to the motor windings differently — each suited for a particular performance goal.

## 1.3 Why "Topologies" Matter in Special Machines

Special machines differ from conventional induction motors because:
- They have **salient poles / variable reluctance paths** requiring precise phase excitation.
- They often need **unipolar** vs. **bipolar** current control.
- They suffer from **high inductance**, which slows current rise/fall — requiring special *forced commutation* circuits.

Hence, the **drive topology** is *as critical* as the machine design itself.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC bus voltage | $V_{dc}$ | **24, 48, 100, 200** | V |
| Phase current (rated) | $I_{ph}$ | **1 – 10** | A |
| Switching frequency | $f_{sw}$ | **10 – 50** | kHz |
| Electrical time constant | $\tau = L/R$ | **0.5 – 10** | ms |
| Holding torque | $T_h$ | **0.1 – 20** | N·m |
