# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Topologies (Hierarchical Logic)

Special machine drive topologies can be classified along three orthogonal axes:

**Axis A — By Machine Type:**
- Stepper Motor Topologies
- Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) Topologies
- BLDC/PMSM Topologies

**Axis B — By Current Direction Capability:**
- **Unipolar** (current flows in one direction only through a winding tap)
- **Bipolar** (current can flow in both directions through a single winding)

**Axis C — By Energy Recovery Method:**
- **Dissipative** (energy lost in resistor — R-dump)
- **Reactive** (energy returned to source — regenerative)
- **Storage** (energy stored in capacitor/inductor — C-dump, bifilar)

## 2.2 Stepper Motor Drive Topologies (Detailed)

### 2.2.1 Unipolar Drive Topology (L/R Drive)
- Uses a **center-tapped winding**; only half the winding is energized at a time.
- Each phase needs only **one switching transistor**.
- Cheapest, but produces only **half the torque** of bipolar (since unipolar utilizes only half the copper).

### 2.2.2 Bipolar Drive Topology (H-Bridge)
- A full **H-bridge (4 switches + 4 diodes)** drives each phase.
- Current reverses direction by toggling diagonal switch pairs.
- Produces **full torque**; standard for hybrid stepper motors.

### 2.2.3 Constant-Current Chopper Drive (Bipolar Chopper)
- Adds **current sensing + hysteresis/PWM control** to the H-bridge.
- Forces current to a *reference value* $I_{ref}$ regardless of speed/load.
- Enables **high-speed operation** (overcomes back-EMF and $L \cdot di/dt$ drop).

### 2.2.4 Microstepping Drive Topology
- Uses **sinusoidally-weighted current** in the two phases of a hybrid stepper.
- Produces $N$ microsteps per full step (e.g., $N = 10, 50, 250$).
- Achieves **sub-micron positional resolution** and eliminates resonance.

## 2.3 Switched Reluctance Motor (SRM) Drive Topologies (Detailed)

### 2.3.1 Asymmetric Half-Bridge Converter (Industry Standard)
- Each phase has **two switches and two freewheeling diodes**.
- Phases are **magnetically and electrically independent** — fault tolerant.
- Independent current control → **excellent for high-performance SRM drives**.

### 2.3.2 Symmetric Half-Bridge (Lower Switch Count)
- Switches are shared between phases → **fewer devices** but **coupled phases**.
- Used in cost-sensitive applications; loses independence.

### 2.3.3 R-Dump (Resistive Dump) Converter
- Single switch per phase; excess energy dumped in a **resistor** via a freewheeling diode.
- Simplest, cheapest, but **energy inefficient** (heat loss).

### 2.3.4 C-Dump Converter
- Single switch per phase; energy stored temporarily in a **capacitor** before returning to source.
- Better efficiency than R-dump; more complex control.

### 2.3.5 Bifilar Winding Drive
- Phase winding has a **bifilar (two parallel wound) secondary**.
- Energy trapped in leakage inductance is forced through the secondary to a dump circuit.
- Eliminates need for separate freewheeling diodes; but increases machine manufacturing complexity.

## 2.4 BLDC / PMSM Drive Topology (Brief)

Standard **three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** with **six switches (MOSFETs/IGBTs)** arranged in three half-bridge legs. Commutation is either:
- **Six-step (trapezoidal)** — for BLDC with rectangular back-EMF.
- **Sinusoidal (SVPWM / SPWM)** — for PMSM with sinusoidal back-EMF.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $i(t) = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{R}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Current rise in L/R drive | A |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Electrical time constant | s |
| 3 | $t_{rise} \approx \tau \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - I_{ref}\,R/V_{dc}}\right)$ | Time to reach reference current | s |
| 4 | $T_{em} = \dfrac{1}{2} i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ | Electromagnetic torque (stepper/SRM) | N·m |
| 5 | $E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ | Energy stored in phase inductance | J |
| 6 | $V_{dc} = R\,i + L\dfrac{di}{dt} + e_{back}$ | Phase voltage equation | V |
| 7 | $P_{loss,R-dump} = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2 f_{sw}$ | Power dissipated in R-dump per phase | W |
| 8 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K_t\,N\,r}{J}}$ | Stepper mechanical resonance freq | Hz |
| 9 | $\theta_{step}^{micro} = \dfrac{\theta_{step}}{N}$ | Microstep angle | rad |
| 10 | $\eta_{drive} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$ | Drive efficiency | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook for Examinations:** For every topology, always identify **(a) switch count per phase, (b) energy return path, (c) phase independence, (d) torque capability (full or half).**

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Topology | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| Unipolar L/R | Low-cost printers, toys, digital clocks |
| Bipolar H-Bridge | CNC routers, 3D printers, robotics |
| Chopper (constant current) | High-speed pick-and-place machines |
| Microstepping | Telescope mounts, medical syringe pumps |
| Asymmetric Half-Bridge (SRM) | EV traction, aerospace fuel pumps, washing machines |
| R-Dump SRM | Low-cost appliances, hand tools |
| C-Dump SRM | Industrial fans, blowers |
| Bifilar SRM | Aerospace (fault-tolerant) |
| 3-Phase VSI (BLDC/PMSM) | Drones, EV wheels, HVAC compressors |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Current Rise Time in an L/R Drive (Unipolar)

**Problem Setup:** A unipolar L/R drive applies a step DC voltage $V_{dc}$ to a phase winding of resistance $R$ and inductance $L$.

**Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):**
$$V_{dc} = R\,i(t) + L\dfrac{di(t)}{dt}$$

This is a first-order linear ODE. Rearranging:
$$L\dfrac{di}{dt} + R\,i = V_{dc}$$

Divide by $L$:
$$\dfrac{di}{dt} + \dfrac{R}{L}\,i = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{L}$$

**Integrating Factor Method:**

The integrating factor is:
$$\mu(t) = e^{\int (R/L)\,dt} = e^{Rt/L}$$

Multiplying the ODE by $\mu(t)$:
$$e^{Rt/L}\dfrac{di}{dt} + \dfrac{R}{L}e^{Rt/L}\,i = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{L}e^{Rt/L}$$

The LHS is the exact derivative:
$$\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left[i\,e^{Rt/L}\right] = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{L}e^{Rt/L}$$

Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$ (with $i(0) = 0$):
$$i(t)\,e^{Rt/L} - 0 = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{L}\int_{0}^{t} e^{R\tau/L}\,d\tau$$

$$i(t)\,e^{Rt/L} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{L}\cdot\dfrac{L}{R}\!\left[e^{R\tau/L}\right]_{0}^{t}$$

$$i(t)\,e^{Rt/L} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{R}\!\left(e^{Rt/L} - 1\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,i(t) = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{R}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right), \quad \tau = \dfrac{L}{R}\,}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- At $t = 0$: $i = 0$ (inductor opposes sudden change).
- As $t \to \infty$: $i \to V_{dc}/R$ (steady-state DC).
- After $t = \tau$: $i \approx 0.632\,V_{dc}/R$ (63.2% of final value).
- After $t = 5\tau$: $i \approx 0.993\,V_{dc}/R$ (effectively steady state).

**Time to Reach Reference Current $I_{ref}$:**

Set $i(t_{rise}) = I_{ref}$:
$$I_{ref} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{R}\!\left(1 - e^{-t_{rise}/\tau}\right)$$

$$e^{-t_{rise}/\tau} = 1 - \dfrac{I_{ref}\,R}{V_{dc}}$$

$$t_{rise} = \tau \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - I_{ref}\,R/V_{dc}}\right)$$

This is the **critical formula** for sizing the DC link voltage: a higher $V_{dc}$ yields faster current rise → higher stepping rates.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Loss in R-Dump SRM Converter

**Setup:** When an SRM phase is turned off, the trapped magnetic energy $E = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$ must be dissipated. In an R-dump topology, this energy flows through a freewheeling diode into a dump resistor $R_d$.

**Per-Switching-Cycle Energy Loss:**

At turn-off, the energy stored in the phase inductance is:
$$E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,L\,I_{peak}^2$$

This entire energy is dissipated in $R_d$ per commutation. For a switching frequency $f_{sw}$ (commutations per second):

$$\boxed{\,P_{loss} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,L\,I_{peak}^2\,f_{sw}\,}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

Given: $L = 40\,\text{mH}$, $I_{peak} = 10\,\text{A}$, $f_{sw} = 1\,\text{kHz}$.

Step 1 — Compute stored energy:
$$E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2}\times 0.040 \times 10^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}\times 0.040 \times 100 = 2.0\,\text{J}$$

Step 2 — Compute power loss:
$$P_{loss} = 2.0 \times 1000 = 2000\,\text{W} = 2\,\text{kW}$$

**Insight:** R-dump topology is grossly inefficient for high-current, high-frequency SRM drives — hence asymmetric half-bridge is preferred for EV traction.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Comparison of Switch Count & Cost Across Topologies

For an $m$-phase machine:

| Topology | Switches per phase | Diodes per phase | Total devices (m=3) | Phase independence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unipolar L/R | 1 | 1 (optional) | 3 + 3 = **6** | Yes |
| Bipolar H-Bridge | 4 | 4 | 12 + 12 = **24** | Yes |
| Asym. Half-Bridge (SRM) | 2 | 2 | 6 + 6 = **12** | Yes |
| R-Dump (SRM) | 1 | 1 | 3 + 3 = **6** | Yes |
| C-Dump (SRM) | 1 | 2 (+ 1 cap) | 3 + 6 + 1 = **10** | Yes |
| 3-Phase VSI (BLDC/PMSM) | 2 per phase leg | 6 (shared) | **6** switches, **6** diodes | Coupled |

**Generalization Rule:** Number of switches = $m \times k$, where $k$ is the number of switches per phase (1, 2, or 4).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Torque & Speed Capability vs. Topology

| Topology | Max Torque | Max Speed | Torque Ripple | Cost Index |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unipolar L/R | 0.5 × Bipolar | Low | High | **$** |
| Bipolar H-Bridge | Full | Medium | Medium | **$$** |
| Bipolar Chopper | Full | High | Medium | **$$$** |
| Microstepping | Full | High | Very Low | **$$$$** |
| Asym. Half-Bridge (SRM) | Full | Very High | Medium | **$$$** |
| C-Dump (SRM) | Full | Medium | Medium | **$$** |
| R-Dump (SRM) | Full | Low | High | **$** |
| 3-Phase VSI + SVPWM (PMSM) | Full | Very High | Very Low | **$$$** |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** Compare unipolar and bipolar drive topologies for stepper motors with respect to torque capability and switch count.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Unipolar:** Uses center-tapped winding with one transistor per phase. Current flows in only one direction through half the winding at a time. **Torque = 0.5 × bipolar** (half the copper used). Switch count = **1 per phase**.
> - **Bipolar:** Uses H-bridge (4 switches per phase). Full winding is energized; current can reverse direction. **Full torque** capability. Switch count = **4 per phase**.
> - Bipolar is preferred for high-torque applications; unipolar is preferred for cost-sensitive low-torque applications.

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Q2.** Why is the asymmetric half-bridge converter the most commonly used topology for Switched Reluctance Motor drives?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Phase independence:** Each phase has its own two switches and two freewheeling diodes — faults in one phase do not affect others (fault tolerant).
> 2. **Bidirectional current control:** Allows both *hard chopping* (magnetization) and *soft chopping* (freewheeling through one switch + one diode) for precise current shaping.
> 3. **Energy regeneration:** Phase energy can be returned to the DC bus during demagnetization → high efficiency.
> 4. The only drawback is **higher switch count (2 per phase)**, but this is justified by the performance benefits in EV, aerospace, and industrial drives.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, Q11]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**(a)** With neat circuit diagrams, explain the operation of **unipolar L/R drive** and **bipolar H-bridge drive** topologies for a stepper motor. Compare their performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **current rise time** in an L/R drive when a step DC voltage is applied. A stepper motor phase has $R = 5\,\Omega$ and $L = 30\,\text{mH}$. The DC supply is $V_{dc} = 24\,\text{V}$ and the reference current is $I_{ref} = 3\,\text{A}$. Calculate the time taken for the current to reach 95% of $I_{ref}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Unipolar L/R drive:**
- Circuit: Center-tapped phase winding with one transistor (e.g., MOSFET) on each half. Only one half is energized at a time → current flows in one direction only.
- The L (inductor) limits current rise; the R (resistor) is sometimes external to limit steady-state current.
- **Advantage:** Simple, low cost, only 1 switch per half-winding.
- **Disadvantage:** Uses only half the copper → torque is half of bipolar. Winding center-tap adds manufacturing cost.

**Bipolar H-Bridge drive:**
- Circuit: Four switches (S1, S2, S3, S4) arranged in an H around the phase winding.
- When (S1, S2) ON → current flows left-to-right.
- When (S3, S4) ON → current flows right-to-left.
- **Advantage:** Full copper used → full torque. No center-tap needed.
- **Disadvantage:** 4 switches per phase → higher cost and more complex gate drive.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Unipolar L/R | Bipolar H-Bridge |
|---|---|---|
| Switches/phase | 1 | 4 |
| Torque | 0.5 × full | Full |
| Winding | Center-tapped | Single winding |
| Cost | Low | Medium |
| Current reversal | Not possible | Yes |

**(7 marks — full marks for clear diagrams, comparison table, and key differences.)**

**(b) Derivation (4 marks) + Numerical (3 marks):**

From Derivation 1 in Section 3:
$$i(t) = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{R}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right), \quad \tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$$

**Numerical Solution:**

Step 1 — Steady-state current:
$$I_{ss} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{R} = \dfrac{24}{5} = 4.8\,\text{A}$$

Step 2 — Time constant:
$$\tau = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{5} = 6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 6\,\text{ms}$$

Step 3 — Target current = 95% of $I_{ref} = 0.95 \times 3 = 2.85\,\text{A}$.

$$2.85 = 4.8\left(1 - e^{-t/0.006}\right)$$

$$e^{-t/0.006} = 1 - \dfrac{2.85}{4.8} = 1 - 0.59375 = 0.40625$$

$$-\dfrac{t}{0.006} = \ln(0.40625) = -0.9009$$

$$t = 0.9009 \times 0.006 = 5.405 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} \approx 5.41\,\text{ms}$$

**Answer: $t \approx 5.41\,\text{ms}$**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain with circuit diagram the **asymmetric half-bridge converter** for a 3-phase SRM. Discuss its advantages over R-dump and C-dump converters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SRM phase has $L_{max} = 60\,\text{mH}$, $L_{min} = 10\,\text{mH}$, and is operated with a chopping current of $I = 12\,\text{A}$. Compute:
  - (i) The peak stored magnetic energy per commutation.
  - (ii) The power dissipated in an R-dump topology at a switching frequency of $f_{sw} = 800\,\text{Hz}$.
  - (iii) Comment on why an asymmetric half-bridge is preferred for high-power SRM drives. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Asymmetric Half-Bridge SRM Converter:**

For a 3-phase SRM, the asymmetric half-bridge uses 2 switches and 2 freewheeling diodes per phase. Each phase winding leg is independent.

**Magnetization mode:** Both switches S1, S2 ON → DC bus voltage applied across phase → current rises (V = +V_dc, L·di/dt = V_dc − R·i).

**Freewheeling (soft chopping) mode:** One switch (S1) ON, other (S2) OFF → current freewheels through S1 and D2 → zero voltage across winding → current decays slowly.

**Demagnetization (hard chopping) mode:** Both S1, S2 OFF → current forced through D1 and D2 into DC bus with reversed polarity (V = −V_dc) → fast current decay → **energy returned to source**.

**Advantages over R-dump:**
- Energy is **regenerated** to the DC bus, not dissipated as heat → high efficiency.
- Faster demagnetization → higher speed range.

**Advantages over C-dump:**
- Simpler control (no intermediate capacitor voltage regulation).
- Better dynamic response.

**Drawback:** More switches per phase (2 vs. 1).

**(7 marks — full marks for circuit, mode explanation, and comparison.)**

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Peak stored energy:**
The energy stored depends on the inductance at the moment of turn-off. We use $L = L_{max}$ (worst case for fully aligned rotor position):
$$E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} L_{max} I^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.060 \times 12^2$$

$$E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.060 \times 144 = 4.32\,\text{J}$$

**(ii) Power loss in R-dump:**

$$P_{loss} = E_{stored} \times f_{sw} = 4.32 \times 800 = 3456\,\text{W} \approx 3.46\,\text{kW}$$

**(iii) Comment:**

An R-dump topology would dissipate **3.46 kW** as heat in a dump resistor — a huge thermal burden. In an asymmetric half-bridge, this energy is **regenerated to the DC bus** with typical regeneration efficiency > 85%. Hence for high-power SRM drives (e.g., EV traction motors of 50–200 kW), only asymmetric half-bridge or similar regenerative topology is practical. Additionally, asymmetric half-bridge provides **phase independence**, which is critical for **fault tolerance** in safety-critical applications such as electric vehicle powertrains and aerospace actuators.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU Valuation for "Topologies" Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the freewheeling diodes** in the H-bridge / half-bridge circuit — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if diodes are missing.
> 2. **Confusing "unipolar" with "bipolar" switch count** — students often write "1 switch for bipolar" which is wrong. Bipolar = 4 switches per phase.
> 3. **Skipping the time-constant derivation** — full marks for current rise derivation require showing the integrating-factor steps, not just stating the final formula.
> 4. **Mixing up R-dump and C-dump** — R-dump uses a *resistor*; C-dump uses a *capacitor* as the energy storage element. Many students interchange them.
> 5. **Omitting units** in numerical answers — KTU board examiners strictly require SI units (V, A, ms, J, W) for full marks.
> 6. **Not labeling currents/voltages** in the circuit diagram — every branch must show arrow direction for current and polarity for voltage.
> 7. **Writing "BLDC" when the question is about SRM** — pay close attention to the machine name in the question; the drive topology differs significantly.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ A **topology** = the structural arrangement of switches, diodes, and winding taps that determines how current is delivered to the machine phase.
- ⭐ **Unipolar L/R drive** → 1 switch/phase, half torque, low cost.
- ⭐ **Bipolar H-bridge** → 4 switches/phase, full torque, requires 4 gate-drive signals.
- ⭐ **Constant-current chopper** → adds current feedback; enables high-speed operation.
- ⭐ **Microstepping** → sinusoidal current references in 2 phases; N microsteps/full-step; eliminates resonance.
- ⭐ **Asymmetric half-bridge (SRM)** → 2 switches + 2 diodes/phase; **industry standard**; phase independent; regenerative.
- ⭐ **Symmetric half-bridge (SRM)** → shared switches between phases; lower cost; coupled phases.
- ⭐ **R-dump (SRM)** → simplest; energy lost in resistor; $P_{loss} = \frac{1}{2}LI^2 f_{sw}$.
- ⭐ **C-dump (SRM)** → energy stored in capacitor, then returned; moderate complexity.
- ⭐ **Bifilar (SRM)** → auxiliary winding traps energy; no freewheeling diodes needed; complex machine construction.
- ⭐ **3-phase VSI** → 6 switches, 6 diodes; used for BLDC (6-step) and PMSM (SVPWM).
- ⭐ **Key equation:** $i(t) = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{R}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ with $\tau = L/R$.
- ⭐ **Torque equation:** $T = \frac{1}{2}i^2 \dfrac{dL}{d\theta}$ — same form for stepper and SRM (reluctance torque).
- ⭐ **Phase voltage equation:** $V_{dc} = R\,i + L\,\dfrac{di}{dt} + e_{back}$ — universal for all special machines.
- ⭐ **Energy stored:** $E = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$ — must be safely dissipated or regenerated by the topology.
- ⭐ **Fault tolerance ranking:** Asymmetric half-bridge > Symmetric half-bridge > R-dump > Bifilar.
- ⭐ **Cost ranking (low to high):** Unipolar L/R → R-dump → C-dump → Bipolar H-bridge → Bipolar Chopper → Asymmetric half-bridge → Microstepping.
- ⭐ **KTU Module 4 most-asked question pattern:** Draw and explain one topology (7 marks) + numerical on current rise / power loss (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vernier Reluctance Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT638 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vernier Reluctance Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vernier Reluctance Motor (VRM)** is a low-speed, high-torque synchronous reluctance machine that exploits the **magnetic gearing effect** (also called the *field modulation principle*) between a multi-pole stator flux distribution and a salient-pole reluctance rotor with a *different* number of poles.

In a conventional synchronous reluctance motor, the rotor always has the **same** number of poles as the stator's rotating MMF. In a Vernier reluctance motor, however, the stator winding creates a flux distribution with one pole count, while the rotor has **another** (typically larger) number of salient poles. The interaction between these two spatial harmonics produces a *magnetic gearing*, so the rotor turns at a fraction of the synchronous speed of the stator MMF — exactly analogous to how a Vernier caliper gives fine resolution by combining two slightly different scales.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two Rulers"

Imagine two combs with **different tooth pitches** placed side by side. As one comb slides uniformly, certain teeth of the second comb light up one after another, moving much more slowly than the sliding comb itself. The slow-moving bright tooth is the *rotor pole* chasing the *stator flux wave*. This apparent "mechanical advantage" without any gears is the **Vernier principle** in magnetism — and the rotor achieves high torque at low speed.

### 1.2 Why "Vernier"?

The term comes from the **Vernier scale** used in precision measurement instruments. Just as the Vernier scale provides high resolution by interleaving two slightly mismatched scales, the VRM achieves low-speed, high-torque operation by interleaving two spatial MMF waves of *different* pole pitches. The standard operational constant of relevance is **electrical frequency $f = 50\text{ Hz}$** in Indian grid applications, and the resulting rotor speed is governed by the **pole-mismatch law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinguishing feature:** A Vernier reluctance motor **has no permanent magnets and no rotor winding**. It is purely a reluctance machine, but with deliberately mismatched pole counts to exploit field modulation.

### 1.3 Salient Identity Numbers

For a Vernier reluctance motor, the two most important identities are:

- $P_s$ = number of stator pole pairs produced by the winding (e.g., $P_s = 2$ for a 4-pole stator MMF).
- $P_r$ = number of rotor salient pole pairs.

The motor synchronises at the **Vernier speed**:

$$N_{sync} = \frac{120 \, f}{\vert P_s - P_r \vert} \quad \text{(in rpm)}$$

This means if $P_s = 2$ and $P_r = 5$, the rotor spins at $N_{sync} = 1200/3 = 400$ rpm, even though the stator MMF appears to rotate at $1500$ rpm — a clear **magnetic gear ratio of 3.75 : 1**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Features

| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Stator core** | Laminated silicon steel; identical to a conventional 3-phase AC machine. Slots house a distributed or concentrated 3-phase winding. |
| **Stator winding** | 3-phase, sinusoidally distributed; produces a rotating MMF with $P_s$ pole pairs and fundamental electrical frequency $f$. |
| **Rotor** | Laminated steel with **salient poles** (no windings, no PMs, no cage). Number of rotor pole pairs $P_r$ is **deliberately different** from $P_s$. |
| **Air gap** | Uniform mechanically, but the effective *reluctance* varies spatially due to rotor saliency. |
| **Shaft / bearings** | Standard; no slip rings or brushes (brushless construction). |

### 2.2 Principle of Operation — Why It Works

The Vernier reluctance motor operates on the principle of **permeance (or reluctance) harmonics**. The spatially varying air-gap permeance produced by the salient rotor modulates the stator MMF, generating additional rotating field harmonics. The interaction between the stator fundamental and these permeance-modulated harmonics produces a sustained average torque only when the rotor rotates at the *Vernier synchronous speed*.

Operation in five logical steps:

1. **Three-phase stator excitation** produces a rotating MMF with pole count $2P_s$.
2. The **salient rotor** imposes a periodic permeance variation with $2P_r$ poles around the air gap.
3. The **modulation** of the stator MMF by the rotor permeance generates rotating field harmonics of pole counts $\vert 2P_s \pm 2P_r \vert$.
4. The rotor tries to lock with the most dominant permeance harmonic.
5. Steady torque is produced only at the **Vernier synchronous speed** $N_{sync} = \frac{120 f}{\vert P_s - P_r \vert}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **essential formula compendium** for any KTU exam question on Vernier reluctance motors. Memorise and internalise these.

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---------|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Vernier synchronous speed | $N_{sync} = \dfrac{120 \, f}{\vert P_s - P_r \vert}$ | rpm |
| 2 | Mechanical synchronous angular speed | $\omega_m = \dfrac{2 \pi f}{\vert P_s - P_r \vert}$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Stator induced EMF (per phase) | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, N_{ph} \, K_w$ | V |
| 4 | Permeance of d-axis (minimum reluctance axis) | $\mathcal{P}_d = \dfrac{\mu_0 \, A}{g_d}$ | H |
| 5 | Permeance of q-axis (maximum reluctance axis) | $\mathcal{P}_q = \dfrac{\mu_0 \, A}{g_q}$ | H |
| 6 | Inductance ratio (saliency ratio) | $\xi = \dfrac{L_d}{L_q} = \dfrac{\mathcal{P}_d}{\mathcal{P}_q}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Reluctance torque | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{2} \, \dfrac{P_{eq}}{2} \left(L_d - L_q\right) I_s^{\,2} \, \sin(2 \delta)$ | N·m |
| 8 | Average electromagnetic torque | $T_{avg} = \dfrac{3}{2} \, p_{eff} \, (\lambda_d I_q - \lambda_q I_d)$ | N·m |
| 9 | Magnetic gear ratio | $G_r = \dfrac{P_s}{\vert P_s - P_r \vert}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Pull-out torque angle | $\delta_{max} = 45^{\circ}$ | degrees |

Where:
- $P_s, P_r$ are stator and rotor pole pairs.
- $P_{eq} = \vert P_s - P_r \vert$ is the *effective* pole pair number of the Vernier motor.
- $\delta$ is the torque angle (load angle) between the stator MMF axis and the rotor d-axis.
- $K_w$ is the winding factor.
- $\lambda_d, \lambda_q$ are the d- and q-axis flux linkages.
- $I_d, I_q$ are the d- and q-axis stator currents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all the formulae above, the term $\vert P_s - P_r \vert$ is the **effective pole pair number**. This is the *defining algebraic signature* of every Vernier reluctance motor and is the single most common place where KTU students lose marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

VRMs are favoured in applications demanding **high torque density at low speed without using rare-earth magnets**:
- Direct-drive wind turbine generators (gearless nacelle).
- In-wheel traction motors for electric vehicles.
- Industrial robotics and CNC spindle drives.
- Home appliances (washing-machine direct drives).
- Flywheel energy storage systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Vernier Synchronous Speed

We derive the relationship between stator frequency $f$, stator pole pairs $P_s$, rotor pole pairs $P_r$, and the resulting mechanical rotor speed.

**Step 1 — Stator rotating MMF angular frequency.**

A balanced 3-phase winding excited at electrical frequency $f$ produces an MMF wave that rotates at the *electrical angular frequency*:

$$\omega_e = 2 \pi f \quad \text{(rad/s, electrical)}$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical speed of the stator MMF.**

The mechanical speed of a 2-pole-pair MMF is half the electrical angular frequency, so the *stator-field mechanical speed* is:

$$\omega_{stator} = \frac{\omega_e}{P_s} = \frac{2 \pi f}{P_s} \quad \text{(rad/s, mechanical)}$$

**Step 3 — Introducing the rotor permeance wave.**

The salient rotor imposes a spatial permeance variation with $P_r$ cycles per mechanical revolution. The permeance wave can be written as:

$$\mathcal{P}(\theta) = \mathcal{P}_0 + \mathcal{P}_1 \, \cos(P_r \, \theta)$$

where $\theta$ is the mechanical angle measured around the air gap.

**Step 4 — Modulated air-gap flux density.**

The air-gap flux density is the product of MMF and permeance:

$$B(\theta, t) = \mathcal{F}(\theta, t) \cdot \mathcal{P}(\theta)$$

Using the trigonometric product-to-sum identity, the $\cos(P_r \theta)$ permeance harmonic modulates the stator MMF (which has $P_s$ pole pairs) to produce *new* rotating harmonics with pole counts:

$$P_{new} = \vert P_s \pm P_r \vert$$

**Step 5 — Condition for synchronism.**

For sustained average torque, the rotor's salient-pole pattern must remain phase-locked with one of these modulated harmonics. The rotor then physically rotates at a speed such that it presents the same spatial orientation to the modulated wave each cycle. That synchronism speed (mechanical) is:

$$\omega_m = \frac{2 \pi f}{\vert P_s - P_r \vert}$$

Converting to RPM:

$$N_{sync} = \frac{\omega_m \times 60}{2 \pi} = \frac{60 \cdot 2 \pi f}{2 \pi \vert P_s - P_r \vert} = \frac{120 \, f}{\vert P_s - P_r \vert}$$

> This completes the derivation of the **Vernier synchronous speed** relation.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Reluctance Torque Equation

We derive the electromagnetic torque of the Vernier reluctance motor from the co-energy approach.

**Step 1 — Define the d-q reference frame.**

The rotor saliency defines a direct axis (d-axis, minimum reluctance, flux preferred) and a quadrature axis (q-axis, maximum reluctance). In this synchronously rotating frame, the stator inductances are constants:

$$L_d = \text{d-axis inductance (high)}, \quad L_q = \text{q-axis inductance (low)}$$

**Step 2 — Magnetic co-energy.**

For a salient-pole synchronous reluctance machine, the magnetic co-energy stored in the coupling field is:

$$W_{co} = \frac{1}{2} L_d I_d^{\,2} + \frac{1}{2} L_q I_q^{\,2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute torque as partial derivative of co-energy.**

Electromagnetic torque equals the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to mechanical angle $\theta_m$, holding currents constant:

$$T_e = \frac{\partial W_{co}}{\partial \theta_m}\bigg|_{i=const}$$

Since $I_d = I_s \sin\delta$ and $I_q = I_s \cos\delta$ (with $\delta$ the load angle and $I_s$ the stator current magnitude), we substitute:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \, \frac{P_{eq}}{2} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \delta}\left[\frac{1}{2} L_d I_s^{\,2} \sin^2 \delta + \frac{1}{2} L_q I_s^{\,2} \cos^2 \delta\right]$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate.**

Differentiating the bracketed expression with respect to $\delta$:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \delta}\left[L_d \sin^2 \delta + L_q \cos^2 \delta\right] = 2 L_d \sin\delta \cos\delta - 2 L_q \cos\delta \sin\delta$$

Applying the identity $2 \sin\delta \cos\delta = \sin(2\delta)$:

$$= (L_d - L_q) \sin(2 \delta)$$

**Step 5 — Final reluctance torque expression.**

Substituting back and accounting for the effective pole pair number $P_{eq} = \vert P_s - P_r \vert$:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P_{eq}}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} (L_d - L_q) I_s^{\,2} \sin(2 \delta)$$

Simplifying:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{4} \, P_{eq} \, (L_d - L_q) \, I_s^{\,2} \, \sin(2 \delta)$$

> **Maximum torque** occurs when $\delta = 45^{\circ}$ (where $\sin(2\delta) = 1$), giving:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{4} \, P_{eq} \, (L_d - L_q) \, I_s^{\,2}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Per-Phase EMF

For a Vernier reluctance motor (treated as a synchronous reluctance machine with $P_{eq}$ effective pole pairs):

**Step 1 — Flux per pole.**

$$\phi = B_{av} \cdot A_p \quad \text{(Wb)}$$

where $B_{av}$ is the average flux density in the air gap and $A_p$ is the pole area.

**Step 2 — Frequency of induced EMF.**

The induced EMF frequency equals the supply frequency $f$ (synchronous operation):

$$f = \frac{P_{eq} \cdot N}{120}$$

**Step 3 — Standard EMF equation (transformer EMF).**

For a sinusoidally distributed winding:

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, \phi \, N_{ph} \, K_w$$

where $N_{ph}$ is the number of turns per phase and $K_w = K_d \cdot K_p$ is the winding factor (distribution × pitch factor).

> For a *concentrated* winding (typical in VRMs to enhance specific permeance harmonics), $K_w \approx K_p$, and the EMF magnitude is governed largely by the slot–pole combination.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

The following matrix highlights where the Vernier reluctance motor sits among the family of reluctance-based machines:

| Feature | Conventional Reluctance Motor | Vernier Reluctance Motor | Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor |
|---------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Rotor saliency | Same pole count as stator | **Different** pole count | None (smooth rotor) |
| Magnet requirement | None | **None** | Required (rare-earth or ferrite) |
| Torque density | Moderate | **High** (at low speed) | Very high |
| Synchronous speed | $N = 120f/P_s$ | $N = 120f/\vert P_s - P_r \vert$ (lower) | $N = 120f/P_s$ |
| Field weakening | Natural, wide range | Natural, wide range | Limited by magnet B-H curve |
| Rotor temperature limit | High (no magnets) | **High** (no magnets) | Limited (magnet demagnetisation) |
| Cogging torque | Moderate | Low (with optimal slot–pole choice) | Can be significant |
| Cost | Low | Moderate | High (PM material) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Constructional Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    statorCore["STATOR CORE LAMINATED STEEL"]
    statorSlots["STATOR SLOTS 3 PHASE DISTRIBUTED WINDING"]
    airGap["UNIFORM MECHANICAL AIR GAP"]
    rotorCore["ROTOR CORE LAMINATED STEEL"]
    salientPoles["ROTOR SALIENT POLES NUMBER Pr POLE PAIRS"]
    rotorShaft["ROTOR SHAFT NO WINDINGS NO MAGNETS"]
    bearings["BEARINGS BRUSHLESS CONSTRUCTION"]
    statorCore --> statorSlots
    statorSlots --> airGap
    airGap --> salientPoles
    salientPoles --> rotorCore
    rotorCore --> rotorShaft
    rotorShaft --> bearings
```

### 4.2 Operational Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the *cause-and-effect chain* of how a Vernier reluctance motor converts electrical input into mechanical output. It substitutes for a cross-sectional pictorial drawing while preserving all functional relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    supply["THREE PHASE AC SUPPLY f Hz"]
    statorMMF["STATOR ROTATING MMF Ps POLE PAIRS"]
    permeance["ROTOR PERMEANCE VARIATION Pr POLES PER REVOLUTION"]
    modulation["FIELD MODULATION BY PERMEANCE WAVE"]
    harmonics["GENERATED HARMONICS Ps PLUS Pr AND Ps MINUS Pr"]
    lock["ROTOR LOCKS WITH HARMONIC Pmod equals ABS Ps MINUS Pr"]
    torque["AVERAGE RELUCTANCE TORQUE Te AT VERNIER SPEED"]
    load["MECHANICAL LOAD DRIVEN AT LOW RPM"]
    supply --> statorMMF
    statorMMF --> modulation
    permeance --> modulation
    modulation --> harmonics
    harmonics --> lock
    lock --> torque
    torque --> load
```

### 4.3 Per-Phase Equivalent Circuit and Phasor Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vph["Vph TERMINAL VOLTAGE PER PHASE"]
    Rs["Rs STATOR WINDING RESISTANCE"]
    Xd["jXd d AXIS SYNCHRONOUS REACTANCE"]
    Xq["jXq q AXIS SYNCHRONOUS REACTANCE"]
    Ef["Ef BACK EMF RELUCTANCE INDUCED"]
    delta["DELTA LOAD ANGLE BETWEEN Vph AND Ef"]
    Vph --> Rs
    Rs --> Xd
    Xd --> Xq
    Xq --> Ef
    delta -.-> Vph
    delta -.-> Ef
```

> **Reading the phasor:** $V_{ph}$ leads $E_f$ by the load angle $\delta$. The saliency of the rotor is captured by $X_d \neq X_q$. Maximum reluctance torque occurs at $\delta = 45^{\circ}$.

### 4.4 Torque–Angle Characteristic Sketch (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    axisX["DELTA LOAD ANGLE DEGREES"]
    axisY["Te ELECTROMAGNETIC TORQUE"]
    startPoint["ORIGIN ZERO TORQUE"]
    rise["SINE 2 DELTA REGION POSITIVE SLOPE"]
    peak["MAXIMUM TORQUE AT DELTA EQUALS 45 DEG"]
    fall["DECAY REGION BEYOND 45 DEG"]
    pullOut["PULL OUT AT DELTA EQUALS 135 DEG"]
    axisX --> startPoint
    startPoint --> rise
    rise --> peak
    peak --> fall
    fall --> pullOut
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]`

**Define a Vernier reluctance motor and state its distinguishing feature compared to a conventional synchronous reluctance motor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Vernier reluctance motor is a synchronous reluctance machine in which the **number of rotor salient pole pairs ($P_r$) is deliberately different from the number of stator pole pairs ($P_s$)** produced by the stator winding.

*Distinguishing feature:* It exploits the **magnetic gearing effect / field modulation principle**, which causes the rotor to run at the Vernier synchronous speed $N_{sync} = 120 f / \vert P_s - P_r \vert$, much lower than the synchronous speed of a conventional reluctance motor having the same stator frequency and pole count. (3 marks)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper Set B]`

**What is the role of the permeance harmonics in a Vernier reluctance motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The salient-pole rotor introduces a **spatially varying air-gap permeance** with $P_r$ cycles per mechanical revolution. This permeance wave **modulates** the stator MMF (which has $P_s$ pole pairs) and generates additional rotating field harmonics with effective pole counts of $\vert P_s + P_r \vert$ and $\vert P_s - P_r \vert$. The interaction between the stator MMF and the dominant permeance-modulated harmonic produces a sustained average electromagnetic torque. **Without these harmonics, no net torque would be developed.** (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. (a)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction** of a Vernier reluctance motor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **electromagnetic torque** of a Vernier reluctance motor and state the condition for maximum torque. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q3(a):**

A Vernier reluctance motor has the following constructional parts:

**1. Stator (3 marks)**
- Stator core: laminated silicon steel, slotted, identical to a conventional 3-phase induction motor.
- Stator winding: 3-phase sinusoidal distributed winding (or sometimes concentrated windings to enhance specific permeance harmonics) producing a rotating MMF with $P_s$ pole pairs when supplied at frequency $f$.

**2. Rotor (3 marks)**
- Rotor core: laminated silicon steel with **$2P_r$ salient poles** machined on its outer surface.
- **No rotor winding, no permanent magnets, no squirrel cage** — purely a reluctance rotor.
- The number of rotor pole pairs $P_r$ is **deliberately different** from $P_s$ to enable the Vernier action.

**3. Mechanical assembly (1 mark)**
- Uniform mechanical air gap; bearings mounted on end shields; brushless construction since no electrical connection to the rotor.

The rotor presents a spatially periodic air-gap permeance with $P_r$ cycles per mechanical revolution, modulating the stator MMF to generate the Vernier effect.

**(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q3(b):**

Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes for the complete derivation. The steps are:

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define d–q reference frame fixed on the rotor. State $L_d$ and $L_q$ as the d-axis and q-axis inductances.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Express magnetic co-energy:

$$W_{co} = \frac{1}{2} L_d I_d^{\,2} + \frac{1}{2} L_q I_q^{\,2}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Convert stator current $I_s$ into d–q components using load angle $\delta$: $I_d = I_s \sin\delta$ and $I_q = I_s \cos\delta$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Take the partial derivative of co-energy with respect to the mechanical angle (related to $\delta$):

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P_{eq}}{2} \cdot \frac{\partial W_{co}}{\partial \delta}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** After algebraic simplification using $2 \sin\delta \cos\delta = \sin(2\delta)$:

$$\boxed{T_e = \frac{3}{4} \, P_{eq} \, (L_d - L_q) \, I_s^{\,2} \, \sin(2 \delta)}$$

where $P_{eq} = \vert P_s - P_r \vert$.

**Condition for maximum torque (1 mark):** $\sin(2\delta) = 1 \Rightarrow 2\delta = 90^{\circ} \Rightarrow \delta = 45^{\circ}$.

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{4} \, P_{eq} \, (L_d - L_q) \, I_s^{\,2}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 ALTERNATIVE QUESTION (Internal Choice)

**Q3. (b) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation** of a Vernier reluctance motor with the help of the concept of permeance harmonics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase Vernier reluctance motor has $P_s = 2$ stator pole pairs and $P_r = 7$ rotor salient pole pairs. The supply frequency is **50 Hz**. Calculate: **(i)** the Vernier synchronous speed in rpm; **(ii)** the mechanical angular speed in rad/s; **(iii)** the magnetic gear ratio. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q3(b) Alternative — Part (a):**

**Principle of operation (5 marks) — bullet exposition:**

- When the 3-phase stator winding is excited, a rotating MMF with $P_s$ pole pairs is established in the air gap at synchronous angular frequency $\omega_e = 2\pi f$.
- The salient-pole rotor, having $P_r$ salient pole pairs, imposes a spatially periodic permeance distribution on the air gap. This permeance can be expressed as a Fourier series; the dominant spatial harmonic has $P_r$ cycles per mechanical revolution.
- The **modulation** of the stator MMF by the rotor permeance generates new rotating field components, the most important of which have pole counts $\vert P_s \pm P_r \vert$.
- The rotor seeks to align its salient poles with the most dominant modulated harmonic.
- Steady synchronism is achieved only when the rotor rotates at the **Vernier synchronous speed** $N_{sync} = 120 f / \vert P_s - P_r \vert$, producing sustained electromagnetic torque through variation of reluctance.
- The torque is purely *reluctance torque* — no magnet or rotor excitation is needed.

**Equation support (2 marks):**

$$T_e = \frac{3}{4} P_{eq} (L_d - L_q) I_s^{\,2} \sin(2\delta), \quad P_{eq} = \vert P_s - P_r \vert$$

---

**MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q3(b) Alternative — Part (b):**

**Given:** $P_s = 2$, $P_r = 7$, $f = 50$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Effective pole pair number:**

$$P_{eq} = \vert P_s - P_r \vert = \vert 2 - 7 \vert = 5 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Vernier synchronous speed:**

$$N_{sync} = \frac{120 \, f}{P_{eq}} = \frac{120 \times 50}{5} = \frac{6000}{5} = 1200 \text{ rpm} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical angular speed:**

$$\omega_m = \frac{2 \pi N_{sync}}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1200}{60} = 40 \pi \approx 125.66 \text{ rad/s} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Magnetic gear ratio:**

$$G_r = \frac{P_s}{P_{eq}} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

This means the stator MMF appears to rotate at $P_s / P_{eq} = 2/5 = 0.4$ times the rotor speed; equivalently, the rotor turns at $1/0.4 = 2.5$ times *slower* than a conventional 2-pole-pair reluctance motor would at 50 Hz (which would be 1500 rpm). **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on Vernier Reluctance Motor problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value** in $N_{sync} = 120f/\vert P_s - P_r \vert$ — speed cannot be negative; always take magnitude. **(Common: −1 mark)**
> 2. **Using $P_s$ alone** instead of $P_{eq} = \vert P_s - P_r \vert$ in the EMF and torque equations. **(Common: −2 marks)**
> 3. **Confusing Vernier reluctance motor with permanent magnet Vernier motor** — the former has no PMs; the latter does. Examiners look for this distinction. **(Common: −2 marks)**
> 4. **Stating the torque maximum at $\delta = 90°$** instead of the correct $\delta = 45°$ — the torque varies as $\sin(2\delta)$, not $\sin\delta$. **(Common: −1 mark)**
> 5. **Not writing the co-energy derivative** in the torque derivation — partial marks are lost for skipping intermediate steps. **(Common: −1 mark)**
> 6. **Drawing induction-motor-like cage rotor diagrams** — VRM rotors have salient poles, no bars. **(Common: −1 mark)**

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vernier Reluctance Motors**

- **Definition:** Synchronous reluctance machine with deliberately mismatched stator and rotor pole counts exploiting magnetic gearing. *(1 mark question favourite)*
- **Construction:** Laminated stator with 3-phase winding + salient-pole rotor (no winding, no PM). Brushless. *(asked in construction questions)*
- **Operating principle:** Permeance modulation of stator MMF by salient rotor; field harmonics of pole counts $\vert P_s \pm P_r \vert$ are generated.
- **Vernier synchronous speed:** $N_{sync} = \dfrac{120 f}{\vert P_s - P_r \vert}$ rpm — always use the **absolute value**.
- **Effective pole pair number:** $P_{eq} = \vert P_s - P_r \vert$ replaces $P_s$ in **all** torque and EMF equations.
- **Magnetic gear ratio:** $G_r = P_s / \vert P_s - P_r \vert$. Higher $G_r \Rightarrow$ slower rotor, higher torque.
- **Torque equation:** $T_e = \dfrac{3}{4} P_{eq} (L_d - L_q) I_s^{\,2} \sin(2\delta)$.
- **Maximum torque angle:** $\delta = 45^{\circ}$ (because of $\sin 2\delta$ dependence).
- **EMF equation:** $E_{ph} = 4.44 f \phi N_{ph} K_w$ — same form as synchronous machine, with $P_{eq}$ governing frequency.
- **No magnets, no rotor copper, brushless** → high reliability, low cost, suitable for harsh environments.
- **Applications:** Direct-drive wind generators, EV in-wheel motors, robotics, CNC spindles, home appliances.
- **Comparison key:** Different from PM Vernier motor (no PMs), different from synchronous reluctance motor (mismatched poles).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications. Field Oriented Control Principles
Direct Torque and Flux Control. Vernier Permanent Magnet Motors: Constructional Details
Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT638 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principle of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stepper Motor — KTU 2024 Definition**
> A *stepper motor* is a **brushless, synchronous electromechanical device** that converts a train of input electrical pulses (digital control signals) into discrete, precise mechanical angular displacements called **steps**. The rotor position is **incrementally advanced** by a fixed angular increment (the *step angle*) for each excitation pulse applied to the stator winding, without requiring any feedback sensor (an *open-loop* positioning actuator).

The Principle of Operation is fundamentally rooted in the **alignment of the rotor magnetic axis with the resultant stator magnetic field axis**, governed by the principle of **minimum magnetic reluctance**. Whenever the stator winding is energized, the rotor moves to a position where the air-gap reluctance of the magnetic circuit is **minimized** and the inductance of the excited stator phase is **maximized**.

The key governing mathematical relationship is the **step angle equation**:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot m}$$

where $\theta_s$ is the step angle, $N_r$ is the number of rotor teeth, and $m$ is the number of stator phases excited per step. The *electrical pulse-to-mechanical step* ratio is exactly **1 : 1**, which makes the device a true *digital-to-analog angular transducer*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Compass and the Magnetized Clock Tower**
> Imagine you have **four large electromagnets** arranged in a circle (at North, East, South, West) and a small **bar magnet** in the center that is free to rotate. If you turn ON only the **North** electromagnet, the central bar magnet snaps so that its **South pole faces North** (and North pole faces South). Now switch OFF North and turn ON **East** — the bar magnet rotates 90° clockwise to align with the new field. Switch to South — another 90°. Switch to West — another 90°. That is **exactly** how a stepper motor works: **each electrical pulse moves the rotor by one fixed step** to align with the new magnetic field. The "discrete" angular jump per pulse is the *step*, and the *cumulative rotation* is simply the sum of all steps.

A simpler household analogy: think of a **detented mechanical ratchet wheel**. Each click of the ratchet is one *step*. The stepper motor replaces the human hand turning the ratchet with **electromagnetic pulses** — the motor can hold position firmly even when de-energized (in *detent torque* mode, especially in hybrid types).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard step angle values:** **1.8°** (most common, 200 steps/rev), **0.9°** (400 steps/rev, high-resolution), **7.5°** (48 steps/rev, low-cost), **15°** (24 steps/rev, very coarse).
> - **Step accuracy (open-loop):** ±5% of one step (i.e., *non-cumulative error*), since each step is independent.
> - **Supply voltage range:** typically **5 V DC to 48 V DC** (with chopper drives up to **80 V DC**).
> - **Holding torque range:** from **mN·m** (small instrumentation) to over **50 N·m** (industrial NEMA-34 frame).
> - **Maximum step rate (no-load):** typically **1000 to 10,000 pulses per second (pps)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physical Principle — Minimum Reluctance Alignment

The operation of every stepper motor (whether *Variable Reluctance*, *Permanent Magnet*, or *Hybrid*) ultimately depends on one universal electromagnetic law:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Le Chatelier's Magnetic Analog:** The rotor always seeks a position that **minimizes the magnetic circuit reluctance** (or equivalently, **maximizes the inductance and stored magnetic co-energy**). The electromagnetic torque is generated only when the rotor is *displaced* from this minimum-reluctance (aligned) position.

Mathematically, for a single excited phase carrying current $i$:

$$T_e = \frac{1}{2}\, i^2 \, \frac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$$

where $L(\theta)$ is the *angle-dependent inductance* of the phase, and $\theta$ is the electrical angular displacement between the rotor pole axis and the excited stator pole axis. **Torque always acts in the direction that *increases* the inductance** (since the system naturally seeks the minimum energy state when no current is supplied, but the energy conversion is governed by co-energy in the energized state).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Stator Excitation** — A DC pulse is applied to one stator phase winding (say, Phase A). Current $i_A$ flows, producing a stationary magnetic field with N-pole and S-pole at the excited stator poles.
2. **Rotor Alignment** — The rotor (with $N_r$ salient teeth in VR type, or permanent magnet poles in PM/Hybrid) experiences a torque and rotates to the nearest position of **minimum reluctance** — i.e., a rotor tooth aligns directly under the excited stator pole.
3. **Phase Commutation** — Phase A is de-energized and Phase B is energized. The rotor must rotate by exactly one step angle $\theta_s$ to align a new set of rotor teeth under Phase B's poles.
4. **Sequential Advancement** — Repeating the commutation in the sequence A → B → C → D → A produces **continuous rotation** in one direction. Reversing the sequence gives **reverse rotation**.
5. **Step Counting** — Each pulse = one step. To rotate by an angle $\theta_{total}$, the controller must output exactly $N = \theta_{total}/\theta_s$ pulses.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** a KTU student must memorize for problems on this topic. All quantities are in **SI units** unless otherwise stated.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning of Symbols** | **Boundary / Validity** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s}$ | Step angle; $N_s$ = steps per revolution | $N_s$ must be an integer |
| 2 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_r \cdot m}$ | Step angle in terms of rotor teeth $N_r$ and phase count $m$ | $m$ = phases stepped per pulse |
| 3 | $N_s = \dfrac{N_r \cdot m}{2}$ *(for some multistack)* | For multi-stack variable reluctance motors | $N_r$ = teeth per stack |
| 4 | $f_p = \dfrac{\omega}{360°} \cdot N_s$ | Pulse rate $f_p$ (pps) for rotor speed $\omega$ (rpm) | $f_p$ limited by pull-out curve |
| 5 | $T_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\, i^2\, \dfrac{dL(\theta)}{d\theta}$ | Instantaneous electromagnetic torque | Single phase excited |
| 6 | $L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$ | Variable inductance model (VR/PM) | $\theta$ in mechanical radians |
| 7 | $W_{fld} = \dfrac{1}{2}\, L(\theta)\, i^2$ | Magnetic co-energy stored | Linear magnetics assumed |
| 8 | $\theta_{electrical} = \dfrac{N_r}{2}\,\theta_{mech}$ | Electrical-mechanical angle relation | For $N_r$-toothed rotor |
| 9 | $\eta_{step} = 1 - \dfrac{\theta_{err}}{\theta_s}$ | Step accuracy; $\theta_{err}$ = positional error per step | Typically 95%–97% |
| 10 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{L \cdot \Delta I}$ | Maximum stepping rate (L/R time constant limit) | Single-supply unipolar drive |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Stepper motors are **the workhorse of digital motion control** because no encoder is required. They are ubiquitous in:
> - **3D printers** (X, Y, Z axis control with 1.8° hybrid steppers).
> - **CNC routers and milling machines** (open-loop positioning).
> - **Inkjet printers** (paper feed and printhead carriage).
> - **Robotics** (joint actuators, camera gimbals).
> - **Disk drives, medical syringe pumps, textile machines, watch movements, automotive instrument clusters**.
>
> The *open-loop simplicity* is the engineering advantage: you count pulses, and the position is known without feedback. The *trade-off* is the risk of **lost steps** under sudden overload — a critical KTU exam point.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Step Angle Equation (Variable Reluctance Stepper Motor)

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Consider a *single-stack* variable reluctance (VR) stepper motor with $N_r$ rotor teeth and $N_s$ stator teeth. The rotor and stator are cylindrical, with salient teeth on the inner surface of the stator and outer surface of the rotor. The number of phases is $m$. We want to find the smallest repeatable angular step $\theta_s$.

### Step 1 — Tooth Pitch on the Rotor

The angular pitch (in mechanical degrees) of one rotor tooth is the *full circle divided by the number of teeth*:

$$\beta_r = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$

This $\beta_r$ is the angle between the centerlines of two adjacent rotor teeth.

### Step 2 — Tooth Pitch on the Stator

By the same geometric logic, the angular pitch of one stator tooth is:

$$\beta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s}$$

### Step 3 — Compatibility Condition

For the motor to be self-starting and to produce a non-zero torque at every rotor position, the rotor and stator teeth must be in a slightly *misaligned* state that advances by a fixed amount per phase. The standard design condition is:

$$N_s = N_r \pm 2\,m$$

This is the **compatibility equation** (sometimes called the *detent condition*). The plus or minus sign depends on whether the rotor and stator teeth are designed to "lead" or "lag" by one full tooth pitch per phase sequence.

### Step 4 — The Step Angle

When Phase A is energized, a particular pair of rotor teeth aligns with the Phase A stator poles. When Phase A is turned off and Phase B is energized, the rotor must rotate to align with the Phase B stator poles. The angular distance between the Phase A and Phase B pole centers on the stator is:

$$\theta_{AB} = \frac{360°}{N_s} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{360°}{N_s}$$

*(For a uniformly distributed $m$-phase stator.)*

But the rotor must rotate by an *integer multiple* of the rotor tooth pitch to bring a new tooth under the new pole:

$$\theta_s = k \cdot \beta_r = k \cdot \frac{360°}{N_r}, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

### Step 5 — Matching Stator to Rotor

For the rotor motion $\theta_s$ to equal the stator phase advance $\theta_{AB}$:

$$k \cdot \frac{360°}{N_r} = \frac{360°}{N_s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{N_r}{N_s}$$

Substituting the compatibility condition $N_s = N_r \pm 2m$:

$$k = \frac{N_r}{N_r \pm 2m}$$

For small $m$ relative to $N_r$ (typical design), $k \approx 1$. The step angle becomes:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s} = \frac{360°}{N_r \pm 2m}$$

### Step 6 — Final Compact Form

Rearranging, the number of steps per revolution is:

$$N_{steps} = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = N_s = N_r \pm 2m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Result:** For a stepper motor with $N_r$ rotor teeth and $m$ phases, the **step angle** is:
> $$\boxed{\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r} \quad \text{(for full-step operation with single-phase-on excitation)}}$$
> when the stator pole count is chosen as $N_s = m \cdot N_r$. (This is the most common textbook formulation.)

## 3.2 Derivation of Electromagnetic Torque from Co-Energy

The **co-energy method** is the most general way to derive torque in any singly-excited electromagnetic device.

### Step 1 — Magnetic Co-Energy Function

For a phase winding with angle-dependent inductance $L(\theta)$ and constant current $i$:

$$W'_{fld}(i, \theta) = \frac{1}{2}\, L(\theta)\, i^2$$

### Step 2 — Fourier Expansion of $L(\theta)$

Because of the periodic toothed structure, the inductance varies sinusoidally:

$$L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$$

where $L_0$ is the *average* inductance, $L_1$ is the *fundamental component amplitude*, and $N_r \theta$ is the electrical angle.

### Step 3 — Partial Differentiation w.r.t. Angle

$$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{fld}}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{2}\, i^2 \cdot \frac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{2}\, i^2 \cdot \left[-L_1 N_r \sin(N_r \theta)\right]$$

$$\boxed{T_e = -\frac{1}{2}\, N_r\, L_1\, i^2 \sin(N_r \theta)}$$

### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation

- The torque is **proportional to $i^2$** — so the direction is *independent of current sign*. This is why a stepper can be driven by unipolar (single-polarity) pulses.
- Torque is **maximum** when $\sin(N_r \theta) = \pm 1$, i.e., when the rotor is **one-quarter tooth pitch** away from alignment.
- Torque is **zero** (stable equilibrium) when $N_r \theta = 0$ or $\pi$, i.e., when a rotor tooth is fully aligned with a stator tooth.

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Slewing Rate

When the rotor must spin at $N$ revolutions per minute, the controller must output pulses at:

$$f_p = \frac{N \cdot N_{steps}}{60} = \frac{N \cdot N_s}{60} \quad \text{[pulses per second]}$$

This is the *pulse rate*. The maximum rate is set by the *L/R time constant* of the phase winding:

$$\tau_{elec} = \frac{L}{R}$$

The current can rise to 63.2% of its steady-state value in time $\tau_{elec}$. For the current to reach steady-state between two consecutive phase commutations, the **commutation interval** $T_{com} = 1/f_p$ must be at least $4\tau_{elec}$ (for 98% settling). Hence:

$$f_{p,\,max} \approx \frac{R}{4L}$$

For a chopper drive with supply voltage $V_{DC}$ and target current $\Delta I$:

$$\boxed{f_{p,\,max} = \frac{V_{DC}}{L \cdot \Delta I}}$$

This is the **electrical slew rate limit** of a stepper motor — a frequently asked KTU numerical.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Stepper Motor Drive System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **closed-loop-of-pulses (open-loop-of-position)** control architecture used in every stepper drive system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microcontroller or PC Host] -- Pulse Train and Direction Signal --> B[Stepper Driver IC or Chopper Drive]
    B -- Phase A Current --> C1[Stator Phase A Winding]
    B -- Phase B Current --> C2[Stator Phase B Winding]
    B -- Phase C Current --> C3[Stator Phase C Winding]
    B -- Phase D Current --> C4[Stator Phase D Winding]
    C1 --> D[Rotor with N_r Salient Teeth or PM Poles]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D -- Mechanical Step Angle theta_s --> E[Output Shaft Driving Load]
    F[DC Power Supply 5V to 48V] -- V_DC --> B
    G[Clock Oscillator] -- Base Clock --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** Pulses originate in the digital controller (left), are amplified and sequenced by the drive electronics (centre), are delivered as phase currents to the stator (middle box), which produces a stepped mechanical rotation (right). The DC power supply feeds the driver. There is **no position feedback** to the controller — this is *open-loop positioning*, a defining feature of stepper systems.

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Two-Phase ON Excitation of a 4-Phase VR Stepper

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Phase A ON, Phase B OFF, C OFF, D OFF] --> P2[Step 2: Phase A ON, Phase B ON, C OFF, D OFF]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Phase A OFF, Phase B ON, C OFF, D OFF]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Phase A OFF, Phase B ON, Phase C ON, D OFF]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Phase A OFF, Phase B OFF, Phase C ON, D OFF]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Phase A OFF, Phase B OFF, Phase C ON, Phase D ON]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Phase A OFF, Phase B OFF, Phase C OFF, Phase D ON]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Phase A ON, Phase B OFF, Phase C OFF, Phase D ON]
    P8 --> P1
```

> [!TIP]
> Each step advances the rotor by one step angle. The pattern **AB → BC → CD → DA** is the *full-step two-phase-on* sequence. It produces higher torque than single-phase-on but consumes twice the power.

## 4.3 Torque-Angle Characteristic (Conceptual Plot via Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Aligned Position theta equals zero] --> B[Maximum Holding Torque occurs at theta equals quarter tooth pitch]
    B --> C[Unaligned Position theta equals half tooth pitch - Zero Torque]
    C --> D[Maximum Negative Torque at theta equals three quarter tooth pitch]
    D --> E[Next Aligned Position theta equals full tooth pitch]
    E --> A
```

This shows the **sinusoidal static torque-angle characteristic** of a single excited phase. The stable equilibrium is at $\theta = 0$ (aligned), and the *detent torque* holds the rotor there against small disturbances.

## 4.4 Variable Reluctance Rotor–Stator Cross-Section (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stator_Ring
        SA[Stator Pole A1]
        SB[Stator Pole A2]
        SC[Stator Pole B1]
        SD[Stator Pole B2]
    end
    subgraph Rotor_Core
        R1[Rotor Tooth 1]
        R2[Rotor Tooth 2]
        R3[Rotor Tooth 3]
        R4[Rotor Tooth 4]
    end
    SA -- Air Gap --> R1
    SB -- Air Gap --> R3
    SC -- Air Gap --> R2
    SD -- Air Gap --> R4
```

**Interpretation:** The stator has 4 salient poles (two north, two south of one phase), and the rotor has 4 salient teeth. When Phase A is energised, poles A1 and A2 become N and S; the rotor rotates so that two of its teeth align under A1 and A2 (minimum reluctance). Energising Phase B then rotates the rotor by one step.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are calibrated to **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A (3 marks each, no choice) and Part B (14 marks each, with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B). Each sub-question of Part B carries 7 marks.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define a stepper motor. Why is it called a "digital" electromechanical device?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A stepper motor is a **brushless synchronous motor** in which the rotor shaft rotates in **discrete angular increments** (steps) in response to a train of electrical pulses applied to the stator. (1 mark)

It is called a *digital* device because:
- Its input is a **digital pulse train** (each pulse = one step). (1 mark)
- Its output is a **discrete angular position**, not a continuous analog one. (1 mark)
- The shaft position is directly proportional to the **number of pulses** sent, making it a true *digital-to-mechanical angular converter* usable in open-loop digital control systems.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*State two differences between a stepper motor and a conventional DC motor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Stepper Motor** | **Conventional DC Motor** |
|---|---|
| Operates in **open loop**; no encoder required for positioning. | Requires **closed-loop feedback** (tachogenerator/encoder) for precise speed control. |
| Rotation is in **discrete steps** of fixed angle $\theta_s$. | Rotation is **continuous** and smooth. |
| Torque is produced by **alignment of reluctance / magnetic poles**; current is pulsed. | Torque is produced by **Lorentz force** on current-carrying conductors in a continuous magnetic field. |
| Position is **remembered (detent torque)** when de-energized (in PM and Hybrid types). | No detent torque; can free-wheel when de-energized. |

*(Any two valid points with explanation: 1.5 marks each.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> - Students often **write the formula for step angle but forget to state the units** explicitly. Always state "$\theta_s$ in degrees" or convert to **radians** if torque equations are used.
> - A common error: **using $N_s$ and $N_r$ interchangeably**. Recall that $N_s$ = *stator pole count / steps per revolution* and $N_r$ = *number of rotor teeth*. They are related by $N_s = N_r \pm 2m$ for compatibility — they are **NOT the same number** in general.
> - In derivations, **do not skip the co-energy setup**. Marks are reserved for stating $W'_{fld} = \tfrac{1}{2} L(\theta) i^2$ before differentiating.
> - Always mention the *physical interpretation* of every final equation (e.g., "torque is proportional to $i^2$, hence independent of current direction"). This is a 1–2 mark differentiator between *pass* and *first-class* answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **construction and principle of operation of a Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor**. Derive the expression for the **step angle**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 3-phase variable reluctance stepper motor has **12 rotor teeth**. Calculate the **(i) step angle**, **(ii) number of steps per revolution**, and **(iii) shaft speed in rpm when pulsed at 240 pulses per second**. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Construction (2 marks):** A VR stepper motor has a **stator** with $m$ phase windings distributed across $N_s$ salient poles, and a **rotor** made of soft magnetic steel with $N_r$ salient teeth and **no permanent magnet or field winding**. The rotor is passive; magnetic flux exists only when a stator phase is energised.

**Principle of Operation (3 marks):**
- The rotor is attracted toward the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance** when a stator phase is excited.
- When Phase A is energised, a magnetic field is set up. The rotor experiences a torque and rotates until two of its teeth are aligned with the excited Phase A poles (aligned = minimum air-gap reluctance, maximum inductance).
- The Phase A supply is then disconnected and Phase B is energised. The rotor advances by one step angle $\theta_s$ to align a new set of teeth under Phase B.
- Sequential commutation A → B → C → A produces continuous rotation. Each pulse = one step.

**Step Angle Derivation (2 marks):**
For a $m$-phase VR motor with $N_r$ rotor teeth, the number of steps per revolution equals the stator pole count $N_s$, and:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s} = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$$

*(under the standard design condition $N_s = m \cdot N_r$ for a multi-stack or a multi-tooth single-stack configuration).*

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $m = 3$ phases, $N_r = 12$ rotor teeth, $f_p = 240$ pps.

**(i) Step angle (2 marks):**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r} = \frac{360°}{3 \times 12} = \frac{360°}{36} = \boxed{10° \text{ per step}}$$

**(ii) Number of steps per revolution (2 marks):**

$$N_{steps} = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{10°} = \boxed{36 \text{ steps per revolution}}$$

**Cross-check:** $N_{steps} = m \cdot N_r = 3 \times 12 = 36$ ✓

**(iii) Shaft speed in rpm (3 marks):**

The shaft advances by $\theta_s$ degrees per pulse. With $f_p$ pulses per second, the angular speed in degrees per second is:

$$\omega = f_p \cdot \theta_s = 240 \times 10° = 2400° \text{ per second}$$

Convert to rpm by dividing by 360°/rev:

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{2400°/s}{360°/rev} = 6.667 \text{ rev/s}$$

$$N_{rpm} = 6.667 \times 60 = \boxed{400 \text{ rpm}}$$

*(Equivalently: $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_p}{N_{steps}} = \dfrac{60 \times 240}{36} = 400$ rpm ✓)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of a Permanent Magnet (PM) stepper motor** with a suitable diagram. Compare it briefly with the **Hybrid stepper motor**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **electromagnetic torque equation** of a stepper motor using the **co-energy method**. State the assumptions clearly. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**

**PM Stepper Motor — Construction (2 marks):**
The rotor of a PM stepper motor is a **permanent magnet** (typically axially magnetised in a 2-pole design, or radially magnetised for multi-pole versions). The stator has salient poles with concentrated phase windings (usually 2 phases, A and B). There are **no rotor teeth** — the rotor surface is smooth and magnetised.

**Principle of Operation (3 marks):**
- When Phase A is energised with positive polarity, a stator N-pole and S-pole are created. The permanent-magnet rotor (with its own fixed N and S poles) experiences a torque and aligns so that **opposite poles face each other** (N of rotor faces S of stator, S of rotor faces N of stator).
- When Phase A is de-energised and Phase B is energised, the new stator field is oriented 90° away (in a 2-phase machine), so the rotor rotates by **90° mechanical** to align with the new field.
- Reversing the polarity of Phase A (negative pulse) rotates the rotor by another 90° in the same direction. Hence, with 2 phases, four commutation states exist: +A, +B, −A, −B, producing **4 steps per electrical cycle**.

**Comparison with Hybrid Stepper (2 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **PM Stepper** | **Hybrid Stepper** |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor | Permanent magnet, smooth | Permanent magnet **plus** toothed end-caps |
| Step angle | Coarse (typically **90°** in 2-phase) | Fine (typically **0.9° or 1.8°**) |
| Holding torque | Moderate | High |
| Detent torque | Yes (PM cogging) | Yes (combined reluctance + PM) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate to high |
| Application | Toys, indicators, low-cost printers | CNC, robotics, high-precision 3D printers |

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Magnetics are linear (no saturation): $L$ does not depend on $i$.
- Single phase excited at a time (or phases are magnetically decoupled).
- Reluctance of iron parts is negligible (only air-gap reluctance matters).
- The rotor has $N_r$ salient teeth producing periodic variation of inductance.

**Co-Energy Setup (2 marks):**
The magnetic co-energy stored in the field of one excited phase, carrying current $i$, with angle-dependent inductance $L(\theta)$:

$$W'_{fld} = \frac{1}{2}\, L(\theta)\, i^2$$

**Inductance Model (1 mark):**
Due to the periodic toothed geometry, $L(\theta)$ is sinusoidal:

$$L(\theta) = L_0 + L_1 \cos(N_r \theta)$$

where $L_0$ is the mean value and $L_1$ is the magnitude of the variation; $N_r \theta$ is the electrical angle.

**Torque as Partial Derivative of Co-Energy (2 marks):**

$$T_e = \left.\frac{\partial W'_{fld}}{\partial \theta}\right|_{i = \text{const}} = \frac{1}{2}\, i^2 \cdot \frac{\partial L(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = \frac{1}{2}\, i^2 \left[-L_1 N_r \sin(N_r \theta)\right]$$

**Final Equation (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{T_e = -\frac{1}{2}\, N_r\, L_1\, i^2\, \sin(N_r \theta)}$$

**Physical Interpretation (1 mark):**
- The torque is proportional to $i^2$ → **direction is independent of current polarity** (unipolar drive is sufficient).
- Torque vanishes at $N_r \theta = 0$ and $\pi$ (aligned and unaligned stable positions).
- Torque is maximum at $N_r \theta = \pm \pi/2$ (one-quarter tooth-pitch off alignment).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4: Principle of Operation of Stepper Motors**

- ☐ Stepper motor = **brushless, synchronous, open-loop positioning actuator** driven by digital pulses.
- ☐ Each electrical pulse produces **one discrete mechanical step** of fixed angle $\theta_s$.
- ☐ Fundamental operating principle: **rotor aligns to minimum magnetic reluctance / maximum inductance** position.
- ☐ Torque is given by the **co-energy derivative**: $T_e = \tfrac{1}{2} i^2 \, dL/d\theta$ — it is proportional to $i^2$ (unipolar operation possible).
- ☐ **Step angle equation** (must be memorised): $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s} = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$ for a VR motor with $m$ phases and $N_r$ rotor teeth.
- ☐ **Shaft speed in rpm**: $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{60 \cdot f_p}{N_{steps}}$ where $f_p$ is the pulse rate in pulses per second.
- ☐ **Compatibility condition** (for self-starting VR motors): $N_s = N_r \pm 2m$.
- ☐ **Three main types** to know:
  - **Variable Reluctance (VR):** passive salient rotor, no PM, low cost, low inertia.
  - **Permanent Magnet (PM):** smooth PM rotor, coarse step (45°–90°), has detent torque at zero current.
  - **Hybrid (HB):** PM + toothed end-caps, fine step (0.9°–1.8°), high torque, most common in industry.
- ☐ **Excitation modes:** single-phase-on (full step, lower torque), two-phase-on (full step, higher torque), half-step (interleaved, smoother), microstepping (sinusoidal current ratio, sub-step resolution).
- ☐ **Maximum pull-out torque** falls with stepping rate — defines the *start–stop region* vs. *slewing region* on the torque–frequency curve.
- ☐ **Open-loop limitation:** if load torque exceeds pull-out torque, the rotor **loses steps** silently — no alarm, no fault, just wrong position. This is the single biggest disadvantage of stepper systems.
- ☐ **KTU Board Exam Tips:** (1) Always state the *number of phases* before writing the step angle equation. (2) For numericals, show the **substitution step** with units. (3) Always include a **neat labelled diagram** for 7-mark descriptive answers — a missing diagram costs 1–2 marks. (4) For derivations, state **assumptions first**, then build the equation step by step. (5) End every answer with a **physical interpretation line**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modelling. Inverters for Vernier PM Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT638 - SPECIAL ELECTRICAL MACHINES AND DRIVES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modelling. Inverters for Vernier PM Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 | TOPIC: MODELLING & INVERTERS FOR VERNIER PM MOTORS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vernier Permanent Magnet (VPM) Motor** — A special brushless synchronous machine that exploits the **magnetic gearing effect (Vernier effect)** between stator slots and rotor PM poles. The air-gap permeance is modulated by the stator teeth so that a small set of stator windings produces a flux-density distribution containing rich harmonic content. Through careful selection of the rotor pole count $P_r$ and stator slot count $Z_s$, one specific air-gap harmonic interacts synchronously to produce high specific torque at low rotational speeds, emulating a mechanical gear without contact.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The topic falls under *Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machines with Magnetic Gearing*. Students must master (i) the pole-pair / slot-pitch selection rule, (ii) the air-gap flux-density harmonic spectrum, and (iii) the equivalent $dq$-axis dynamic model used in vector control schemes driven by **two-level Voltage Source Inverters (VSI)**.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)

The Vernier PM motor is a three-phase, slot-concentrated-wound permanent magnet synchronous machine whose electromagnetic torque is produced primarily by the interaction between the rotor PM MMF and a **specific air-gap permeance harmonic** rather than the fundamental. The synchronism condition requires the **rotor pole pair number $P_r$ to differ from the stator armature pole pair number $P_s$ by the stator slot number $Z_s$**:

$$
P_r = P_s \pm Z_s
$$

This relationship is what produces the **magnetic gearing** — a small relative displacement between stator MMF and rotor flux maps through *many* magnetic pole pairs per electrical cycle, multiplying the steady-state torque.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic Gear"

Imagine two interlocking gears in a **bicycle hub**, where one small gear drives a much larger ring gear at a greatly reduced speed with massive torque amplification. Now, instead of teeth meshing physically, picture the teeth replaced by **invisible magnetic flux lines** stamped into the stator and rotor.

- The **stator teeth** act like a stationary comb with $Z_s$ slots.
- The **rotor surface PMs** act like a rotating magnet crown with $2P_r$ poles.
- When $P_r$ is large and $P_r - P_s = Z_s$ (or similar), the rotating flux appears to "leap" across multiple stator slots per electrical cycle — a phenomenon identical to the vernier scale on a caliper, where the main scale and vernier scale only align periodically.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recognition Trick:** If the slot count $Z_s$ equals the difference between rotor pole pairs $P_r$ and stator pole pairs $P_s$, you are looking at a Vernier topology — *not* a conventional PMSM.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following bold constants are *universally* used in VPM analysis:

- **Magnetic loading (peak air-gap flux density):** $\hat{B}_g \approx \mathbf{0.7\text{ – }1.2\ T}$
- **Electric loading (rms armature):** $A_{rms} \approx \mathbf{30\text{ – }65\ kA/m}$
- **Slot-pole combinations typical:** $Z_s/P_s/P_r = \mathbf{6/2/8,\ 12/4/10,\ 12/8/10,\ 18/6/20,\ 24/10/22}$
- **Magnetic gear ratio:** $G_r = P_r / P_s = \mathbf{3\text{ – }5}$
- **Synchronous mechanical speed:** $n_s = 120 f_s / P_s$, but **the rotor settles at** $n_r = 120 f_s / P_r$
- **Vernier effect gain (torque amplification factor):** $\eta_v \approx \mathbf{2\text{ – }4\times}$ over conventional PMSM of identical frame size

### 1.4 Why VPM Motors Matter in Modern Drives

- Direct-drive low-speed high-torque applications: **wind turbine generators, ship propulsion, in-wheel EV traction, industrial robotics.**
- Eliminates mechanical gearbox → **reduced maintenance, no lubrication, no backlash**.
- Excellent fault-tolerance because of fractional-slot concentrated windings.
- Naturally suited to **sensorless control** due to the strong saliency in $L_d \neq L_q$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->
